Daily Brief — 2026-07-06

What we covered

  • World news: Global Tensions Rise Ahead of Key NATO Summit
  • SPY learning: Building a Directional View on SPY using Moving Averages
  • Market brief: SPY Market Brief for July 6, 2026
  • PM/analytics: How I run autonomous coding agents from my phone with OpenAI Symphony + Linear | Alessio Fanelli (Kernel Labs)

Summary

In the Americas, Russian strikes on Kyiv have heightened fears ahead of the NATO summit, with 19 casualties reported and significant damage to infrastructure. This escalation underscores the ongoing conflict’s potential to disrupt global energy markets and security dynamics. Over in Europe, the situation in Ukraine dominates headlines, particularly with the recent Russian missile attacks just before the NATO summit. The implications for European defense spending and energy policies could ripple through markets as leaders gather to discuss responses. In Asia, Peru aims to recalibrate its economic ties with China, looking to move beyond raw mineral exports. This shift could impact global commodity flows, especially as China remains a key player in Peru’s economy. In the Middle East and Africa, the humanitarian crisis continues to escalate, with reports of a baby dying due to blocked medical access in the West Bank. Such incidents highlight the fragility of the region and may influence international aid and political responses. In the Asia Pacific, Japan’s Sapporo is investing $643 million in a joint venture with Carlsberg, signaling potential shifts in the beverage industry landscape. This could lead to increased competition and impact supply chains across the region.

Imagine you and your friend are at a carnival, trying to decide which roller coaster to ride. You notice that every time you ride the Ferris wheel, it goes up and down slowly and gives you a good view of the entire park. When you see the Ferris wheel is at its highest point, you know it’s a great time to hop on the coaster because you can see where it’s going. This is like using a guideline to help you make a decision. Just as you can predict the fun of a roller coaster ride by looking at the Ferris wheel, traders use moving averages to see the direction of a stock, like SPY, to make informed choices.

Now, let’s connect that to trading. The Ferris wheel represents the moving average, which smooths out the ups and downs of SPY’s price over time. When the price of SPY is above the moving average, it’s like riding the coaster when it’s at the top; you feel confident that things are going well. Conversely, if SPY drops below the moving average, it’s like seeing the Ferris wheel stuck at the lowest point — that’s a signal something might be off. For example, today SPY is trading at 748.22. If you take a look at the 50-day moving average and it’s around 740, you would see that SPY is currently above that average, which suggests a bullish trend.

So, what does this mean for you as a trader? Knowing that SPY is currently above its moving average, you might be more inclined to consider bullish strategies, like selling puts or buying calls. Given that SPY’s current IV rank is at 0.0, indicating that implied volatility is low, you could potentially sell premium on options with a higher probability of profit. For instance, if you look at the options chain, you see the ATM strike at 748.0, with calls showing decent volume. This suggests that traders are anticipating SPY to stay above this level, and with low volatility, you might feel comfortable selling a put spread below the moving average for defined risk. So, if your view is that SPY will continue on this upward trend, you can use those moving averages as a tool to back up your strategy, helping you make more calculated trading decisions.

As you navigate through today’s trading landscape, remember that just as the Ferris wheel gives you a broader perspective on the carnival, moving averages can provide you with a clearer view of the market direction, helping you make those pivotal decisions in your trading strategy.

Today, SPY closed at 748.22, marking a gain of 0.46% for the day. While this move might seem modest, it’s significant in the context of the week, especially considering the recent volatility we’ve seen. Just last week, SPY was hovering around 741, so this uptick reflects a bit of momentum building as we head into the second half of the year. The volume today was around 5.3 million shares, which is solid, indicating that traders are engaged and possibly looking for direction after a few days of mixed performance. Overall, this move could signal a shift in sentiment as we approach key economic indicators and earnings reports later this month.

Diving into sector performance, Technology (XLK) led the charge today, rising 2.24%, which accounted for a significant portion of SPY’s gains. This strength in tech is likely a reflection of ongoing investor confidence in the sector, especially with the recent advancements in AI and cloud computing. On the flip side, we saw Healthcare (XLV) lagging behind, down 1.93%. This decline could be attributed to ongoing concerns regarding regulatory pressures and potential changes in healthcare policy, which have weighed on investor sentiment in that space. Other laggards included Consumer Staples (XLP) and Real Estate (XLRE), both of which fell over 1%, suggesting that defensive sectors are struggling as risk appetite increases.

The macro backdrop continues to play a crucial role in shaping these sector movements. With the unemployment rate dropping to 4.2% and inflation indicators showing signs of stabilization, it seems that the market is digesting the idea that the economy might be on a more stable footing. The recent CPI reading of 4.27% is still high, but it’s a slight improvement from previous months, which could be giving investors a reason to feel more optimistic about growth prospects. Additionally, the yield curve has steepened slightly, which often signals a more favorable outlook for economic growth in the near term. This macro context is likely fueling the tech rally we’re seeing today.

Now, let’s talk about implied volatility. Currently, SPY’s IV rank sits at 0.0, which is quite low, indicating that options are relatively cheap right now compared to historical norms. With the current IV at 15.23 and a VIX of 16.32, this could present an interesting opportunity for those looking to sell premium. If you’re considering selling options, this low IV rank suggests that you might be able to capture some decent premium while the market is relatively calm. However, keep in mind that low IV can also mean that big moves could be on the horizon, so stay vigilant.

As for key price levels, immediate support for SPY is around 744, which aligns with the recent lows we’ve seen this week. On the upside, resistance is likely to be found at the 750 level, a psychological barrier that traders will be watching closely. If SPY can break through that level, it could open the door for a more substantial rally, especially if tech continues to lead.

Looking ahead, there are a few events on the calendar that could impact market sentiment. We have the Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update releasing this afternoon, which could provide insights into the housing market and consumer sentiment. Additionally, the next Fed meeting is scheduled for July 29, which is still a few weeks away but will be a critical event for traders to keep an eye on. The Fed’s stance on interest rates will undoubtedly influence market dynamics, especially as we navigate through earnings season.

In summary, for options traders considering positions this week, the low IV rank presents an attractive opportunity to sell premium, particularly if you believe that SPY will remain within a defined range in the near term. Just keep an eye on those key price levels and upcoming economic indicators, as they could provide the volatility you need to make your strategy work effectively.

Here’s the thing: the future of trading and product management isn’t just about making decisions in isolation; it’s about leveraging automation and real-time data to amplify your edge. Alessio Fanelli’s approach of using autonomous coding agents from his phone is a game-changer. What that means in practice is that you can harness AI not just for analysis but also for executing trades based on predefined criteria. Imagine having a system that can scan for underpriced options on SPY, execute trades when your criteria are met, and adjust in real time based on market movements—all while you focus on broader strategic decisions.

Let’s break it down with a concrete example. Think about your current process for identifying premium selling opportunities on SPY. You might be looking at IV rank, historical volatility, and market sentiment. Now picture integrating a coding agent that continuously monitors these factors and alerts you when conditions align for a high-probability trade. This isn’t just about speed; it’s about precision in execution. The move here is to shift from a reactive approach to a proactive one, where your setup not only identifies opportunities but also acts on them without emotional interference.

This connects directly to how you build and measure your trading strategies. By automating parts of your process, you can free up cognitive resources to think more strategically about risk management, position sizing, and portfolio diversification. You’re not replacing your expertise; you’re enhancing it.

So, what can you do this week? Start by exploring tools like OpenAI’s Codex or other automation platforms that let you build your own trading agents. Set up a simple script that can scan for SPY options trades based on your criteria and notify you when to act. This way, you’ll not only gain insights into your trading patterns but also start the shift toward a more systematic, automated trading approach. The key is experimentation—start small and iterate based on what you learn.

Summary

In the Americas, Citadel’s hedge funds reported broad first-half gains, with their tactical trading fund climbing 14.3% through June. This strong performance signals resilience in hedge fund strategies that may influence investor sentiment across the board. In Europe, a Ukrainian woman has been named as a suspect in a Monaco bombing that injured a prominent Ukrainian businessman. This incident heightens geopolitical tensions in the region, potentially impacting investor confidence and capital flows. In Asia, Malaysian Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim has vowed to pursue fugitive financier Jho Low over the 1MDB scandal, despite a potential US presidential pardon. This commitment underscores ongoing efforts to address corruption, which could affect Malaysia’s international reputation and economic stability.

Imagine you’re at a pizza shop, and you want to get your favorite slice, but there’s only one pie left. The owner tells you that the pizza will be ready in 30 minutes and that it’s super popular right now. You know that if you wait, more people might come in and buy it, making it harder for you to get your slice. So you decide to stake your claim right away, reserving that last slice for yourself. This reflects how we often make decisions based on what we perceive as trends or popularity at any given moment. If the pizza is hot and everyone wants it, your chances of getting it might diminish if you wait too long to act. In this case, acting quickly based on current demand is key to securing what you want.

Now, let’s connect that pizza shop scenario to trading SPY options. The slice of pizza represents a specific price point of SPY—currently at 744.78. The trend we’re observing is the direction SPY is moving, which you can gauge by looking at the recent prices and market activity. Just like the popularity of that pizza, the current price and movement of SPY can indicate whether to buy or sell options. Today, SPY has an implied volatility (IV) rank of 100%, which means it’s at the highest level compared to its history. This high IV suggests that traders expect the price to swing significantly, just like a busy pizza shop during lunchtime. When the market is hot, just like that pizza, you need to act quickly to make the most of it.

Understanding these dynamics can fundamentally alter how you approach trading SPY options. With SPY’s IV rank at 100%, this indicates that the market is expecting movement, which can provide opportunities for traders looking to sell premium. For instance, if you’re considering a defined-risk spread strategy today, you might look at selling calls or puts close to the current ATM strike of 745. Given that the IV is high, you can collect more premium for taking on the risk, like reserving that pizza slice at a premium price because you know demand is strong. However, it’s essential to remain aware of market trends; if SPY dips below 740, that could signal a change in momentum, similar to a sudden drop in pizza demand. Thus, keeping an eye on both the IV and the price action will guide your trading decisions, helping you to act decisively when the opportunity is hot and to adjust your strategy if the trend shifts.

Today, SPY closed at 744.78, down just 0.13% from the previous day. It opened at 747.4, reached a high of 751.31, and dipped to a low of 740.03. While the decline may seem minor, it’s significant when you consider that this week has seen some volatility, particularly after a strong rally earlier in the week. The SPY has been fluctuating within a range, and today’s close puts it closer to the lower end of that range. With a weekly high of 760.4 and a low of 617.87, today’s movement reflects a cautious sentiment as traders digest recent economic data and sector performances.

In terms of sector performance, Healthcare (XLV) led the charge today, rising 2.63%, which accounted for a substantial portion of SPY’s overall movement. Utilities (XLU) and Consumer Staples (XLP) also showed strength, gaining 2.21% and 2.03%, respectively. On the flip side, Technology (XLK) was the laggard, falling 2.71%. This divergence highlights a shift in investor sentiment, as they seem to be favoring defensive sectors amid uncertainty. The strength in Healthcare could be attributed to ongoing discussions around healthcare reforms and the potential for increased spending in that sector, while the weakness in Technology may reflect concerns over valuation and growth sustainability.

The news backdrop is quite telling as well. The recent economic indicators, including a slight uptick in CPI year-over-year to 4.27%, have added to the cautious tone in the markets. Investors are weighing the implications of inflation against the backdrop of a stable unemployment rate, which has improved to 4.2%. This mixed economic picture is likely contributing to the sector rotation we’re seeing, with investors flocking to more stable, income-generating sectors like Healthcare and Utilities while pulling back from the higher-growth areas like Technology.

As for implied volatility, the current IV rank stands at a striking 100%. This means that options are relatively expensive compared to their historical norms, which could make selling premium attractive for those looking to capitalize on potential mean reversion. With the VIX hovering around 15.94, it suggests that market participants are bracing for some movement, but the high IV rank indicates that there might be a premium to be had if you’re considering selling options. For traders, this could be a prime opportunity to explore defined-risk spreads, especially if you believe that the market will stabilize or move sideways in the near term.

Looking at key price levels, immediate support for SPY is around the 740 mark, while resistance is likely to be found at the 750 level. If SPY breaks below 740, we could see further downside, potentially testing the lower bounds of this week’s range. Conversely, a push above 750 could signal a return to bullish sentiment, especially if accompanied by positive economic data or sector performance.

In the next 48 hours, we have the Non-Farm Payrolls report releasing this morning. This is a high-impact event that could significantly influence market sentiment, particularly given the current economic backdrop. Traders will be keenly watching the job growth figures, as they could provide insights into the health of the labor market and the broader economy. Additionally, the Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update is scheduled for release in six days, which could also have implications for the housing market and consumer sentiment.

For options traders considering their positions this week, the takeaway is clear: with IV at a premium, now might be a good time to explore selling strategies, particularly if you’re looking at defined-risk spreads around the current price levels. Keep an eye on the upcoming economic data, as it could provide the necessary catalyst for movement in either direction.

Here’s the thing: product builders often fall into a cognitive bias that can seriously skew their decision-making. This bias is rooted in four embedded beliefs that can cloud judgment and lead to misaligned priorities. Understanding and addressing this bias isn’t just an academic exercise; it’s critical for making better product decisions that resonate with user needs and stakeholder expectations.

What that means in practice is that as you’re developing your strategies, whether it’s for a new feature or a trading algorithm, you need to consciously challenge the assumptions that you hold dear. For instance, let’s say you’re developing a new trading strategy on SPY options. You might be inclined to believe that a certain technical indicator is infallible. But, what if it’s not? This kind of thinking can lead to confirmation bias, where you only seek out evidence that supports your pre-existing beliefs. Instead of blindly trusting your indicators, take a step back and ask, ‘What’s the data actually telling me?’ This pivot can reveal gaps in your strategy that you might not have noticed otherwise.

The move here is to implement a systematic approach to challenge these biases. One concrete tactic is to set up a regular review process where you and your team can critically assess past decisions and their outcomes. Maybe you start a monthly meeting dedicated to dissecting what worked, what didn’t, and why certain assumptions held true or failed. This isn’t just about accountability; it’s about fostering a culture where questioning and exploration are encouraged. By regularly exposing your ideas to scrutiny, you can prevent cognitive biases from steering your strategy off course.

Now, let’s connect this back to your trading decisions. If you’re considering a defined-risk spread on SPY, apply this mindset: before you commit, review the underlying market conditions. Look at the implied volatility, the Greeks, and the overall trend, and ask whether your initial thesis holds water under scrutiny. Doing this will enhance your decision-making framework.

So, this week, I challenge you to reevaluate your assumptions about your current trades. Pick one position, dig into the data, and see if your original reasoning still stands. You might be surprised by what you find.

Summary

In the Americas, the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz has raised concerns about an oil surplus destabilizing global energy markets. This shift could impact energy prices and trade flows, particularly for oil-importing nations. In Europe, the region remains quiet with no significant market-relevant developments today. There are ongoing discussions related to political issues but nothing that could affect trade or economic policy. In Asia, China’s tightening of capital controls is causing ripples in investment flows, as the Securities Regulatory Commission has fined brokerages for unauthorized access to overseas stocks. This could impact how global investors view China’s market accessibility and lead to a reevaluation of capital flows. In India, Pakistan’s Prime Minister is set to visit Iran, focusing on enhancing bilateral trade and investment. This could have implications for regional trade dynamics, particularly in energy. In the Middle East and Africa, the reopening of Hormuz could signal a shift from an oil shortage to a glut, potentially impacting global oil prices. This is crucial as many economies rely heavily on stable energy prices for their fiscal health. In the Asia Pacific, the Japanese yen has surged, hitting a 160 range due to fears of government intervention. This could influence currency trades and affect global investors’ positioning.

Imagine you’re at a big pizza party, and everyone is excited about getting their favorite slice. Each pizza represents a different kind of option, and the slices are like different prices you can choose. Now, let’s say you see a delicious pepperoni pizza that everyone wants. You can make a deal with your friend: for a small fee, you reserve a slice for later, even if the pizza gets more popular and the price goes up. If the party gets wild and more people show up, your slice is still safe for you at the price you agreed on, no matter how much everyone else wants it. So, if you really love pepperoni, you’re happy because you secured your slice at a good price, even if others have to pay more. That’s kind of how trading options works, where you pay a little bit now to have the right to buy or sell something at a specific price later, regardless of what happens in between.

Now, let’s connect that to SPY options. When we talk about SPY, we’re referring to the S&P 500 ETF, which tracks the performance of 500 of the largest U.S. companies. Just like you reserved that slice of pizza, options give you the right to buy or sell shares of SPY at a specific price, known as the strike price, before a certain date, called the expiration date. For example, if SPY is currently priced at $750.82 and you think it’s going to go up, you might buy a call option with a strike price of $751. If SPY climbs above that, you can exercise your option to buy at $751, even if the market price is higher. Today’s implied volatility, sitting at 13.06 with an IV rank of 98.5, tells you that options are relatively cheap compared to where they might be in the future, adding a layer of opportunity to your trades.

Understanding this concept transforms how you view trades. With SPY’s current price at $750.82 and a high IV rank, you might think, “Given that IV rank is at 98.5 today, this indicates that options are very cheap relative to their historical prices. So, if I’m considering a defined-risk spread strategy, it could be a good time to sell premium.” For example, if you think SPY will stay below $751 over the next few weeks, you might consider selling a call spread, where you sell a call at $751 and buy another at $753. By doing this, you can potentially earn the premium while limiting your risk. This strategy is appealing right now because of the low implied volatility, meaning you get to collect premium from the options you sell while keeping your risks in check. You’re not just thinking about the immediate price moves; you’re anticipating how the market might behave based on current volatility metrics, using that to your advantage in your trade management.

Today, SPY closed at 750.82, marking a gain of 0.68% from the previous day. The index opened at 747.4 and reached a high of 751.24, which is quite significant considering the volatility we’ve seen this week. Over the past five days, SPY has shown a positive trend, climbing from a low of 728.99 last Friday to this current level. This upward movement indicates a potential shift in market sentiment, especially as we approach key economic indicators that could further influence trading decisions.

In terms of sector performance, Healthcare (XLV) led the charge today, rising 1.5% and contributing significantly to SPY’s overall gain. Utilities (XLU) followed closely with a 1.02% increase, while Materials (XLB) and Consumer Staples (XLP) also posted respectable gains of 0.89% and 0.88%, respectively. On the flip side, Consumer Discretionary (XLY) lagged, dropping 0.49%, and Communication Services (XLC) fell slightly by 0.22%. The strength in Healthcare can likely be attributed to ongoing discussions around healthcare reforms and innovations, which have been a hot topic lately. Meanwhile, the weakness in Consumer Discretionary may reflect broader concerns about consumer sentiment, especially with recent data showing a decline in retail sales.

The macroeconomic backdrop is also worth noting. The latest Consumer Price Index (CPI) data showed a year-over-year increase of 4.27%, up from 3.95%, which could be influencing sector rotation as investors reassess their risk exposure. The unemployment rate has improved slightly to 4.2%, but consumer sentiment remains low at 44.8, down from 49.8. This mixed economic picture is likely causing traders to be cautious, particularly in sectors sensitive to consumer spending. With the Fed’s next meeting coming up on July 29, many are watching closely to see how these economic indicators will influence monetary policy.

Currently, SPY’s implied volatility (IV) stands at 13.06, with an impressive IV rank of 98.5. This high IV rank suggests that options are relatively expensive compared to historical norms, which could be an attractive opportunity for those looking to sell premium. If you’re considering selling options, this elevated IV could provide a favorable backdrop, especially if you believe that SPY will remain within a certain range over the coming weeks. However, keep in mind that high IV can also indicate potential for significant price movement, so risk management is key.

As for key price levels, immediate support for SPY appears to be around 747, which aligns closely with today’s low. Resistance is clearly defined at the 751 level, where we saw the high today. A break above this resistance could signal further bullish momentum, while a drop below support might prompt a reevaluation of long positions. Keeping an eye on these levels will be crucial as we navigate the coming days.

Looking ahead, the next 48 hours could be pivotal for the market. The Non-Farm Payrolls report is set to be released tomorrow, and given its high importance, it could lead to increased volatility in SPY and related sectors. Traders will be keen to see how the job market is performing, especially in light of the recent economic data. Additionally, the Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update is scheduled for next week, which could also impact market sentiment, particularly in the housing sector.

In summary, for options traders considering a position this week, the elevated IV suggests that selling premium could be a viable strategy, especially if you anticipate SPY will stay within the current range. However, be mindful of the upcoming economic data that could shake things up. Stay sharp and adjust your strategies accordingly.

Here’s the thing: as we navigate the complexities of product management, especially in areas like software engineering, we often overlook the power of trust as a foundational element—not just among teams, but also in how we engage with our stakeholders. Kent Beck, a pioneer in Agile methodologies, emphasizes that the future of software engineering, particularly in an AI-driven context, hinges on building trust rather than merely generating code. What that means in practice is that when you’re working on a project, whether it’s developing a new feature for your product or integrating a complex system, fostering an environment of trust can dramatically influence the outcome. For instance, consider a scenario where you’re leading a team through a tight deadline. If the team trusts that you have their best interests at heart, they are more likely to go the extra mile, share innovative ideas, and collaborate effectively. This can lead to not just meeting the deadline, but potentially delivering a superior product that exceeds expectations. The move here is to prioritize trust-building activities—this could be regular check-ins where you genuinely solicit feedback, celebrate small wins, or even create a safe space for discussing challenges without fear of repercussion. Now, let’s connect this to your trading decisions. Just like in software projects, trust plays a role in how you evaluate the market and your positions. If you build a framework of trust in your analysis—a consistent method of assessing risk and potential reward—you’re more likely to stick to your strategy, even in volatile conditions. This week, I challenge you to identify one area in your current projects or trading practice where you can strengthen trust—be it with your team, stakeholders, or your own trading methodology. Take that step, and you might just find that it leads to more robust outcomes down the line.

Summary

In the Americas, the U.S. is grappling with inflation pressures as consumer spending shows signs of cooling, raising concerns about whether the Federal Reserve will adjust interest rates in its upcoming meeting. This could significantly impact the dollar and broader market sentiment. Over in Europe, Zelenskyy emphasized the urgency for the EU to maintain its commitment to Ukraine’s accession talks amidst rising tensions in the region. Any delays in these discussions could complicate trade relations and energy supply chains as the conflict continues. From Asia, Chinese cyberattacks are intensifying, targeting a broader range of industries as competition with the U.S. escalates. This could affect global supply chains, particularly in tech, as companies reassess their security measures. In India, Russia has reportedly purchased 60,000 metric tons of gasoline to address its fuel shortages, sourced from India. This trade move could influence global oil prices and impact energy supply chains significantly. In the Middle East and Africa, Algeria heads to legislative polls amid fears of record-low voter turnout, driven by political apathy. This shift could lead to currency pressures and impact trade balances in the broader ASEAN region.

Imagine you’re at a carnival with your friends, and you each want to win a giant stuffed bear. You spot a game where you can throw balls at bottles to win one. You pay a few tokens for a chance to throw, and if you hit a certain number of bottles, you get a bear. Now, let’s say you hit three bottles right away. Your friend, who’s been watching, tells you that if you can hit two more bottles, you’ll win the biggest bear of all. But, here’s the catch: the longer you wait, the more people come over, and the game might get too crowded or the chances might change. So, should you keep playing for that big bear or grab one of the smaller ones that you could win right now? It’s a tough choice, right? That’s how we often have to think about taking profits when we play games or trade options.

Now let’s bring this back to trading. When we look at SPY options, taking profits early can be similar to deciding when to cash in on your game winnings. In the options world, this is often framed as taking 50% of your maximum profit. For instance, if your SPY option is currently valued at $300, and you anticipate it could go as high as $600, that means you have a potential maximum profit of $300. Taking 50% of that would mean selling at $450. This strategy allows you to secure gains while managing the risk of the option losing value as the market shifts. Today, with SPY sitting at $745.85 and an IV rank of 0.0, the current environment is pretty low on volatility. This might suggest that while you could potentially wait for a bigger payoff, you might also want to consider that the market isn’t offering a lot of movement right now, making those early exits appealing.

So, what does this mean for your trading decisions? Knowing the importance of taking profits early helps you to not only secure gains but to also avoid getting greedy, which can lead to losses. With SPY at $745.85 today, and considering that the implied volatility is at such a low 13.68, this could be a sign that options are not expected to move significantly in either direction. If your current position has already gained a good amount, say 50% of its max profit, it might be smart to cash out rather than risk a sudden change in direction that could diminish your profits. The natural question here is: Why should I consider exiting at 50%? Here’s how to think about it: In a low volatility environment like we’re seeing today, the likelihood of big moves is reduced, meaning your options could stagnate or even lose value. So, by taking that profit early, you’re not just securing gains, but you’re also making a strategic decision based on current market conditions. It’s all about balancing risk with reward, and today, that balance might tip in favor of locking in those profits. You want to play it smart, not just take every risk for the sake of a bigger win, especially when the odds don’t support that move right now.

Today, SPY closed at 745.85, down slightly by 0.12% from the previous day. It opened at 745.00 and reached a high of 745.90, but the overall movement was relatively muted, especially considering the volatility we’ve seen over the past week. This slight dip comes after a couple of days of solid gains, where SPY had climbed from a low of 734.30 just a few days ago. So, in the context of the week, this minor pullback seems more like a consolidation phase rather than a significant trend reversal. With volume clocking in at just over 7.7 million shares, it’s clear that traders are still engaged, but perhaps waiting for more directional cues.

Looking at sector performance, Communication Services (XLC) led the charge today, rising 2.5%, which accounted for a significant portion of SPY’s overall movement. Financials (XLF) also contributed positively, up 1.04%, as optimism around economic recovery continues to bolster investor sentiment. On the other hand, Technology (XLK) lagged behind, falling 1.49%, which is noteworthy given its prominence in the market. Energy (XLE) also struggled, down 0.29%. The divergence in sector performance highlights a rotation in investor focus, likely driven by recent macroeconomic data and earnings expectations.

The strength in Communication Services can be attributed to several factors, including ongoing discussions around digital advertising and media consumption trends that are proving resilient even in a fluctuating economy. Meanwhile, the decline in Technology is likely a reaction to the rising interest in value stocks as opposed to growth stocks, especially in light of inflation concerns. The broader market seems to be digesting the implications of recent economic indicators, including a slight uptick in the Consumer Price Index and a steady unemployment rate, which could be influencing sector rotations.

As for implied volatility, we see that the current IV rank is sitting at 0.0, which indicates that options are relatively cheap right now. The current implied volatility is at 13.68, and with the VIX at 16.77, this low IV rank suggests that selling premium could be a favorable strategy for traders looking to capitalize on the current market conditions. Given the low volatility environment, options traders might find it advantageous to consider selling out-of-the-money options, especially as we approach the end of the month.

In terms of price levels to watch, immediate support for SPY appears to be around the 742 level, which was tested today. If that breaks, we could see a move towards the 740 mark, which is a psychological level as well. On the upside, resistance is clearly defined at the 750 level, which has been a significant barrier in the past week. Watching these levels will be crucial for any short-term trading strategies.

Looking ahead, there are a couple of key events that could impact market sentiment in the next 48 hours. Tomorrow, we have the Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update, which could provide insights into the housing market and consumer borrowing costs. Then, on July 3rd, the Non-Farm Payrolls report is set to be released, and this is a high-impact event that could significantly influence market direction, especially given the current economic backdrop. Traders should be prepared for potential volatility around these announcements.

In summary, for options traders considering positions this week, the takeaway is clear: with low implied volatility and a mixed bag of sector performance, it might be a good time to look at selling premium, particularly in sectors that are showing strength like Communication Services, while being cautious of the lagging Technology sector. Keep an eye on the upcoming economic data, as it could provide the catalyst for the next significant move in SPY.

Here’s the thing: if you’re not leveraging AI in your daily decision-making, you’re likely missing a trick that other top PMs are using to amplify their impact. The core insight here is that AI isn’t just a tool; it’s a fundamental shift in how you can approach prioritization and stakeholder engagement. What that means in practice is that you can use AI to sift through massive amounts of data and identify trends that would otherwise take weeks or even months to discover manually.

Take, for example, the way you might currently assess the potential success of a new product feature. Traditionally, you might rely on customer feedback, usage metrics, and maybe some A/B testing. But with AI, you can analyze historical data and user interactions at a scale that reveals deeper insights. Imagine feeding your existing user data into an AI model that highlights not only what features users like, but also predicts which features might lead to increased engagement based on patterns you’ve never even considered.

The move here is to integrate AI-driven analytics into your decision-making framework. This isn’t about replacing your gut instincts; it’s about enhancing them with data-backed insights. For instance, let’s say you’re deciding between two features to prioritize for your next release. Instead of relying solely on team discussions or gut feelings, you can run simulations using AI algorithms that take into account user behavior, market trends, and even the competitive landscape. The output might surprise you, giving you a clear data-driven path forward.

If you’re thinking about how this connects to your role in trading SPY options, consider this: just as you want to analyze market movements and sentiment before entering a trade, using AI can help you identify subtle shifts in market dynamics that could inform your strategy for selling premium. You can leverage AI tools to analyze implied volatility patterns and determine optimal strike prices for your spreads, ensuring you’re not just reacting to the market, but anticipating it.

So, what’s one specific action you can take this week? Start by identifying one area in your current workflow where you can introduce an AI tool or framework. Whether it’s for analyzing user feedback or assessing market data, make that leap to incorporate AI into your processes, and see how it transforms your decision-making.

Daily Brief — 2026-06-30

What we covered

  • World news: Global Markets on Edge: Trade, Tech, and Tensions Drive the Week
  • SPY learning: Rolling Trades: Extending Duration When Things Go South

Summary

In the Americas, small-cap stocks are experiencing their best first half in 35 years, driven by a resurgence in investor confidence and AI-related trades. This shift could reshape the market landscape as more capital flows into these previously underperforming assets. Over in Europe, the UK is making headlines with a 300-billion-pound defense investment plan that includes more than 5 billion pounds for drones and autonomous systems. This significant commitment could bolster the defense sector and impact related supply chains across the continent. In Asia, China’s economy is showing signs of recovery in June, thanks to rebounding U.S. exports. This uptick in trade may have implications for global supply chains and could ease concerns about a slowing Chinese economy impacting global growth.

Imagine you’re at a birthday party, and you’ve got a ticket for a game you really want to play. The game is super fun, but as you’re waiting in line, the host suddenly announces that they’re going to close the game in just a few minutes. You feel a bit worried because you haven’t had your chance yet. So, you think, what if I could just get a new ticket that lets me play longer? Maybe if I ask nicely, or trade my ticket with someone who’s leaving early, I can keep enjoying the game without losing out. It’s all about keeping the fun going, even if things don’t go as planned at first.

Now, let’s think of that ticket as your option in the stock market. When you buy an option, you’re essentially buying a ticket to a game that lets you benefit from price movements of a stock, like SPY. If the game (or your trade) isn’t going well and time is running out, rolling your option means you’re getting a new ticket for a later date. This can help you avoid losing that initial investment and continue to play the game as the market evolves. It’s about extending your opportunity when your first attempt didn’t go as you hoped.

Now, let’s connect the dots to the real world of SPY options trading. Today, SPY is trading at $743.22, and you’ve got an option that’s not performing as expected with the expiration date coming up soon on July 31. If the market isn’t moving in your favor, the natural question here is: how do I manage this situation? Here’s how to think about it: if your option is losing value, you can roll it to a later date, say to August or September. Essentially, you’re exchanging your current position for one that gives you more time to see if the SPY will move in a favorable direction. This way, you don’t just abandon your original position—it’s a chance to adjust your strategy while remaining invested.

Given that SPY’s implied volatility rank is at 97.96 today, this means there’s a high level of expectation for future price swings, which could benefit your trading strategy. High IV can make options more expensive, but if you’re rolling your trade, you could potentially get a better deal on a later expiry option. So, if you started with an option that’s expiring soon, consider rolling it to one with more days left, especially if you’re seeing that SPY has the potential to move based on current market sentiment. This is where thinking like a systematic trader really pays off; you’re not just reacting to price changes, but strategically planning your next steps based on the data in front of you.

In a nutshell, rolling your trade doesn’t just keep you in the game longer; it gives you a fresh chance to capitalize on future price movements in SPY. With today’s SPY price and high IV, it’s a good time to assess whether rolling your trade is a smart move to enhance your position, rather than simply exiting a trade that’s not going your way. Remember, it’s all about managing risk and maximizing opportunity, and rolling your options can help you do just that.

Today, SPY closed at 743.22, marking a modest gain of 0.3% from the previous day. This move, while not monumental, is significant in the context of the week, especially considering SPY had a rough patch earlier, dipping to 728.99 just a few days ago. The upward trajectory over the last two days suggests a potential reversal of sentiment, and traders are keenly watching to see if this momentum can be sustained into July. Volume today was around 9.18 million shares, which is decent but not extraordinary, indicating that while there’s some interest, it’s not a full-blown rally just yet.

In terms of sector performance, Technology, represented by the XLK, was the clear leader today, rising 1.69% and accounting for a significant portion of SPY’s gains. This strength in tech is likely a reflection of ongoing enthusiasm around AI and tech innovation, which has been a hot topic lately. On the flip side, Real Estate (XLRE) lagged the index, falling 1.91%. The decline in real estate could be attributed to rising mortgage rates, which are currently at 6.49%. Higher borrowing costs typically dampen demand in this sector, and today’s performance seems to echo that sentiment.

The broader macro context also plays a role here. The recent uptick in inflation, with the CPI year-over-year at 4.27%, has traders on edge, and the Fed’s next meeting on July 29 is looming large. The market is still digesting the implications of these economic indicators, particularly as the yield curve has flattened, suggesting potential economic headwinds. With the VIX hovering around 17.27, it indicates a moderate level of fear in the market, but it’s not at panic levels, which is somewhat reassuring for traders looking to sell premium.

Currently, the implied volatility rank for SPY stands at 97.96, which is quite high. This means that options are relatively expensive right now. If you’re considering selling premium, this could be an opportune moment, but you’ll want to be cautious about the potential for volatility spikes, especially with the Fed meeting on the horizon. The high IV rank suggests that the market is pricing in significant movement, and if you’re looking to sell options, you might want to consider strategies that can capitalize on this elevated volatility.

As for key price levels, immediate support for SPY is around 740, while resistance is seen at the 744.53 level, which was today’s high. Watching these levels will be crucial as they can guide your trading decisions. If SPY can break above 744.53, it could signal further bullish momentum, while a drop below 740 might indicate a return to bearish sentiment.

Looking ahead, we have the Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update releasing this afternoon, which could impact the real estate sector and overall market sentiment. Additionally, the Fed meeting on July 29 is a significant event that traders will be watching closely. Any hints of policy changes or economic outlooks could lead to increased volatility in the weeks to come. With the market still processing recent economic data, these upcoming events could be pivotal in shaping the direction of SPY.

In summary, for options traders considering positions this week, the high IV rank presents an attractive opportunity for selling premium, but be mindful of the potential for increased volatility as we approach key economic events. The market is at a critical juncture, and positioning yourself wisely could yield favorable outcomes.

Here’s the thing: top product managers are increasingly leveraging AI not just as a tool, but as a strategic partner in decision-making. This is especially relevant for you as a senior PM looking to streamline your workflow and enhance your intuition for options trading like with SPY. What that means in practice is shifting your mindset to see AI as an extension of your analytical capabilities rather than just a shiny tech toy.

For instance, consider how you might apply this in your current role. Instead of manually sifting through data to identify trends or pricing anomalies, imagine deploying an AI model that can analyze historical SPY option price movements and volatility patterns, adjusting for factors like IV rank in real-time. This way, you’re not just reacting to market conditions; you’re proactively shaping your trading strategies based on predictive analytics. When you think about it, it’s like having an extra set of eyes on the market—eyes that can process vast amounts of data instantly and provide insights that might take you hours to uncover on your own.

Now, let’s connect this to your trading decisions. If you start integrating AI tools that can simulate various market scenarios based on your defined-risk spreads, you gain a clearer picture of potential outcomes. This can help you prioritize which trades to execute based on their probability of success and risk-reward ratios. The move here is to experiment with this technology not just for efficiency, but for strategic advantage, allowing you to make data-driven decisions that might otherwise feel gut-driven.

So, here’s a specific action you can take this week: identify one area of your current workflow that feels particularly time-consuming or data-heavy. Research AI tools or platforms that can help automate or analyze that aspect. Whether it’s a tool for better data visualization or a predictive analytics platform, find something that resonates with your needs. By the end of the week, integrate that tool into your routine and see how it shifts your approach to both product management and your trading strategies. This small step could lead to significant gains in your efficiency and decision-making prowess.

curriculum.lesson: Stop losses on spreads: the 2x rule and when to break it

segments.name: SPY Learning: Stop Losses on Spreads: The 2x Rule and When to Break It
PM Concept: GitHub for PMs: Version Control for Everything You Build With AI

episodes.shownotes_html:
Daily Brief — 2026-06-29

What we covered

  • World news: SpaceX Nasdaq Surge Meets China Export Rebound—ETF Flows and Trade Thaw in Focus
  • SPY learning: Stop Losses on Spreads: The 2x Rule and When to Break It
  • Market brief: SPY Daily Brief: June 29 — Comm Services Leads Recovery, But Tech Stumbles
  • PM/analytics: GitHub for PMs: Version Control for Everything You Build With AI

Summary

SpaceX is being fast-tracked into the Nasdaq-100 through a new inclusion framework, which will trigger automatic ETF buying demand and reshape mega-cap tech weightings. This is a structural liquidity event for large-cap indices and a signal that the Nasdaq is willing to move quickly on private-to-public transitions. China’s economy is picking up steam in June, driven partly by a rebound in U.S. exports flowing into the country. This suggests the trade war pause is allowing Chinese manufacturers to rebuild inventory and restart production cycles that feed global supply chains. Hormuz oil transits are continuing despite recent attacks, though shipping owners remain spooked and risk premiums are elevated. The corridor is holding for now, but any escalation would immediately spike energy costs and inflation expectations across developed markets.

Let me paint a picture for you. Imagine you’re at a concert venue, and you’ve bought a ticket for the front section at full price. But you also bought insurance — you paid someone a smaller fee to agree that if the show gets cancelled, they’ll refund you the difference between what you paid and what your ticket’s actually worth at that point. Now here’s the thing: you didn’t pay for that insurance upfront out of kindness. You negotiated it because you wanted to sleep at night. If the show starts looking like it might get rained out, you don’t immediately panic and sell everything. You watch the weather. You think about how much the insurance is actually protecting you. And you make a decision: do I bail now, or do I let this play out? The key insight is this — your maximum loss is locked in. You know exactly how bad things can get. So the question isn’t whether you’re protected. It’s whether the protection you bought is still working the way you expected it to work.

That’s the essence of managing a spread. When you sell a call spread or a put spread, you’re doing exactly this — you’re taking in money upfront, you’re capping your maximum loss, and you’re agreeing that if things move against you, you’ll accept a predetermined hit. The natural question here is: if my loss is already capped, why do I need a stop loss at all? Here’s how to think about it: capping your loss and managing your loss are two different things. Yes, your spread has a maximum loss built in. But that maximum loss only happens if you hold all the way to expiration and the trade goes completely against you. A stop loss is about deciding: at what point does the risk-reward of this trade stop making sense, even though I could technically hold longer? It’s about capital efficiency and not letting bad trades tie up your money waiting for a miracle.

Now let’s translate this to real trading. Today SPY is at 734.57, and volatility is running hot — IV rank is sitting at 100, which is extreme. That matters because when you sell a call spread, you’re selling premium, and premium is juicier when IV is elevated. Let’s say you sold the 735/740 call spread on the July 24 expiration, which is 25 days out. You collected maybe 1.20 in credit. Your max loss is 3.80 — the width of the spread minus what you collected. The traditional rule that gets thrown around is the 2x rule: if your spread loses twice what you collected, you take the loss and move on. So in this case, that would be a 2.40 loss, which is about 63% of your max loss. Here’s the reasoning: if you collected 1.20 and you’re already down 2.40, the math is telling you something. Either volatility crushed and you’re not getting paid for the risk anymore, or the market moved hard against you and time decay isn’t saving you like you thought it would. The 2x rule is a way of saying: this trade isn’t working as designed, so let’s not hope it magics itself back into profitability.

But here’s where it gets interesting, and this is where thinking like a systematic trader means knowing when to break your own rules. The 2x rule assumes all spreads are created equal, and they’re not. Today’s environment is a perfect example. IV rank at 100 means volatility is at the absolute top of its range over the past year. When you sold that 735/740 call spread, you were selling into peak IV. That’s good for premium collection. But it also means if IV compresses — and it will, because 100 is unsustainable — your spread gets crushed even if SPY doesn’t move much. So imagine this scenario: it’s three days from now, SPY is still at 734, but IV rank drops to 75. Your spread, which looked profitable when you sold it, is now showing a loss because the premium you collected is evaporating faster than theta is working in your favor. Now you’re at your 2x stop loss. The old rule says: exit. But here’s the reasoning that might make you pause: if IV came in that hard that fast, it might be overdone. You’re 22 days from expiration. The 735/740 calls are still out of the money. Theta is about to accelerate. The question becomes: am I exiting because the trade is broken, or am I exiting because the market had a tantrum and I’m panic-selling into weakness? Those are very different things.

This is where you need to layer in what happened to get you to the stop loss. The 2x rule works beautifully when you’re stopped out because the market ripped through your short strike — that’s a clear signal the trade didn’t work. But if you’re stopped out because IV compressed and the market barely moved, you’re in a different situation. You’re looking at a trade that’s still structurally sound but temporarily underwater due to volatility mean reversion. That’s a situation where breaking the 2x rule might make sense. Not because you ignore risk, but because you’re making a conscious decision that the premium you collected is still adequate for the risk you’re taking, given what you now know about volatility. Today, with IV rank at 100 and communication services leading the market up 2 percent while materials lag down 1.5 percent, we’re in a regime where sector dispersion is high and broad-based volatility might not sustain. If you’re short calls on SPY, that’s actually a tailwind, not a headwind. Your spread is fighting for you.

So here’s the practical implication for today: if you’re considering selling premium on SPY ahead of July expiration with 25 days to go, the 2x rule is your safety net, but it shouldn’t be your only decision-making tool. You set it as a hard stop if the trade breaks structurally — if SPY blows through your short strike on a massive IV crush, that’s a clear exit. But if you’re stopped out on time decay and volatility compression while the market is still respecting your strikes, you pause and ask yourself: what’s the actual risk here now, not what was the risk when I entered? With IV rank at 100, you’re getting paid for rare events. If those rare events don’t happen and IV normalizes, that’s not the trade failing — that’s the trade working exactly as designed, just with a temporary mark-to-market loss. The difference between a trader who grinds out consistent returns and one who blows up is knowing the difference between a stop loss that protects you from a broken trade and a stop loss that just locks in losses at the worst possible time.

All right, let’s break down what happened in the market today. SPY closed at 734.57, up 0.77 percent on the day. Now, that’s a modest move in absolute terms, but context matters here. We’re coming off a pretty ugly Friday last week where the index dropped 0.72 percent, so today’s bounce is basically a full retracement of that damage. We’re now sitting just shy of where we were at the start of the week, which tells me the market is consolidating rather than committing to a directional bias. Volume came in at 8.4 million shares, which is below average for a Monday, so there’s not a ton of conviction behind this move. That’s important to note if you’re thinking about selling premium into this.

Let’s talk about what actually drove the move, because the sector breakdown is where the real story lives. Communication Services, ticker XLC, absolutely led the charge today, up 2.01 percent. That’s the biggest contributor to SPY’s gain by a wide margin. Consumer Discretionary followed suit, up 1.98 percent, so you had the growth-oriented, cyclical sectors really doing the heavy lifting. Industrials added 0.66 percent, and Financials chipped in 0.47 percent. But here’s the kicker: Technology, which is typically the engine of SPY, actually fell 0.5 percent today. That’s a meaningful divergence. Real Estate was the worst performer, down 0.66 percent, and Materials really got hammered, dropping 1.49 percent. So we had a classic “risk-on” day, but it was selective. You didn’t get broad participation. Tech weakness is something to watch closely because it usually signals either profit-taking or a shift in sentiment about growth.

Why did Comm Services and Discretionary pop while Tech stumbled? The headlines today give us some clues. SpaceX is being fast-tracked into the Nasdaq-100, which is driving ETF buying demand in that index. That’s a Comm Services and Tech story, but the real beneficiary seems to be the broader Comm Services complex rather than pure-play tech names. On the macro side, China’s economy is showing signs of life in June, with exports rebounding thanks to increased shipments to the U.S. That’s a cyclical tailwind for discretionary and industrial names. Meanwhile, the Fed Funds rate sits at 3.63 percent, down just one basis point from last month, and the 10-year Treasury is at 4.4 percent. We’re not in a cutting cycle yet, which means growth stocks have to earn their valuation on fundamentals, not on rate-cut expectations. That’s probably why Tech is taking a breather today.

Now let’s talk about the IV picture, because this is crucial for your decision-making as a premium seller. IV Rank is sitting at 100.0, which is an absolute extreme. That means we’re at the highest IV level in the past year. IV Percentile is 45.56, which tells us we’re above the median but not at the top of the distribution on a percentile basis. The current IV is 19.73, and the VIX is at 18.38. Here’s what this means for you: IV Rank at 100 is screaming “sell premium.” This is textbook territory for short calls, short puts, and defined-risk spreads. The call side is particularly juicy because you’ve got ATM calls at the 735 strike with 19.73 IV and a 0.53 delta. That’s fat premium relative to where realized vol is likely to be over the next 25 days. The put side is actually cheaper in absolute terms—11.93 IV on the 735 puts versus 19.73 on the calls—which suggests the market is pricing in more upside risk than downside risk. If you’re thinking about a call spread, you’re selling into peak IV. If you’re thinking about a put spread, you’re actually selling into relatively depressed IV on the put side, which is less attractive. The asymmetry here is worth noting.

Let’s nail down the levels you need to watch. SPY is currently at 734.57. Immediate resistance is at 739.89, which is today’s high and a natural technical ceiling. If we break above that, the next level to watch is 745, which would put us back toward the 52-week high of 760.4. On the downside, support is at 734.12, which is today’s low. Below that, we’ve got 728.99, which was Friday’s close. That’s the line in the sand. If we break below 728.99, we’re back in a downtrend, and that changes the character of the market. For now, we’re in a narrow range, which is actually ideal for selling premium because range-bound markets are premium seller’s markets.

What’s on the calendar that could move things? We’ve got the Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update coming in three days on July 2nd. That’s medium importance, so it could nudge things but probably won’t be a market-mover. The real event is the Fed Meeting on July 29th, which is 30 days away. That’s outside your immediate trading window if you’re working with 25-day expirations, but it’s definitely on the radar for longer-dated positioning. There are no major earnings releases in the next seven days, which means we’re in a relatively quiet period. That’s actually good for premium sellers because you don’t have binary event risk to worry about. The lack of catalysts means the market is likely to consolidate, and consolidation is where you make money selling spreads.

Here’s your one-liner for the day: IV Rank at 100 is a green light to sell call spreads or put spreads, but watch the put-call IV skew—calls are expensive relative to puts, so short call spreads are the higher-probability trade right now, especially if you’re targeting the 735 to 745 range as resistance.

Here’s the thing that separates a PM who ships consistently from one who’s always firefighting: you need version control for your decision-making infrastructure, not just your code. And I know that sounds abstract, so let me ground it in something you’re actually doing right now—building systematic trading rules.

When you’re iterating on a spread strategy, you’re making changes to your eval criteria constantly. You tighten your IV rank thresholds. You adjust your risk-reward ratio. You change which market regimes you’re willing to trade in. And what usually happens? You lose the thread. Six weeks in, you can’t remember if that tightened threshold actually improved your win rate or if you just got lucky on three consecutive trades. You’ve got no audit trail. No way to revert to what actually worked.

The insight from Aakash is that you should treat your trading rules exactly like you’d treat a codebase. Your entry criteria, your exit rules, your position sizing logic—these are your CLAUDE.md. They have versions. They evolve. And if you’re not tracking those versions with dates, rationale, and measured outcomes, you’re flying blind.

What that means in practice: every time you change a rule, you document it. Not in Notion where it disappears. In a structured format—literally a git repo if you’re technical, or a versioned spreadsheet with timestamps and A/B test results if you’re not. You write down why you made the change. What signal made you think it was necessary. Then you measure whether it actually improved your Sharpe ratio or your win rate over the next fifty trades.

The move here is that this forces you to think like a scientist, not a gambler. You can’t just chase the last losing trade and tweak everything. You have to isolate variables. You have to know which change actually moved the needle. And when something stops working—and it will—you can revert to a known good state instead of spiraling into a new optimization.

I’ll use an analogy: a PM shipping products without version control is like a trader who changes their entire system after every loss. You’re guaranteed to break something that was working while you’re trying to fix something that wasn’t. Version control for your rules gives you the confidence to experiment because you always have a rollback.

Your action this week: pick your single most important trading rule—let’s say it’s your entry IV rank threshold for premium sells. Document its current state with today’s date. Then commit to measuring its impact over your next twenty trades. That’s it. One rule, one version, one week of clean measurement. That’s how you build the discipline that separates systematic traders from reactive ones.
