segments.name: SPY Learning: Position Sizing: The One Rule That Separates Traders From Gamblers
PM Concept: How to Build a Company OS in Claude Code with Jiaona Zhang, CPO at Laurel

episodes.shownotes_html:
Daily Brief — 2026-06-26

What we covered

  • World news: Fed Succession Drama Meets Corporate Reshuffles—What Sticks for Markets
  • SPY learning: Position Sizing: The One Rule That Separates Traders From Gamblers
  • Market brief: SPY Daily Brief — June 26, 2026 | Healthcare Carries the Load as Tech Stumbles
  • PM/analytics: How to Build a Company OS in Claude Code with Jiaona Zhang, CPO at Laurel

Summary

Two big moves at JPMorgan: Doug Petno and Troy Rohrbaugh named co-presidents as Marianne Lake exits, effectively setting up the succession race to eventually replace Jamie Dimon. Meanwhile, incoming Fed Chair Warsh is stacking his advisory team with internal Fed picks, signaling he’s ready to reshape how the central bank thinks about inflation and monetary policy. Nothing market-relevant in Europe today—heat waves and earthquakes dominate the headlines, but no trade, rate, or supply-chain moves. China released new national standards for regulating AI agents with digital identity systems, part of Beijing’s broader push to control autonomous tech development. Hong Kong’s civil service got a flat 2 percent pay rise this year with promises of better compensation once the economy recovers, reflecting continued economic softness in the territory. India’s health ministry proposed easier drug imports for R&D and relaxed shelf-life rules to reduce pharma compliance burdens and improve inventory management. This is a modest pro-business regulatory move aimed at attracting more pharmaceutical research activity to the country. Iraq’s new PM launched an anticorruption campaign seizing $86 million in assets, a signal of institutional cleanup that could improve investment confidence in the region over time. A missile alert sounded in Dubai over an incoming projectile, adding to regional security tensions. Bangladesh is courting China for infrastructure and trade deals as it diversifies partnerships. Indonesia’s miner Merdeka Gold raised $310 million in a Hong Kong IPO, tapping Asian capital markets for expansion. Japan and Airbus are teaming up on an anti-submarine drone, reflecting regional defense spending and tech partnerships in the Indo-Pacific.

Let me start with something you’ve probably experienced without realizing it’s the exact same principle as position sizing in trading. Imagine you’re at a casino, and you walk in with two hundred dollars. You could throw all two hundred at the roulette wheel on your first spin. You might win big, sure. But if you lose, you’re done for the night. Now imagine instead you decide before you walk in: I’m only going to risk five dollars per spin. That way, even if you lose ten spins in a row, you still have a hundred fifty dollars left to keep playing. The person who bets all two hundred on one spin is hoping to get lucky. The person who risks five per spin is playing a game where they can survive bad luck long enough to hit good luck. That’s the entire intuition behind position sizing. It’s not about being conservative or timid. It’s about staying in the game long enough for your edge to actually work.

Here’s another way to feel it: think about a baseball player stepping up to bat. A rookie might swing as hard as possible at every pitch, trying to hit a home run every time. He’s going to strike out a lot. A veteran hitter, though, adjusts his swing based on the situation. Sometimes he bunts. Sometimes he takes a pitch. Sometimes he swings for the fences. He’s not playing scared—he’s playing smart. He knows that his edge over the pitcher compounds over hundreds of at-bats, but only if he’s still in the game. If he gets thrown out of the game in the second inning because he swung recklessly, his statistics for the season don’t matter. Position sizing is the batter’s discipline. It’s saying: I believe I have an edge in this market, but I’m only going to deploy enough capital on any single trade that a string of losses won’t force me out of the game.

Now let’s translate that to what’s actually happening with your money in the options market. Position sizing means this: before you enter a trade, you decide in advance what percentage of your total trading capital you’re willing to lose on that single position if it goes against you completely. Most professional traders risk between one and three percent of their account on any one trade. Some aggressive traders go to five percent. Almost nobody serious goes above that. Here’s how it works in practice: let’s say you have a fifty thousand dollar trading account. A one percent risk means you’re willing to lose five hundred dollars maximum on any single trade. A two percent risk means one thousand dollars. That’s your hard stop. You size your position—meaning you decide how many contracts to buy or sell—such that your maximum loss, if the trade hits your stop loss price, equals that predetermined amount.

Let’s make this concrete with SPY right now. SPY is trading at seven hundred thirty-three dollars as of today, and we’re looking at the July 24 expiration, which is twenty-eight days out. Say you’re considering a short call spread—you sell the 735 call and buy the 740 call, for example. Your maximum loss on that spread is the width of the strikes minus what you collected. The natural question here is: why would I care about this abstract percentage when I could just think about dollar amounts? Here’s how to reason through it: if you just think in dollars, you’ll make different decisions on different days based on your mood or how much you won yesterday. On a day when you’re feeling confident, you might risk a thousand dollars. On a day when you’re feeling cautious, five hundred. That inconsistency is exactly what kills accounts. But if you commit to a percentage of your account before the market opens, you remove emotion from sizing. You’re saying: my account is fifty thousand, I risk two percent, that’s one thousand dollars, period. Every trade gets the same discipline. That consistency is what lets you actually measure whether your edge is real, because you’re not confusing sizing decisions with trading decisions.

Here’s the second natural question: doesn’t limiting my risk to a percentage mean I can never make big money? Actually, the opposite. This is counterintuitive, but think about it: if you blow up your account by risking fifty percent on one trade and losing, you can’t make any money at all, because you’re done trading. The way you make big money is by staying in the game, taking consistent small wins, and compounding them. A trader who risks two percent per trade and wins on sixty percent of trades will, over time, turn fifty thousand into five hundred thousand. A trader who risks twenty percent and wins on sixty percent will blow up before they get there, because the law of large numbers hasn’t kicked in yet. The math works in your favor only if you’re still around to see it.

So what does this mean for you right now, looking at SPY at 733 on June 26th with IV rank at essentially zero—meaning volatility is near the bottom of its historical range? Here’s the practical implication: low IV rank is exactly when a lot of traders get excited about selling premium, because the premiums look juicy relative to what’s coming. But here’s what you need to think about: if you’re going to be more aggressive because IV is low and opportunity looks good, you absolutely cannot increase your position size. In fact, you might even want to decrease it slightly. Why? Because low IV rank means the market is complacent, and complacency often precedes volatility spikes. If you sold a 735 call spread when IV rank was fifty and it worked great, you might be tempted to sell the same position size now that IV rank is zero, thinking you’re just repeating a winning trade. But you’re not. You’re taking the same dollar risk in an environment where IV could expand violently, which means your Greeks are going to move differently. Your delta might not move as much as you expect, but your vega—your sensitivity to IV changes—could hurt you fast. The disciplined approach is to keep your percentage risk the same, which might actually mean selling fewer contracts or buying wider protection.

Let’s get even more specific with today’s data. SPY is at 733, and let’s say you have that fifty thousand account and you risk two percent per trade. That’s one thousand dollars maximum loss. Now, you’re looking at selling the 735 call, which has a delta of 0.51 and IV of 15.31. If you sold one call spread—short one 735 call, long one 740 call—your max loss is five hundred dollars (the five-dollar width). That means you could sell two spreads and still stay within your one thousand dollar risk budget. But here’s where position sizing forces you to think clearly: before you sell those two spreads, you need to ask yourself, not what the market might do, but whether you’re comfortable with the probability that both spreads blow through your max loss at the same time. With IV rank at zero, the market is pricing in less movement than usual. That 0.51 delta on the 735 call means roughly a fifty-one percent probability it finishes in the money. That’s not a high-probability trade. It’s a coin flip. So selling two of them, even though you can afford the dollar risk, might be psychologically and probabilistically wrong. Position sizing forces you to think about that alignment.

The real trading implication is this: position sizing is not a conservative constraint—it’s your edge-protection system. It’s how you separate traders who win from traders who get lucky then blow up. When you see IV rank at zero like it is today, when you see sectors like Healthcare leading with a 2.63 percent gain while Technology lags at negative 0.96, when you see SPY down just 0.17 percent but having dropped 1.45 percent two days ago—all of that creates a temptation to do more, to take bigger bites, to capitalize on the moment. Position sizing says: no. You take the same size bite every time. That discipline is what lets you actually compound gains instead of giving them back in one bad trade. You’re building a system, not hunting for a score. And systems beat hunting every single time.

All right, let’s break down what happened in the market today. SPY closed at 733.02, down just 0.17 percent on the day. Now, that’s a small move in absolute terms, but context matters here. We’re down about 1.5 percent from Monday’s close of 744.39, so we’re in the middle of a mild pullback that started mid-week. Volume came in at 7.4 million shares, which is pretty normal for a Friday afternoon in late June. The real story isn’t the size of the move — it’s the composition of it. This wasn’t a broad-based decline. This was a tale of two markets, and that’s exactly the kind of nuance you need to see when you’re thinking about where to position premium.

Let’s talk sector attribution, because this is where the intelligence lives. Healthcare, the XLV, absolutely crushed it today, up 2.63 percent and carrying most of SPY’s weight. That’s a meaningful outperformance. Consumer Staples followed with a 1.11 percent gain, and Real Estate added 0.72 percent. These are defensive, lower-beta plays, and they’re the ones doing the heavy lifting. On the flip side, Technology, the XLK, fell 0.96 percent — that’s the biggest drag on the index by a country mile. Industrials dropped 0.67 percent, and Energy was slightly negative at minus 0.31 percent. So what you’re seeing is a classic risk-off rotation. Money is moving out of the high-beta, growth-sensitive sectors and into the safety trades. That’s a signal worth paying attention to.

Why is this happening? Well, the macro backdrop is getting a little bit tense. We’ve got inflation at 4.27 percent year-over-year, up from 3.95 percent last month — that’s a 32 basis point jump. The Fed funds rate is sitting at 3.63 percent, and the 10-year Treasury has actually come down to 4.41 percent from 4.50 percent, which means the yield curve is steepening slightly. The spread between the 2-year and 10-year is now 31 basis points, up from 30 basis points yesterday. That’s not a huge move, but it’s consistent with a market that’s pricing in some economic uncertainty. Consumer sentiment has also rolled over — it’s at 49.8, down from 53.3 in April. That’s a 3.5-point drop, and that kind of deterioration tends to favor defensive sectors like healthcare and staples over discretionary and tech. The market is essentially saying: inflation is sticky, growth might be slowing, and we’re going to rotate into the stuff that doesn’t care about the economic cycle.

Now let’s talk about what this means for your premium-selling decision. Current IV rank is sitting at zero, which is genuinely interesting. That’s the lowest percentile reading you can get. IV percentile is at 49.4, which means we’re right in the middle of the range. The VIX closed at 20.21, which is elevated but not panicked. Here’s the thing: an IV rank of zero tells you that implied volatility is at the absolute bottom of its 52-week range. That’s usually a signal that premium is cheap, and for someone who wants to sell defined-risk spreads, cheap premium is a problem. You’re not getting paid much for the risk you’re taking. The call IV on the ATM 733 strike is 15.71, and the put IV is 17.80 — notice the skew. Puts are trading at a 2.09 point premium to calls, which is typical in a risk-off environment. But the absolute levels are low. If you’re thinking about selling a call spread or a put spread, you’re going to be collecting thin premium in a market that’s showing signs of volatility expansion. That’s not ideal timing.

Let’s talk about the technical picture. SPY’s 52-week high is 760.40, and we’re currently sitting at 733.02, so we’re about 27 points below that high. The 52-week low is 610.83, which means we’re in the upper half of the range, but we’ve given back about 3.6 percent from the recent peak. Immediate support is around 726.86 — that’s today’s low. If we break below that, the next level to watch is the 724 to 725 area, which is where we found support earlier in the week. Resistance is right here at 733 to 735. If we can clear 735 with conviction, the next target is 740 to 742. But honestly, with the sector rotation we’re seeing and the IV environment, I wouldn’t be surprised to see us test the lower support levels before we get another push higher.

On the calendar front, we’ve got the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment index releasing this afternoon. That’s a medium-importance data point, and given that the headline number is already weak at 49.8, there’s a risk that today’s release could surprise to the downside and accelerate the risk-off move we’re already seeing. Beyond that, there’s nothing major until the Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update in six days, and then the Fed meeting is 33 days out on July 29th. The lack of major economic data over the next 48 hours actually gives the market room to digest what we’ve already learned about inflation and sentiment. No earnings to speak of in the next week, which means the move is purely macro-driven.

Here’s your practical takeaway: if you’re thinking about selling premium this week, you’re fighting two headwinds — IV rank at zero and a market rotating into defensive sectors, which typically compresses volatility further. Wait for either a bounce in IV or a clearer technical setup before you leg into a defined-risk spread. The risk-reward isn’t there yet.

Here’s the thing that should actually matter to you as you’re thinking about moving into a GPM role: the constraint isn’t your ability to see what needs building anymore. It’s your ability to compress execution cycles without losing coherence across the org. And that’s a fundamentally different problem than what you’ve been solving as a senior PM.

Jiaona Zhang at Laurel figured out something that sounds almost too clean to be true—a team of nine shipping what used to require ninety people. But it’s not magic, and it’s not about working harder. It’s about building what she calls a Company OS, and the move here is understanding what that actually means operationally, because it changes how you think about discovery, prioritization, and how you hand off work.

A Company OS isn’t a tool. It’s a shared, living artifact that codifies your product thinking, your go-to-market logic, your metrics definitions, and your decision trees—all in a format that AI can actually reason over. Think of it like this: right now, when you brief a junior PM or a new engineer, you’re doing a lot of translation work in real time. You’re filling in context gaps, correcting misinterpretations, re-explaining trade-offs. That’s not a people problem; that’s an information architecture problem. When that information lives in Slack threads, docs, and your own head, every new person starts from zero.

What changes when you systematize it is that your leverage multiplies without you adding management overhead. A junior PM can run discovery against the OS and surface insights that actually cohere with your strategy. An engineer can reason about why a particular feature matters relative to others without needing a thirty-minute sync. You’re not replacing judgment; you’re removing the translation tax.

For someone building intuition around premium selling on SPY, there’s a direct parallel here. You don’t want to be manually recalibrating your IV rank thresholds, your delta targets, and your exit logic every single trade. You want those rules codified and testable so that when market conditions shift, you’re adjusting the parameters, not re-inventing the framework. Same principle.

The specific action this week: audit one cross-functional workflow you own—something that touches discovery, engineering, and go-to-market. Write down the decision criteria you’re actually using that live only in your head or in scattered documents. That’s your starting point for building a scalable OS. You don’t need Claude or fancy tooling yet. You just need to see the gap.

segments.name: SPY Learning: Reading the Market for Trade Setup: Your Weekly Routine When IV Is Historically Crushed
PM Concept: How to Build a Company OS in Claude Code with Jiaona Zhang, CPO at Laurel

episodes.shownotes_html:
Daily Brief — 2026-06-25

What we covered

  • World news: Oil Relief and Banking Strength: Fed Stress Test Green Light Meets Middle East De-escalation
  • SPY learning: Reading the Market for Trade Setup: Your Weekly Routine When IV Is Historically Crushed
  • Market brief: SPY Daily Brief: Industrials Lead as Tech Stumbles, IV Rank at Historic Lows
  • PM/analytics: How to Build a Company OS in Claude Code with Jiaona Zhang, CPO at Laurel

Summary

JPMorgan and Goldman Sachs both got clean bills of health from the Federal Reserve’s stress test and are now returning capital aggressively—JPMorgan announced a $50 billion buyback and Goldman raised its dividend. This signals the Fed sees the banking system as resilient enough to weather a serious downturn, which matters because it removes tail risk from the market’s baseline scenario. Oil prices have fallen back to pre-Iran war levels as shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz is gradually resuming, easing one of the biggest geopolitical supply shocks of the year. For Europe and global energy markets, this is significant because it reduces inflation pressure and takes some heat off central bank rate expectations. China is publicly vowing to pursue tariff cuts and win-win cooperation with the US while simultaneously restricting dozens of American firms in response to earlier US actions, showing that cooperation and confrontation are running in parallel. This mixed signal matters because it keeps the trade war in a holding pattern rather than escalating, but doesn’t resolve the underlying uncertainty for multinational supply chains.

Let me walk you through how to think about preparing for a trading week, and I’m going to anchor this to something very real happening right now: SPY is at 730.94, we’ve dropped about 16 points in five days, and IV rank is sitting at zero. That’s the bottom. That’s not normal. So let’s start with an analogy that makes this intuitive.

Imagine you’re a parent planning your week. Every Sunday night, you look at the weather forecast. Some weeks the forecast is wild—thunderstorms everywhere, unpredictable, lots of uncertainty. Other weeks the forecast is boring: sunny, calm, no surprises. Now here’s the thing: when the forecast is boring and predictable, you don’t need to prepare as heavily. You don’t buy extra umbrellas or cancel outdoor plans. But when the forecast is chaotic, you prepare differently. You check multiple sources, you have backup plans, you’re more cautious about commitments. You’re not predicting the weather; you’re just reading the signal it’s sending and adjusting your week accordingly. The same logic applies to markets. Some weeks the market is sending signals that things are calm and orderly. Other weeks it’s screaming that volatility is about to spike. Your job as a trader isn’t to predict what happens—it’s to read what the market is currently telling you and structure your week around that reality.

Now let’s translate that to what’s actually happening with SPY options right now. When we talk about “reading the market for your weekly routine,” we’re really talking about understanding implied volatility rank and percentile, and then building your trade setup calendar around that signal. Here’s the concrete version: SPY’s IV rank is at 0.0—meaning it’s at the absolute bottom of its 52-week range. The IV percentile is 99.4, which means implied volatility is lower than it’s been 99.4% of the time over the past year. Your VIX is 18.76, which is calm. The natural question here is: does this mean volatility is about to explode, or does it mean the market genuinely feels safe right now? Here’s how to think about it: IV rank and percentile don’t predict direction. They tell you about the cost of protection and the expectation of future movement that’s already priced in. When IV rank is at zero, it means traders collectively are pricing in very little expected movement for the next 29 days. That’s not a prediction—it’s a consensus expectation. The 730 call has an IV of 23.92, the 730 put has an IV of 10.34. Notice the skew? Calls are priced with more uncertainty than puts. That’s telling you something about where the market thinks risk lives. But here’s the key for your weekly routine: when IV rank is this low, you have two very different weeks available to you. You can be a seller of premium, betting that realized volatility stays below that 23.92 expectation. Or you can be a buyer of premium, betting that something breaks and IV explodes. Your weekly routine changes based on which one you’re positioned for.

So what does this actually change about how you prepare your week? Let’s get specific with today’s data. You’re looking at SPY down 16 points in five days—that’s realized volatility. You’re looking at IV rank at zero—that’s the price of protection. Here’s the trader’s reasoning: the market has sold off, but it hasn’t panicked. The VIX is 18.76, not 25. The IV percentile of 99.4 tells you that even after this selloff, traders are still pricing in less future volatility than normal. That’s the setup signal. Your weekly routine should include this question: is this a capitulation bottom where IV is about to spike, or is this just a normal correction in a market that’s genuinely calm? Look at the sector data. Industrials (XLI) are up 2.11%, Healthcare is up 1.48%, Materials up 1.11%. Technology is the laggard at minus 0.98%. This isn’t a panic. This is a rotation. When you see that kind of breadth—most sectors up even as SPY is down—you’re looking at a market that’s rebalancing, not breaking. That changes your week. If this were a panic, you’d see broad weakness, VIX spiking, and IV rank moving up fast. Instead, you’ve got calm IV and sector rotation. Your routine this week should be: assume the selloff is contained. That means premium selling is more attractive than premium buying. The 730 call at 23.92 IV is cheap relative to historical norms, but it’s the right premium to sell if you think SPY stays range-bound.

Now here’s where the routine gets tactical. You’ve got 29 days to July 24 expiration. The natural question is: should I sell weeklies and adjust constantly, or should I sell monthlies and let theta work? Here’s the reasoning: when IV rank is at zero, you’re in a harvesting mode, not a hunting mode. Weeklies give you faster theta decay, but they require active management. Monthlies give you less theta per day, but they give you room to work. With IV this compressed, monthlies make more sense because you’re not trying to catch a volatility spike—you’re trying to extract premium before the market realizes it’s underpriced. The 733 call has a delta of 0.55 and IV of 0.2. That’s 29 days of time decay, one strike above the money, and almost no implied volatility. If you sold a 733 call and bought a 735 call, you’d have a defined-risk call spread. Your max profit is the width of the spread minus what you paid. Your risk is capped. And here’s the key: because IV rank is at zero, that spread is cheaper to buy protection on than it normally would be. You’re selling 0.55 delta at a very low IV, and you’re buying 0.35 delta protection at an even lower IV. The math favors the seller this week.

But let’s zoom out to your actual weekly preparation routine. Here’s what you’re doing every Sunday night when you see data like this: First, you check IV rank and percentile. Today it’s 0.0 and 99.4. That’s the loudest signal on the board. Second, you look at realized volatility over the past five days—we dropped 16 points, which is about 2.2% in five trading days. That’s moderate, not extreme. Third, you check sector rotation—are we seeing panic selling or rebalancing? We’re seeing rebalancing. Fourth, you ask yourself: what’s the market pricing in for the next 29 days? With IV at 23.92, the market is pricing in roughly 2.4% annualized volatility, which translates to about 0.8% expected daily moves. SPY moves more than that on most days. So the market is underpricing expected movement. That’s your setup. Your routine for the week is: identify where premium is cheapest relative to what might actually happen, then build defined-risk structures that profit if you’re right. You don’t have to predict direction. You just have to read what the market is telling you and position accordingly.

All right, let’s talk about what happened in the market today. SPY closed down about 23 basis points, finishing at 730.94. Now, that’s a small move in absolute terms, but it matters in context. We’re down about 16 points from last Thursday’s close of 746.74, so we’re in the middle of a modest pullback that started on Tuesday with that 1.45 percent drop. Today’s decline was the third down day in five sessions, and the fact that we’re down on lighter volume — we did about 8 million shares versus the typical 10 to 12 million — tells me there’s not a lot of conviction behind either direction right now. We’re still up about 30 points from the 52-week low of 608, so we’re not in panic territory, but the momentum has definitely shifted from the early-June strength.

Here’s what’s interesting about today’s move: it was a sector rotation story, not a broad-based selloff. Industrials, the XLI, absolutely crushed it, up 2.11 percent and carrying most of the weight that kept SPY from falling harder. Healthcare followed with a 1.48 percent gain, and Materials added 1.11 percent. Those three sectors are what kept the index from being down half a percent or more. But here’s the problem — Technology, the XLK, fell 0.98 percent, and that’s your laggard. Consumer Discretionary dropped 0.66 percent, and Communication Services was down 0.64 percent. So you’ve got a classic defensive-to-cyclical rotation happening, where investors are rotating out of the mega-cap tech names and into industrials and healthcare. That’s a tell. When tech is the biggest drag on a day like this, it usually means there’s some concern about growth or valuations, or both.

Why the rotation? Well, the macro backdrop is worth examining. CPI came in hotter than expected last month at 4.27 percent year-over-year, up from 3.95 percent the prior month. That’s a 32 basis point jump, and it’s keeping the Fed in a holding pattern. The 10-year Treasury is sitting at 4.5 percent, down just one basis point from yesterday, so bond yields are relatively stable but elevated. The Fed Funds rate is at 3.63 percent, essentially unchanged. What this tells me is that the market is pricing in a longer period of higher rates, which is exactly the environment where you want to own industrials — companies that benefit from infrastructure spending and capital expenditure — and avoid the high-multiple, low-earnings-yield tech names that need rates to fall to justify their valuations. The JPMorgan stress test results and the $50 billion buyback announcement probably helped financials and industrials today, signaling that the banking system is healthy and ready to return capital. That’s a green light for cyclical strength.

Now let’s talk about what this means for you as someone thinking about selling premium. IV rank is sitting at zero, which is genuinely remarkable. That means implied volatility is at the lowest percentile of the past year. The current IV is 23.92, and the VIX closed at 18.76. For context, that’s a very calm market. The IV percentile of 99.4 tells you that IV is higher than 99.4 percent of all days in the past year, which sounds bullish for premium sellers, but here’s the catch — IV rank of zero means we’re at the absolute floor. There’s no room to compress further. What that means is you’re not getting paid for volatility expansion, and you’re taking on directional risk without the cushion of rising IV to bail you out if the trade goes against you. This is the environment where you want to be very selective about where you sell. You’re not getting paid enough for the risk unless you’re picking strikes that are genuinely out of the money and have a high probability of expiring worthless. The 731 call at 29 days to expiration is showing a delta of 0.53, which means it’s roughly at the money, and the IV is 23.92. That’s not a screaming opportunity to sell. You’d be better off looking at the 735 or 740 calls if you want to sell premium with a margin of safety.

Let’s talk about the technical picture. We’ve got immediate support at 729.60, which was today’s low. Below that, the next level is around 725, which would represent a break of the recent consolidation. Resistance is at 739.35, today’s high, and if we can clear that, the next target is 745 to 750, which would take us back to where we were last week. The 52-week high is 760.40, so we’re about 30 points below that. The key thing to watch is whether we hold 729 or if we break lower. If we break 729, we’re looking at a test of the 720 level, and that would be a more meaningful breakdown.

On the calendar, we’ve got the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment index releasing tomorrow, and that’s a medium-importance event. Consumer sentiment has been deteriorating — it’s at 49.8, down from 53.3 a month ago — so if tomorrow’s print comes in weak, that could reinforce the narrative that the consumer is slowing and that growth is at risk. That would probably accelerate the rotation out of tech and into defensives. Beyond that, the next big event is the Fed meeting on July 29th, which is 34 days away. That’s still a ways off, but it’s on the radar. There are no major earnings releases in the next seven days, so we’re in a relatively quiet period from a fundamental perspective.

Here’s your practical takeaway: IV rank at zero means you’re not getting paid for volatility, so if you’re going to sell premium this week, focus on defined-risk spreads where you’re collecting a reasonable credit relative to the width of the spread, and make sure you’re targeting strikes that have at least a 65 to 70 percent probability of expiring worthless — because without IV expansion as a tailwind, you need probability and time decay working hard in your favor.

Here’s the thing that should make you sit up: a nine-person team shipping what used to require ninety people isn’t about working harder or being smarter. It’s about fundamentally changing how you think about leverage and decision velocity.

What Jiaona Zhang did at Laurel is relevant to you as a PM in a way that goes beyond “oh, AI is cool.” The insight is about constraint-driven design. When you’re genuinely resource-constrained—not hypothetically, but actually—you stop building features. You start building systems that let your team compound their output. That’s the move.

In the options world, you see this with defined-risk spreads. You don’t have unlimited capital, so you engineer your edge into a structure. You’re not trying to win on direction alone; you’re winning on efficiency per dollar deployed. Same principle. Laurel didn’t hire more people. They built a “company OS”—a repeatable operating system that let nine people make decisions and execute like ninety.

What that means in practice: as you scale from IC PM to senior PM to GPM, you’re moving from shipping features to shipping decision frameworks. A junior PM thinks “we need to build X.” A senior PM thinks “we need to build the system that lets the team decide what to build, and execute it faster than competitors.” That’s leverage.

The concrete move here is thinking about your own bottleneck. Right now, where is your team getting stuck? Is it discovery? Prioritization? Execution? Stakeholder alignment? Most teams think they need more people. What Laurel figured out is that you often need better structure. A clearer north star. Faster feedback loops. Cleaner handoffs between teams. Those are free or nearly free if you design them right.

For you specifically, as someone learning to trade defined-risk spreads: this is about position management at scale. When you’re managing a portfolio of spreads, you can’t monitor each one individually at market open. You need rules. Adjustments triggers. Exit criteria. A system. That system is what lets you scale from managing two positions to managing twenty without blowing up.

Your action this week: pick one decision your team makes repeatedly—could be “what do we ship next sprint” or “how do we prioritize bugs”—and write down the actual process. Not the ideal process. The real one. Then ask: where are we losing time? Where do we re-litigate the same argument? That’s your leverage point. That’s where you build your company OS.

episodes.shownotes_html:
Daily Brief — 2026-06-24

What we covered

  • World news: Oil Peace Deal Reshapes Energy Markets; Prediction Markets Face Regulatory Squeeze
  • SPY learning: Reading the Market for Trade Setup: Your Pre-Trade Checklist
  • Market brief: SPY Daily Brief: Energy Collapse, Consumer Strength, and IV at the Peak
  • PM/analytics: Slow down to speed up: so much has changed in 6 months’ time

Summary

Americas: Trump administration ordered the DOJ to investigate major oil companies for ‘price gouging’ as gasoline prices remain elevated despite a sharp drop in global crude following a new US-Iran peace agreement. This signals potential antitrust pressure on energy majors and could reshape downstream margin dynamics if retail prices are forced lower. Europe: A severe heatwave is sweeping the continent with 94 million people experiencing temperatures above 35°C, causing power outages in France and triggering rare red alerts across the UK and Italy. Extreme heat typically spikes energy demand for cooling and can strain grid capacity, potentially supporting European natural gas and power prices. Asia-Pacific: South Korean stocks rebounded from sharp losses while Japanese markets extended declines, and China detained two Japanese nationals over alleged export control violations. Regional equity volatility and Japan-China trade friction add uncertainty to tech supply chains and semiconductor flows that feed into US corporate earnings. Middle East & Africa: US Secretary of State Rubio is touring Gulf allies to reassure them on details of the new US-Iran memorandum of understanding after crude prices fell sharply on the deal news. The geopolitical de-escalation is reshaping energy market expectations and could influence how Saudi Arabia and OPEC respond to lower prices. India: No market-relevant stories published today.

Alright, let’s talk about what you actually need to look at before you put a single dollar into a trade. Think of it like this: imagine you’re about to book a flight. Before you click buy, you check a few things, right? You look at the price, sure, but you also check if the airport is actually open that day, if there are delays, if the weather is going to be a nightmare. You’re not just looking at the ticket price in isolation. You’re building a picture of whether this is actually a good moment to fly. That’s exactly what a pre-trade checklist is. It’s your insurance policy against walking into a trade that looks good on paper but is actually a disaster waiting to happen.

Let’s say you’re thinking about selling premium on SPY. You see a nice fat premium number on a call spread, and you’re tempted. But before you do anything, you need to ask yourself: what’s the actual state of the market right now? Is volatility elevated or crushed? Is the underlying trending or choppy? Are there any major economic events coming? Are the options actually liquid enough to get out if you need to? These aren’t boring questions. These are the questions that separate traders who blow up from traders who compound money year after year.

Here’s the natural question that comes up: why does it matter what the overall market looks like if I’m just trying to make money on a specific spread? Here’s how to think about it. Your spread exists inside a much larger ecosystem. If the market is in a panic, volatility expands, and suddenly your short calls are underwater. If the market is flat and boring, premium decays faster than you expected, but you might also get whipsawed by random moves. If you’re selling premium, you need to know if you’re selling into a situation where volatility is likely to stay elevated or if it’s about to collapse. You also need to know if the underlying is in a strong trend, because that changes your risk profile dramatically. A short call spread is much safer in a choppy market than in a market that’s making new all-time highs every day. So the checklist isn’t busywork. It’s foundational risk management.

Let’s ground this in today’s actual market. SPY closed at 735.61 today, up 0.28 percent. That’s basically flat. But look at what happened over the last five days: we went from 740.96 last Wednesday, ripped up to 746.74 on Thursday, sold off to 733.58 yesterday, and bounced back to 735.61 today. That’s a 13-point range in five days on a 735 stock. That’s meaningful chop. Now, here’s where the checklist starts to matter. IV rank is at 100 right now. That’s maxed out. That means volatility is at the highest level it’s been over the last year. The actual IV is 17.72, which is elevated compared to where it’s been. The VIX is at 18.99. So the market is nervous. It’s not panicking, but it’s definitely not comfortable.

Now, the natural follow-up question is: if IV is at 100, does that mean I should sell premium? And here’s where you have to think like a trader, not a textbook. IV rank at 100 means volatility is high relative to its recent history. That sounds great for premium selling, right? You’re getting paid more. But you have to ask: why is volatility so high? Is it because the market is nervous about something specific that’s about to resolve, or is it because the market is actually uncertain and that uncertainty might persist? Look at the sectors today. Consumer Discretionary is up 1.73 percent, leading. Energy is down 2.04 percent, lagging. Technology is basically flat, down 0.13 percent. There’s no clear conviction. The market is bifurcated. That’s a yellow flag for a premium seller. When you have mixed signals like this, you’re in a regime where things can move in unexpected ways. IV might stay high, or it might compress quickly once we get clarity. You don’t know yet.

So here’s what your checklist actually looks like on a day like today, and this is the part that separates professionals from amateurs. First, you look at price action and trend. SPY is choppy, not trending. That’s actually neutral to slightly bullish for a short call seller because the underlying isn’t making aggressive new highs. But it’s also not giving you a clear downtrend to short puts into. You’re in a range, which is fine, but it means you need to be more careful about strike selection. Second, you check volatility regime. IV rank at 100 is elevated, but it’s also unstable. It got here because of the sell-off yesterday. That means you’re probably not at peak panic, and you might be looking at a regime where IV starts to compress as the market digests what happened. This matters for your trade because if you sell a call spread at these IV levels, and then the market stabilizes, your short premium will decay faster than your long premium, which is good for you. But if the market sells off again, IV will expand, and you’ll be sitting on losses. Third, you check liquidity. Look at the options chain for July 24th expiry, which is 30 days out. The ATM strike is 736. The 736 call has 393 open interest and 5 volume today. The 738 call has 803 open interest and 65 volume. That’s solid. You can actually trade these without moving the market. The puts are even more liquid, which tells you the market is willing to take both sides.

Fourth, and this is critical, you check for macro events or earnings. We’re not at an FOMC meeting. There are no major economic data points dropping today. SPY components don’t have a huge earnings dump coming in the next 30 days. That means you’re not walking into a known volatility catalyst that could blow up your trade. Finally, you check the actual Greeks to see if the premium you’re getting paid justifies the risk you’re taking. Look at the 738 call for July 24th. It’s got a delta of 0.51, which means it’s basically at-the-money on the upside. IV is 17.39. If you sold a call spread like 738/745, you’d be collecting premium on a short 738 call with delta 0.51 and buying back that risk with a long 745 call with a much lower delta. The question is: for the premium you collect, are you comfortable with the risk that SPY rallies past 745 in the next 30 days? Given the choppy price action and the mixed sector leadership, that seems like a reasonable risk to take. But you only know that because you did the checklist.

Here’s what this all means for you right now. Today’s setup is not a screaming sell-premium day, but it’s a valid one. IV is high enough that you’re getting paid decent premium. The underlying is choppy enough that you have room to work. Liquidity is good enough that you can get in and out. Macro is clean enough that you’re not walking into a surprise. But because IV is at 100 and unstable, you probably want to be a bit more conservative with your strike selection than you would be on a normal day. Maybe you don’t sell the 738 call. Maybe you sell the 740 or 741 call instead, give yourself a bit more breathing room, and accept that you’re giving up some premium. Why? Because you did your checklist, and the checklist told you that volatility is fragile right now. It got to 100 because of recent fear, not because of structural uncertainty. So you want to be positioned for a potential IV compression. You also want to make sure that if you’re selling calls, you’re in a market where the upside risk is real but not imminent. The 1.73 percent pop in Consumer Discretionary today is a reminder that rallies can happen quickly. So you protect yourself.

The pre-trade checklist isn’t sexy. It’s not the part of trading that makes you feel like a genius. But it’s the part that keeps you in the game. Every professional trader you know does this. They don’t always do it consciously anymore because they’ve internalized it, but they do it. They look at price action, they check volatility regime, they verify liquidity, they scan for events, and they validate that the Greeks match their risk appetite. Then they trade. And then they win more often than they lose. That’s the difference between a trader and a gambler. So before you place your next SPY spread, run through the checklist. It’ll take you five minutes. And those five minutes will save you thousands of dollars over the course of your trading career.

All right, let’s break down what happened in the market today. SPY closed at 735.61, up just 0.28 percent on the day. Now, that’s a modest move in absolute terms, but context matters here. We’re coming off a brutal 1.45 percent drop yesterday that took us down to 733.58, so today’s bounce is a relief rally more than anything else. We’re still down about five points from where we were at the start of the week, and we’re sitting roughly 25 points below the 52-week high of 760.40. The volume came in at 8.4 million shares, which is decent but not exceptional. This feels like a day where the market caught its breath after yesterday’s selloff, but conviction isn’t particularly strong either direction.

Now, here’s where it gets interesting for your thesis. The move today was not broad-based. Consumer Discretionary, represented by XLY, absolutely led the charge, up 1.73 percent. That’s the biggest contributor to SPY’s gain. Industrials followed with a 1.29 percent pop, and Materials added 1.1 percent. Healthcare and Consumer Staples were in the middle of the pack, both positive but modest. But here’s the real story: Energy got absolutely hammered. XLE fell 2.04 percent, making it the worst performer by a significant margin. Financials also lagged, down 0.46 percent. Technology, which you’d normally expect to be a driver, was essentially flat, down just 0.13 percent. So what we’re seeing is a rotation away from the defensive and cyclical-sensitive sectors into more economically sensitive names. That’s a risk-on signal, but it’s being tempered by weakness in energy and financials.

Energy’s decline is worth dwelling on because it’s the biggest drag on the index today. We’re looking at a 2.04 percent drop in XLE, which is substantial. This likely reflects a combination of factors: crude oil weakness, concerns about global growth demand, and possibly some profit-taking after recent strength. The energy sector has been volatile, and today it’s signaling that traders are less confident about economic momentum. Financials’ weakness is also telling. We’ve seen the 10-year Treasury yield tick up five basis points to 4.51 percent, and the 2-year is now at 4.24 percent. That’s actually steepening the yield curve, which you’d think would be good for banks, but the market’s pricing in something else—maybe recession risk, maybe just a reassessment of rate expectations. Consumer Discretionary’s strength, on the other hand, suggests that at least some traders believe the consumer is holding up, even as sentiment data shows cracks. That’s the tension we’re living in right now.

Let’s talk about implied volatility, because this is critical for your premium-selling thesis. IV rank is sitting at 100.0. That’s the highest reading possible. We’re at the absolute peak of the volatility distribution over the past 52 weeks. Your current IV is 17.72, and the VIX closed yesterday at 18.99. This is a textbook environment for selling premium. When IV rank is at 100, you’re getting paid maximum dollar value for the theta decay you’re harvesting. The IV percentile of 45.18 tells you that while we’re at the peak of our own range, we’re not at extreme levels in absolute terms—the VIX isn’t at 30 or 40. But relative to where this market has been, this is as good as it gets for a premium seller. The skew is also interesting: your ATM calls at 736 strike are trading at 17.72 IV, but your puts are trading at 14.72 IV. That’s a 300 basis point skew in favor of calls, which is typical in a risk-off environment. Puts are cheaper, which means the market is pricing in less downside risk than upside. That’s something to keep in mind if you’re thinking about call spreads versus put spreads.

Let’s nail down the key levels you need to watch. Immediate support is sitting right around 733 to 734, which is where we bounced yesterday morning. That’s a critical zone. If we break below that, the next level of support is around 730, which would represent a more meaningful breakdown. On the upside, resistance is at 738 to 740. We haven’t been able to sustain a move above 740 this week, so that’s your ceiling. The 52-week high of 760 is still in play, but it’s going to take a meaningful shift in sentiment to get there. For your spread construction, I’d be thinking about that 738 to 740 zone as your short call strike if you’re selling call spreads, and 730 to 733 as your short put strike if you’re selling put spreads. You’ve got room to work with, and the IV is compensating you for it.

On the calendar, we’ve got some data coming that could move things. Tomorrow, the Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update releases. That’s medium importance, but given that housing starts have already rolled over—we’re down 215,000 from the previous month to 1.177 million—any weakness in mortgage rates could be a positive signal. Then in two days, on Friday, we get the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment number. This one matters because the last reading came in at 49.8, down 3.5 points from the previous month. Consumer sentiment is deteriorating, and that’s a headwind for Consumer Discretionary despite today’s strength. If Friday’s number comes in weak, you could see a reversal of today’s rotation. The Fed meeting isn’t until July 29th, so we’ve got some runway before that becomes the dominant narrative.

Here’s your practical takeaway: IV rank at 100 is screaming at you to sell premium, but the sector rotation and energy weakness suggest the market is nervous about growth. If you’re building a defined-risk call spread, you want to be short the 738 to 740 zone with a 30-day window. You’re getting paid 17.72 IV to take that risk, and the skew is in your favor. Just make sure you’re sizing for the possibility that energy weakness spreads to the broader market, because that’s the tail risk nobody’s talking about right now.

Here’s the thing that separates PMs who scale into GPM roles from those who plateau: they understand that velocity isn’t the same as direction. And right now, the engineering landscape is shifting so fast that most teams are optimizing for the wrong metric entirely.

What that means in practice is this. You’re probably measuring your team’s output by tickets closed, sprints completed, features shipped. But if the tech stack, the hiring market, the architectural constraints, or the competitive landscape have fundamentally changed in the last six months—and they have—then you’re measuring speed on a road that’s moved. You’re fast, sure. But you’re fast toward yesterday’s goal.

The move here is to build in deliberate deceleration cycles. Not because it feels good or because your team needs rest, though both are true. But because the cost of shipping the wrong thing at high velocity is exponentially higher than the cost of shipping the right thing slowly. Think about it like managing a short strangle in a high-IV environment. You can collect premium fast by selling everything, but if the underlying moves against you, your losses compound faster than your gains ever could. The PMs who win are the ones who size down, reassess, then re-enter with conviction.

For you specifically, this looks like: every six weeks, pull your team out of the sprint. Not for a retro, not for planning—for a genuine audit. What’s changed in your market? What’s changed in how your competitors are moving? What’s changed in your own org’s constraints or capabilities? Most teams skip this step because it feels like lost time. But it’s the opposite. It’s the difference between reacting to change and anticipating it.

Here’s the concrete move: this week, schedule a two-hour “state of the world” session with your tech lead and one senior IC. Don’t talk about what you’re building. Talk about what’s changed since January. The tools, the talent market, the regulatory environment, the cost structure. Write it down. Then ask yourself: are we still optimized for the world we’re building in, or are we optimized for the world we were in? If it’s the latter, your next sprint isn’t about shipping faster. It’s about shipping different. That’s the insight that gets you to GPM.

Daily Brief — 2026-06-23

What we covered

  • World news: Global Commodity Dynamics Shift Amid Trade Tensions
  • SPY learning: Reading the Market for Trade Setup
  • Market brief: SPY Daily Brief: Tech Selloff Drags Index Lower as IV Rank Hits Extremes
  • PM/analytics: Agent Loops for PMs: 20+ You Can Run This Week

Summary

In the Americas, the U.S. Soybean Export Council is ramping up efforts to reclaim market share from Brazil in China’s soybean market, emphasizing the superior quality of American crops. This rivalry could reshape trade flows and pricing in the agricultural sector, impacting commodities globally. In Europe, former Brexit negotiator Michel Barnier suggested the UK could rejoin the EU on a ‘short’ timeline, depending on future political maneuvers. Any shift in the UK’s relationship with the EU could have significant implications for trade agreements and economic stability in the region. In Asia, China’s consumer spending continues to languish, with the recent 618 shopping festival showing sharply reduced sales growth. This slowdown in consumption could signal broader economic challenges, affecting global supply chains and demand for imported goods. In India, the successful transit of 11 vessels carrying oil and LPG through the Strait of Hormuz comes after a new U.S.-Iran deal aimed at reducing regional tensions. This development is crucial for energy supply security, potentially stabilizing oil prices and impacting global markets. In the Middle East and Africa, Kenya has halted plans for a U.S.-run Ebola facility amid public backlash and a court order. This could affect international health funding and collaboration in the region, potentially influencing capital flows and investment sentiment. In the Asia Pacific region, recent reports reveal that Nippon Steel shares have fallen due to rising debt overshadowing gains from U.S. Steel’s revival. This financial distress could impact the steel market and related industries, influencing global supply dynamics.

Imagine you’re at a busy pizza place, and there are only a few tables left. Some people are eyeing a table near the window, while others are huddled around a table in the corner. Now, picture this: you overhear a couple of folks at the window table discussing how they think the pizza place will be even more packed tomorrow because of a big game. They might be betting on that table to get busier, so they decide to leave their name and pay a little extra to reserve it for later. This way, they get to enjoy their pizza without the hassle of waiting. In this pizza shop, the idea of reserving the table is like planning ahead based on what they think will happen in the future.

Now, let’s take that pizza analogy and connect it to the world of trading options on SPY. When you hear about options, think of them as a way to reserve your spot in the market—just like those diners at the pizza place reserving their table. If SPY is currently at 738.27, and you see the price of a call option at the 740 strike for next month, it’s like putting down a deposit to secure that table. You’re betting that SPY will be above 740 when it’s time to sit down. The ‘reservation’ you pay is the premium, which reflects how likely you think it is that SPY will reach that point by the expiry date. The current implied volatility (IV) of 15.51 shows how much the market expects SPY to move, kind of like how busy you expect the pizza place to be tomorrow.

Now, knowing this helps you as a trader because you can make more informed decisions. With an IV rank of 100 today, you’ve got a unique opportunity. It means that options are currently more expensive than they have been historically, so if you’re considering selling premium—like reserving those pizza tables—you might want to think strategically. For instance, if you sell a call spread around that 740 strike, you’re banking on SPY not exceeding that price by expiry. But here’s the kicker: with SPY’s current price and the high IV, you’re likely to collect a higher premium than usual, giving you a better risk-reward setup. If SPY starts to move down, or even if it stays flat, you could keep that premium, just like those diners enjoying their pizza while those who didn’t reserve wait in line. It’s all about reading the market, understanding the dynamics of supply and demand, and making your next trade based on informed intuition. So, when you see high IV, think of it as a chance to capitalize on the market’s expectations, just like how you’d want to snag that window table at the pizza place before everyone else does.

All right, let’s talk about what happened in the market today. SPY closed at 738.27, down 0.82 percent on the day. Now, that’s not a massive move in absolute terms, but context matters here. We’re down about 12 points from last Tuesday’s close of 750.33, and that’s a meaningful pullback over just five trading days. We’ve been choppy all week—down 1.25 percent on Wednesday, bounced 0.78 percent on Thursday, then basically treaded water yesterday before today’s decline. So we’re in a consolidation pattern, and frankly, it feels like the market is searching for direction.

Let’s dig into what actually drove today’s move, because the sector breakdown tells the real story. Technology absolutely got hammered. XLK fell 2.95 percent, and that’s the biggest drag on the index by a country mile. That’s your mega-cap tech exposure—the Magnificent Seven, the AI darlings, all of it—taking a real hit. On the flip side, you had Consumer Staples leading the charge up 1.42 percent, Healthcare up 1.08 percent, and Real Estate up 0.87 percent. So the market is rotating hard into defensive, lower-volatility names. Industrials also sold off, down 1.23 percent, which suggests some concern about economic momentum. Materials down 0.55 percent, Consumer Discretionary down 0.51 percent. Energy basically flat at minus 0.08 percent. Financials barely moved, up just 0.18 percent. This is a classic risk-off day dressed up as a modest decline, and the sector composition is screaming that traders are de-risking from growth and moving into safety.

Why is tech getting hit? The macro backdrop is worth examining here. CPI came in hotter than expected last month at 4.27 percent year-over-year, up from 3.95 percent the prior reading. That’s a 32 basis point jump, and it’s putting pressure on the narrative that inflation is cooling. The Fed funds rate is sitting at 3.63 percent, essentially unchanged, but the 10-year Treasury is at 4.46 percent, down just 3 basis points from last week. So rates aren’t collapsing, but they’re not rising either. What we’re seeing is a bit of a squeeze on growth expectations. Tech is the most rate-sensitive sector, and when inflation ticks up and growth concerns emerge, that’s when you see the kind of rotation we’re watching today. Add in the fact that China’s 618 shopping festival saw sharply slowing growth—that’s a real-time consumer spending indicator—and you’ve got a narrative that global demand might be softening. That hits tech earnings expectations.

Now, let’s talk about what this means for premium selling, because this is where it gets interesting for you. IV rank is sitting at 100.0 right now. That’s the absolute top of the range. IV percentile is 41.57, which means we’re in the upper quartile of realized volatility relative to the past year. The VIX closed at 19.61 today, up from 17.28 yesterday. That’s a meaningful spike. Current IV on the July 24 expiry—which is 31 days out—is 15.51. Here’s the thing: IV rank at 100 is screaming at you that premium is expensive relative to historical context. This is exactly the kind of environment where selling premium makes sense, but you need to be surgical about it. The market is rotating, not crashing, so you’re not looking at a volatility explosion. You’re looking at elevated but not extreme levels. For a defined-risk call spread seller, this is actually a decent setup. You’re getting paid for volatility that’s genuinely elevated, and the directional bias is downward in growth names, which gives you a tailwind.

Let’s establish some levels to watch. SPY’s 52-week high is 760.4, and we’re now 22 points below that. Immediate resistance is right around 740 to 742, which is where we’ve been bouncing this week. Support is 732.30, which was today’s low. If we break below that, the next level is around 728 to 730. On the upside, if we rally back through 745, we’re testing the 750 level that we closed at last Tuesday. The ATM strike for July 24 is 738, and you can see the call delta at that strike is 0.54, which means the market is pricing in roughly a 54 percent probability of finishing above 738 at expiration. That’s basically a coin flip, which tells you the market genuinely doesn’t know which way this goes from here.

Looking ahead at the calendar, we’ve got the Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update coming in two days on June 25th. That’s medium importance, but mortgage rates have been drifting lower—the 30-year is at 6.47 percent, down from 6.52 percent last week—so that could be a non-event. More important is the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment survey releasing in three days on June 26th. That’s a real-time read on how Americans are feeling about their financial situation, and given that the last print came in at 49.8, down sharply from 53.3 the prior month, this could be a market-moving number if it deteriorates further. The Fed meeting isn’t until July 29th, so that’s 36 days away and not an immediate concern, but it’s on the radar. No major earnings in the next week, so we’re in a relatively quiet period from a catalyst standpoint.

Here’s your practical takeaway: IV rank at 100 with a defensive sector rotation and a tech selloff is a textbook setup for selling call spreads on weakness. Target the 740 to 745 resistance zone, sell the 745 call, buy the 750 call, and collect premium on a 31-day window where you’ve got room to work and elevated volatility to compensate you for the risk. The market is telling you it’s nervous about growth, and that’s your edge.

Here’s the thing: as a senior PM, you’re likely already juggling multiple priorities, but what if I told you that the real magic happens when you start running agent loops? These are not just checklists; they’re a systematic way to improve your workflows and decision-making. Think of agent loops as a feedback loop on steroids, where each cycle not only captures data but actively informs your next steps. It’s about creating a living document that evolves with your project.

What that means in practice is that instead of waiting for a project to reach its conclusion to evaluate success, you’re embedding metrics and acceptance criteria into the regular workflow. For instance, let’s say you’re launching a new feature for SPY options trading on your platform. Instead of just waiting for user feedback post-launch, you could set up an agent loop that includes regular check-ins with your analytics team, tracking user engagement metrics in real-time, and clustering feedback from both users and stakeholders as the feature rolls out. This allows you to pivot quickly based on what’s working or not, rather than relying on a retrospective analysis.

The move here is to integrate these loops into your existing processes. For example, rather than a single end-of-sprint review, have a mini-review every week where you assess current metrics against your acceptance criteria. This keeps the team agile and aligned with user needs, while also minimizing wasted effort on features or functionalities that aren’t resonating.

This week, I challenge you to implement one agent loop in your current project. Identify a key metric that you can track regularly and set up a feedback mechanism around it. This could be as simple as a weekly check-in with your team or using a shared document to capture insights. The goal is to create a culture of continuous improvement that drives better decision-making and ultimately enhances your product outcomes.
