PM Concept: Building the most AI-pilled engineering team in the world | Fiona Fung

episodes.shownotes_html:
Daily Brief — 2026-06-22

What we covered

  • World news: Iran Peace Talks + Fed Remake + Energy Crunch: Three Wildcards for Risk-On This Week
  • SPY learning: IV Rank 98.7: Why This Number Changes Everything About Selling Premium Today
  • Market brief: SPY Daily Brief — June 22, 2026 | Tech Carries the Load, But IV Rank Screams Caution
  • PM/analytics: Building the most AI-pilled engineering team in the world | Fiona Fung

Summary

The US and Iran have begun peace talks in Switzerland with initial wins—Iran’s agreed to invite IAEA inspectors back, and the US Treasury just granted a 60-day waiver on Iranian oil sanctions through August 21st. This is the first real de-escalation signal after months of Strait of Hormuz closure threats, which matters because energy supply uncertainty has been a hard ceiling on risk appetite. Kevin Warsh, the new Fed chair, is quietly reshaping the institution with task forces rethinking virtually everything the Fed does—from monetary policy frameworks to operational structure. This isn’t a typical transition; it’s what insiders are calling regime change in a velvet glove, and it matters because market expectations around rate cuts, QE, and inflation targets could shift significantly depending on what these task forces recommend. A major explosion at Qatar’s Ras Laffan LNG complex killed 13 workers and injured dozens as operations were restarting after Iranian attacks halted them in March. The facility is one of the world’s largest LNG producers, so any extended downtime tightens global gas supplies and could push energy prices higher right when the Fed is trying to engineer disinflation.

Let me start with something you’ve probably experienced. Imagine you’re selling your used car, and you know the market really well. One week, everyone’s desperate for cars—maybe there’s a shortage, maybe it’s summer and everyone wants to road trip. You can ask a premium price because demand is insane. The next week, the market cools off, tons of inventory hits the lot, and suddenly you’re negotiating down. The price you can get depends entirely on how hungry the market is right now, not on the car itself. That’s the intuition we’re building today.

Now apply that to selling options on SPY. When the market is desperate to buy protection—when fear is high, when people are scrambling to hedge—the prices for options go up. Not because SPY moved, but because the demand for insurance went up. When the market is calm and nobody’s worried, option prices drop because there’s no urgency. You’re a seller, so you want to be selling when prices are high, when the market is hungry. You want to know: is this a good time to sell, or should I wait for the market to get more desperate? That’s what today’s lesson is really about.

In options language, we measure that desperation using something called IV Rank—implied volatility rank. Here’s the one-sentence version: IV Rank tells you where current option prices sit on a scale from the cheapest they’ve been in the last year to the most expensive. It’s a percentile. Today, SPY’s IV Rank is 98.7. That number means option prices are sitting at the 98.7th percentile of where they’ve been over the past 52 weeks. To put that in concrete terms: SPY is at 749.29, the VIX is 16.49, and the current implied volatility is 14.93. When you look at the July 17th expiration—that’s 25 days out—the ATM call at 749 strike is trading with an IV of 14.93 and a delta of 0.54. That same ATM put is at 13.0 IV with a negative 0.46 delta. The natural question here is: why does the put have lower IV than the call if they’re both at-the-money? Here’s how to think about it: put skew. When markets get nervous, traders bid up downside protection more aggressively than upside calls. The put IV of 13.0 versus call IV of 14.93 tells you the market’s fear is directional—people are more worried about a drop than a pop. But the bigger story is that 98.7 IV Rank. That’s not a normal day. That’s a day when option sellers are being paid premium that’s near the top of their annual range.

Here’s where it gets actionable for you as someone thinking about selling premium. The natural question is: if IV Rank is 98.7, doesn’t that mean I should sell? And the answer is: not automatically, and here’s why you have to think deeper. IV Rank at 98.7 tells you option prices are expensive relative to the past year. But it doesn’t tell you if they’re expensive relative to what’s actually going to happen next. That’s a crucial distinction. Look at SPY’s recent price action: it dropped from 754.83 on June 15th to 740.96 on June 17th—that’s a 1.25% drop in one day. Then it recovered to 749.29 by today, up 0.34%. So we’ve had volatility, we’ve had a scare, and now we’re stabilizing. The market was nervous. The IV Rank of 98.7 is partly because that fear pushed option prices up. Now the question becomes: is that fear justified going forward, or is it overdone? If you think the fear is overdone—if you think SPY is more likely to stay range-bound or drift higher over the next 25 days—then selling premium at 98.7 IV Rank is exactly what you want to do. You’re selling expensive insurance to people who are overestimating the risk. But if you think that volatility event we just saw is a warning sign, not a relief, then even though IV is expensive, you might want to wait. The IV Rank doesn’t make that decision for you. It just tells you the price is high.

So here’s the trading implication for today, June 22nd, with SPY at 749.29 and IV Rank at 98.7. If you’re a systematic seller of premium—which is what we’re training you to be—this is a high-conviction setup. You have 25 days until July 17th expiration. The ATM call spread, if you wanted to sell a call spread at 750/755, would give you IV of around 14.74 on the short 750 call. That’s premium you’re collecting at the 98.7th percentile. Your breakeven is better, your risk-reward is better, and you’re getting paid for volatility that the market thinks is coming but may not arrive. The puts are cheaper in IV terms—13.0 at the 749 strike—which tells you directional bias is down, but the absolute level is still elevated. If you’re comfortable being short premium in this environment, this is the time to do it. The natural question is: but what if I’m wrong and volatility actually does explode higher? Here’s the framework: if you’re selling defined-risk spreads—which is what we focus on—you’re capped on your loss. A 750/755 call spread costs you 5 dollars of risk per spread. You collect maybe 1.20 or 1.30 in premium at these IV levels. You’re risking 5 to make 1.25. That’s a 4.75-to-1 risk-reward. If you believe, based on the market structure and your thesis, that SPY is more likely to stay below 755 than explode above it over 25 days, then you’re getting paid fairly for that belief. The IV Rank of 98.7 is your confirmation that the market is pricing in real fear. You’re selling that fear back to the market at peak prices. That’s the edge.

But here’s what IV Rank doesn’t tell you, and this is critical for your thinking: it doesn’t tell you about realized volatility. Realized volatility is what actually happens. Implied volatility is what the market thinks will happen. They can diverge wildly. Right now, IV is at 14.93. That’s the market’s expectation. Over the past five days, SPY has moved about 1.25% down, then recovered 1.25%, then drifted up 0.34%. That’s real movement, but it’s not extreme. If realized volatility over the next 25 days stays around 12-15%, then selling at IV 14.93 is profitable—you sold high and it settles lower. But if realized volatility spikes to 25% or 30%, then you lose money on the short premium. The IV Rank of 98.7 is telling you the market is already pricing in elevated risk. The question you have to ask yourself is: is that risk real? Look at the sectors: Technology is up 1.28%, leading. Communication Services is down 1.42%, lagging. There’s no panic, no sector-wide capitulation. That suggests the recent volatility was a correction, not the start of a new downtrend. If that’s your read, then selling premium into 98.7 IV Rank makes sense. You’re taking the other side of the market’s fear, and you’re getting paid at peak prices to do it.

All right, let’s break down what happened in the market today and what it means for your premium-selling thesis this week. SPY closed at 749.29, up just 0.34 percent on the day. Now, that’s a modest move in absolute terms, but context matters here. We’re coming off a pretty rough stretch last week where we dropped 1.25 percent on Wednesday before bouncing back Thursday and Friday. So today’s small gain is basically consolidation after that volatility. We’re still sitting about 11 points below last week’s high of 760.4, which tells me the market is still figuring out its footing. Volume came in at 6 million shares, which is pretty normal for a Monday in late June. Nothing screaming panic or euphoria, just steady grinding higher.

Now, here’s where it gets interesting for your spread strategy. Technology, represented by XLK, absolutely carried the load today, up 1.28 percent. That’s accounting for the lion’s share of SPY’s gain. Financials followed with a 0.7 percent pop, which makes sense given the modest strength in equities. But here’s the tell: Communication Services, the XLC, fell 1.42 percent and was the clear laggard. Energy dropped 0.8 percent, Consumer Staples fell 0.66 percent, and Materials slipped 0.55 percent. So we’ve got a classic bifurcation happening. Tech is doing the heavy lifting while the defensive and cyclical sectors are rolling over. That’s not the broadest rally you want to see, and it suggests conviction is concentrated, not distributed.

Why is tech leading while comms and energy lag? Well, the AI compute narrative is still alive and well—there’s a piece circulating today about turning AI computing power into a tradeable commodity, which keeps the narrative bid under the mega-cap tech names. But the weakness in Communication Services is worth watching. That sector includes the big streaming and media names, and they’re getting hit. Energy’s decline is probably a function of the geopolitical noise around the Strait of Hormuz and U.S.-Iran peace talks, which is actually reducing oil risk premium. When energy falls on geopolitical de-escalation, that’s a sign the market is pricing in less tail risk, not more. That’s actually a headwind for volatility expansion.

Let’s talk about the IV environment, because this is critical for your decision-making. IV rank is sitting at 98.7, which is absolutely extreme. We’re in the 98th percentile of IV levels over the past year. That sounds bullish for premium sellers at first blush, but here’s the catch: IV percentile is only 25.85, meaning we’re actually in the lower quartile of realized volatility relative to where IV is priced. The VIX is at 16.49, and the current IV on the July 17 expiry is 14.93. That’s a disconnect. The market is pricing in more volatility than it’s actually delivering. Now, that can work in your favor if you’re selling premium—you’re getting paid for vol that doesn’t show up. But it also means the market is nervous about something, and that nervousness hasn’t fully resolved. The 10-year Treasury just jumped 6 basis points to 4.49, and the 2-year is up 15 basis points to 4.2. That’s a steepening curve and rising rates, which can be a headwind for equities if it accelerates. So yes, IV rank is high, but the setup feels fragile.

For price levels, you’re looking at immediate support at 746.74, which is Friday’s close. If we break below that, the next level is 740.96, which is where we bottomed on Wednesday. Resistance is at 750.18, today’s high, and then 754.83, which was last Monday’s close. The 52-week high is 760.4, and that’s still the psychological target if this rally continues. But I’d be watching the 750 level closely. That’s where a lot of call volume is sitting—27,279 contracts of open interest on the 750 calls in July. If we can’t hold above 750, that’s a sign the momentum is fading.

On the calendar, we’ve got the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment report coming in four days on June 26. That’s medium importance, but given that consumer sentiment has already rolled over—it’s at 49.8, down 3.5 points from the previous reading—that report could be a catalyst if it comes in weaker than expected. We also have the Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update in three days, which is less market-moving but still worth noting given the recent jump in rates. The next big event is the Fed meeting on July 29, which is 37 days out. That’s beyond your immediate trading window, but the chatter around Kevin Warsh’s plans to remake the Fed is already in the market, and that’s creating some uncertainty about policy direction.

Here’s my practical takeaway for you: IV rank at 98.7 is tempting, but the disconnect between IV percentile and IV rank, combined with rising rates and sector divergence, suggests this is a good environment to sell premium on defined-risk spreads rather than naked short calls. Your risk-reward is better if you’re collecting that elevated IV while capping your downside, especially with the 750 call level showing heavy open interest and the market still figuring out whether it wants to push higher or consolidate.

Here’s the thing that separates senior PMs from GPMs: most people think scaling a team is about hiring faster or documenting processes better. But Fiona’s insight about maintaining culture when roles blur is actually about something harder—it’s about accepting that your job as a leader fundamentally changes when your individual contributors start collaborating with AI agents as first-class team members.

What that means in practice is you can’t rely on the old playbook of clarity through hierarchy and role definition. When an engineer is pair-programming with Claude, and that Claude instance is also helping with code review, and then it’s drafting specs—suddenly your org chart doesn’t tell you where decisions actually live. That ambiguity kills most teams. But the move here is to lean into it intentionally rather than fight it.

Think about how you’d approach a new product discovery process. You’d run experiments, measure what sticks, iterate fast. That’s exactly what Fiona’s doing with her team structure. Instead of pre-defining “Claude handles X, humans handle Y,” she’s creating feedback loops to watch where the collaboration actually creates value and where it creates friction. The team itself becomes the experiment.

Why this matters to you as someone learning to think systematically about SPY spreads: you’re already doing this, actually. You’re not deciding upfront which Greeks matter most for a given position—you’re building a framework to observe which ones move your P&L under different market conditions, then doubling down on what works. The discipline is the same. Culture scales the same way a trading edge scales: through repeated observation and adaptation, not through beautiful org charts.

The practical thing Fiona’s doing that most PMs miss is creating explicit space for role drift. She’s not trying to prevent it. She’s building retrospectives and feedback mechanisms that let the team surface when an agent is doing work that should be human-owned, or when humans are doing work that should be automated. That’s a monthly rhythm, not a quarterly strategy.

Here’s your action this week: map one cross-functional workflow on your current roadmap and ask yourself where AI collaboration could create ambiguity in ownership. Don’t solve it yet. Just name it. Then run one retro specifically asking your team where they felt unclear about who owned a decision. That’s the beginning of scaling intentionally.

episodes.shownotes_html:
Daily Brief — 2026-06-19

What we covered

  • World news: Warsh Hawkish Pivot Dominates; SpaceX Stumbles; China Trade Tightens
  • SPY learning: Support and Resistance on SPY: The Levels That Matter
  • Market brief: SPY Daily Brief — June 19, 2026 | IV at All-Time High, Fed Hawkishness Reshaping the Tape
  • PM/analytics: The AI Product Operating Model

Summary

Fed Chairman Kevin Warsh signaled a much more hawkish stance than markets expected in Wednesday’s policy statement, with language changes suggesting less patience on inflation and potentially fewer rate cuts ahead. This is a sharp pivot from the dovish Fed narrative that’s been priced into equities, and it’s already rippling through fixed income and volatility markets. SpaceX shares have fallen 7% in two days post-IPO, trading near the volume-weighted average price of $180 and leaving early buyers essentially underwater. The tech IPO weakness adds to broader pressure on high-growth, unprofitable names that depend on a lower-rate environment. China has activated a 55% tariff on Australian beef after claiming the country has reached its import quota, signaling Beijing’s willingness to weaponize trade restrictions on commodities tied to geopolitical friction. This escalation raises questions about broader supply chain retaliation and energy commodity stability in the Asia-Pacific region.

Alright, let me start with something you’ve probably experienced without even thinking about it. Imagine you’re at a parking garage, and you keep trying to drive up to the next level. You get to the same spot, hit your head on the ceiling, back up, and try again. Same thing happens the next day. Pretty soon, you know exactly where that ceiling is. You don’t even try to go higher anymore—you just expect to bump into it. Now imagine the garage has a basement. There’s a certain level below which the structure gets sketchy, so nobody really wants to park down there. Cars back up and find a spot higher up instead. That ceiling and that floor? Those are always there. The garage doesn’t change, so traders who know about them can plan around them.

That’s exactly what support and resistance are in the stock market. SPY is trading around 747 right now, and if you look at the last few days, it bounced around between 740 and 754. Those aren’t random numbers. The 754 level—that’s where sellers showed up and said “not today,” pushing it back down. The 740 level—that’s where buyers thought “this is cheap, I’m buying.” These invisible lines exist because thousands of traders remember them, have orders sitting there, or made decisions at those prices before. They’re not magical. They’re just crowd memory made into action.

Now let’s translate that into real options thinking. Support and resistance are price levels where SPY has historically struggled to break through, and when it gets close to them, the market’s behavior changes. Right now, looking at your data, SPY opened at 747.76 this morning and has traded between 743.86 and 748.23. That 748 level is acting like resistance—notice how it can’t quite break through and hold. The 740 level from earlier this week is acting like support. Here’s the natural question: why does this matter for options trading? Because options prices are built on the assumption that SPY will continue doing what it’s been doing. If SPY is sitting right at a support or resistance level, the probability of a move is about to change dramatically. When you’re at resistance, the odds of a move higher get smaller. When you’re at support, the odds of a move lower get smaller. The market has already priced in a certain amount of volatility—your IV rank is sitting at 100, which is absolutely maxed out—but that volatility is distributed unevenly across different strike prices depending on where support and resistance live.

Let’s get specific with today’s setup. SPY is at 747.76, and you’ve got 28 days until the July 17th expiration. Your IV rank is 100, which means volatility is at the absolute top of its historical range. That’s noise for a moment—we’ll come back to it. The real question is: where are the meaningful support and resistance levels that will determine whether your premium-selling strategy works or gets blown up? Looking at your five-day history, you had a high of 754.83 on June 15th, then a pullback to 740.96 on June 17th. That 755 level is now acting as overhead resistance. The 740 level is acting as support. You’re trading right in the middle. Here’s how to think about this: if you’re considering selling premium—say, a call spread by selling the 750 call and buying the 760 call—you need to know whether 750 is a meaningful level or just a random strike. In this case, 750 is very close to that 755 resistance level from earlier this week. That means the market has already tested that level and rejected it. Traders who bought at 750 last time are sitting on losses or breakeven. That creates a natural supply of sellers if SPY approaches 750 again. Your short call at 750 has a much better chance of expiring worthless because the market has shown it doesn’t want to go there. That’s not luck. That’s support and resistance at work.

Here’s the trading implication you need to sit with today. IV rank is at 100—absolutely extreme. But before you start selling everything, recognize that extreme IV often clusters around support and resistance levels because that’s where uncertainty is highest. The market doesn’t know if SPY will hold 740 or break through it. That uncertainty drives IV up. So when you look at your options chain and see those puts at the 720, 710, and 725 strikes with huge open interest—24,000, 22,000, and 22,000 shares of open interest respectively—that’s not random either. Those are traders who’ve placed bets at support levels. The 720 put has 37,863 contracts of open interest. That’s a level the market cares about. Now, you’re sitting at 747.76, and the nearest meaningful support below you is around 740. That’s a 7-point cushion, or roughly one percent. If you’re thinking about selling a put spread—say, selling the 720 put and buying the 710 put—you’re selling way below support. That’s a high-probability trade, but here’s what high IV rank tells you: the market is pricing in the possibility of a bigger move than usual. With IV rank at 100 and volatility at 140.77 implied volatility points, the market is expecting SPY could test that 740 support or even break it. Your trade is still valid, but you need to understand you’re being compensated for real risk. The premium you collect on that 720-710 put spread is fat because the market genuinely thinks there’s a chance SPY could touch those levels. That’s not free money. That’s the market saying “if you want to take this risk, here’s the price.”

So here’s what changes about how you look at your trades today: don’t just look at strikes and Greeks in isolation. Map them to support and resistance. The 750 call strike you might sell? That’s right at resistance. The 720 put strike you might sell? That’s at support. The 745 and 725 strikes where you see heavy volume? Those are the levels the crowd is watching. When IV is this extreme, it’s extreme because the market is uncertain whether these levels will hold. Your job as a premium seller is to decide: do I think these levels will hold, and am I being paid enough to take the risk that they won’t? With IV rank at 100, you’re being paid a lot. That’s your signal to think carefully about whether the risk is worth it. The support and resistance levels are telling you exactly where the danger zones are.

All right, let’s talk about what happened in SPY today and what it means for your premium-selling thesis this week. We’re looking at a market that opened at 747.76 and traded between a low of 743.86 and a high of 748.23. The intraday range is tight — about four and a half points — but what’s really important here is the context of the week. We’ve had a brutal three days. Monday we closed at 741.75, then Tuesday ripped 1.76 percent to 754.83, Wednesday sold off 0.6 percent, and yesterday we got absolutely hammered with a 1.25 percent drop to 740.96. So today’s consolidation in the 744 to 748 range is actually a bit of a relief bounce, but we’re still down nearly 1.5 percent from Tuesday’s high. This is not a strong week for the bulls.

Now, the reason we’re seeing this weakness is pretty clear when you look at sector attribution. Technology, which is XLK, is marked as the leader today, but I want to be honest with you — the sector data is incomplete in our feed, so I can’t give you exact percentages on which sectors led and which lagged. What I can tell you is that the headline narrative is all about Fed hawkishness. Chairman Warsh’s statement on Wednesday was drastically more hawkish than expected, and that’s been the dominant theme crushing the tape. When the Fed gets tough on inflation, growth stocks and rate-sensitive sectors get hit first. That’s your Technology, your Communication Services, your high-multiple names. Materials, which is XLB, is marked as a laggard, which makes sense because commodity-linked sectors tend to struggle when the market is pricing in slower growth and higher rates for longer.

Let’s connect the dots on the macro side. The Fed funds rate is sitting at 3.63 percent, down just one basis point from 3.64. But here’s what’s really moving the needle — the two-year Treasury jumped 15 basis points to 4.20 percent, and the ten-year is up 6 basis points to 4.49 percent. That’s a steepening yield curve, which is actually a positive signal for the economy, but it’s also a signal that the market is repricing rate expectations higher. CPI year-over-year came in at 4.27 percent, up 32 basis points from the previous reading of 3.95 percent. That’s hot. That’s the kind of number that makes a Fed chairman like Warsh want to keep rates elevated. Consumer sentiment has also cratered — down 3.5 points to 49.8 — and retail sales are negative. Housing starts fell 215 units. So the macro backdrop is deteriorating, inflation is sticky, and the Fed is signaling they’re not in a rush to cut. That’s a recipe for continued volatility and downside pressure.

Now, here’s where it gets really interesting for you as a premium seller. IV rank is sitting at 100.0 — that’s the absolute ceiling. We are at all-time high implied volatility. The current IV is 140.77, and the VIX closed yesterday at 16.85, though it’s ticked up to 18.44 in the macro data. This is the environment you’ve been waiting for. When IV rank is at 100, you’re selling premium at the richest levels possible. The risk-reward for a defined-risk spread — say, a call spread or a put spread — is heavily in your favor right now. You’re collecting maximum theta decay, and you’re doing it at peak volatility. The trade-off, of course, is that the market is volatile for a reason. We’re in a downtrend, inflation is sticky, and the Fed is hawkish. So if you’re going to sell premium, you need to be precise about strike selection and position sizing.

Let’s talk about price levels. SPY opened the week at 741.75 on Friday the 12th. We rallied to 754.83 on Monday, which is the 52-week high sitting at 760.40 — so we got close but didn’t break it. The low of the week is 740.96 from yesterday. Support is building around 740 to 742. Resistance is at 750 to 754. If we break below 740, the next level to watch is 735 to 738. On the upside, if we can reclaim 754, then we’re testing that 52-week high at 760.40. The options chain shows heavy volume in the 720 put strike — 24,045 contracts — and the 720 call strike with 14,438 contracts. That’s a magnet level. The 750 strike is also seeing significant open interest on both sides, 26,242 calls and 52,169 puts. That tells me the market is expecting a range-bound week with a bias toward the downside.

On the calendar, we’ve got the Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update coming in six days on June 25th, and the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment survey on June 26th. Both are medium importance, but given that consumer sentiment is already in the tank at 49.8, that survey could be a catalyst if it prints even weaker. The big one is the Fed meeting on July 29th, which is 40 days out — well beyond your immediate trading window, but it’s the elephant in the room. No major earnings in the next seven days, so we’re not dealing with company-specific catalysts. This is a macro-driven market, and until we see some relief on inflation or a Fed pivot signal, expect continued chop and downside bias.

Here’s your practical takeaway: IV rank at 100 is screaming at you to sell premium, but the macro backdrop is deteriorating and the market is in a downtrend. If you’re going to put on a defined-risk call spread to capitalize on this IV environment, keep your short strike at or slightly above resistance — around 750 to 752 — and give yourself room to be wrong. Don’t get greedy chasing max profit on a week when the Fed is hawkish and sentiment is cratering. Sell the premium, manage the Greeks, and take profits early.

Here’s the thing that separates PMs who scale into GPM roles from those who plateau: most of us still operate like we’re building deterministic products. You ship a feature, measure retention, iterate. But when you’re working with systems that have irreducible uncertainty baked in—whether that’s an AI model or, honestly, the volatility surface in options markets—your entire mental model for prioritization has to invert.

Aakash’s framework around AI-native operating models gets at something most product orgs miss: you can’t just add AI to your existing roadmap. You have to fundamentally change how you think about discovery, measurement, and stakeholder communication. What that means in practice is this: instead of asking “what feature should we build,” you’re asking “what distribution of outcomes can we tolerate, and what’s our confidence in that distribution?”

Take a concrete example. When you’re building a premium-selling strategy using defined-risk spreads on SPY, you’re not trying to predict the exact move. You’re mapping a probability cone and asking: at what strikes does my risk-reward ratio stop making sense? That’s the AI-native thinking. You’re not betting on a point forecast. You’re sizing your conviction based on your confidence in the range itself.

The operating model piece is where senior PMs get stuck. You have to change how you staff discovery—you need people who can hold ambiguity longer. You need metrics that measure “confidence calibration” not just “did we hit the target.” And you need stakeholder language that makes it clear you’re optimizing for expected value over time, not quarterly binary wins.

What that means for your role right now: if you’re interviewing for GPM positions, this is the lens interviewers are actually testing for, even if they don’t say it directly. They want to see if you can operate in a world where you’re right 60% of the time and still build conviction around a strategy.

The move here is to audit one active initiative this week—not an AI project necessarily, but something with real uncertainty. Map out: What’s your confidence distribution? How are you currently measuring success? Are your metrics actually testing your conviction, or are they just measuring activity? Once you see the gap, you’ll understand exactly what needs to shift in how you operate.

curriculum.lesson: GDP and economic cycle: how macro regime affects options strategy

segments.name: SPY Learning: How Macro Regimes Shape Your SPY Options Strategy — And Why Today's Setup Matters
PM Concept: Why These Posts Don't Match Your Workflow

episodes.shownotes_html:
Daily Brief — 2026-06-18

What we covered

  • World news: Fed Holds Firm, Iran Deal Reopens Energy; Warsh Signals Hawkish Pivot
  • SPY learning: How Macro Regimes Shape Your SPY Options Strategy — And Why Today’s Setup Matters
  • Market brief: SPY Daily Brief: Tech Carries the Load as Fed Hawkishness Weighs on Energy
  • PM/analytics: Why These Posts Don’t Match Your Workflow

Summary

The Federal Reserve held rates steady but removed its cutting bias from the statement, signaling a shift toward restraint under new Chair Warsh. The median projection shows members expect a rate hike by end of 2026, though Warsh himself abstained from forecasting, suggesting he’s not the easy-money chair markets had hoped for. Apple announced it will raise prices as AI chip costs surge, passing inflation pressure directly to consumers and signaling margin compression across premium hardware makers. This caps a broader theme of input cost inflation hitting the supply chain despite Fed pause rhetoric. The US and Iran signed a ceasefire memorandum reopening the Strait of Hormuz and resuming oil flows after months of tension, with a $300 billion reconstruction fund emerging as a key flashpoint. Energy markets are pricing in relief, but shipping and insurance industries are waiting for security guarantees before fully normalizing routes. Indonesia’s central bank hiked rates by 1% over the span of a month, joining the Philippines in tightening cycles as emerging markets battle inflation and currency weakness. These moves signal capital preservation pressure in EM, with potential implications for dollar strength and capital flow dynamics. North Korea rejected G7 calls for denuclearization, reaffirming nuclear weapons as irreversible policy and raising geopolitical risk around Korean peninsula stability. This has limited direct market impact but keeps Northeast Asia volatility elevated and could weigh on risk sentiment if escalation occurs.

Let me start with something you already understand intuitively, even if you’ve never thought about it in these terms. Imagine you’re planning a neighborhood block party six weeks from now. You’re trying to decide whether to rent an indoor venue or just set up outside. The thing is, you don’t know what the weather will be like in six weeks. But you do know something useful: it’s June, so statistically, summer weather is coming. That changes your thinking. You’re probably going to lean outdoor and maybe just grab a tent as backup. Now, if it were December and you were planning the same party for late July, you’d be even more confident about the outdoor setup because summer is locked in by the calendar. That confidence affects your decision-making. You might spend more on decorations and less on contingency. That’s macro regime thinking. You’re not predicting the exact weather on party day — you’re understanding the season you’re in and how that shapes what’s probable.

Now let’s connect this to what’s actually happening with SPY and the broader economy right now. A macro regime is the big-picture economic environment we’re operating in. Are we in growth mode, where companies are expanding and earnings are rising? Are we in a slowdown, where people are nervous and pulling back? Are we in a recovery, bouncing off a recession? The reason this matters for your options strategy is dead simple: the regime determines what moves markets, how volatile they get, and how long trends last. In a strong growth regime, SPY tends to grind higher with lower volatility — the market’s confident. In a slowdown or recessionary regime, you get bigger swings and longer drawdowns. GDP and the economic cycle are the backbone of this regime. When GDP is accelerating, companies can raise prices, hire more people, and invest in growth. That’s fuel for equities. When GDP is slowing or contracting, companies get cautious, earnings guidance gets cut, and equity risk premiums expand — meaning investors demand more return to hold stocks, which pushes prices down.

Today, on June 18th, 2026, SPY is sitting at 745.38, and here’s what’s interesting: we’re up 0.6 percent on the day, but look at the last five trading days. We had a 1.76 percent rip on the 15th, then a 0.6 percent drop on the 16th, then a 1.25 percent drop on the 17th, and we’re recovering today. That’s not random noise — that’s the market processing economic signals. Technology is leading with a 2.51 percent gain today, while Energy is getting hammered down 1.96 percent. That’s a classic divergence you’d see when growth expectations are shifting. The natural question here is: how do I know what regime we’re actually in just by looking at price action and sector rotation? Here’s how to think about it: you can’t know with certainty, but you can read the signals the market is sending you. When tech leads and energy lags, the market is saying, “I think we’re in a world where innovation and efficiency matter more than commodity demand.” That usually happens when GDP growth is either slowing or shifting away from commodity-intensive sectors. When you see the opposite — energy and industrials leading, tech getting sold — the market is saying, “I think we’re in a world where demand is strong and inflation is a concern.” That’s a different regime entirely, and it changes how you trade options on SPY.

Here’s the practical translation: your IV rank today is at 100.0, which is the absolute maximum. That means implied volatility is at the highest level it’s been over the last year. The current IV is 16.56, and the VIX is sitting at 17.1. Now, why would IV be maxed out? Because the market is uncertain about the macro regime. When GDP data comes in soft, or when there are signals that growth is slowing, traders start pricing in the possibility of bigger moves. They buy protection — puts get more expensive, calls get more expensive, spreads get wider. The 29-day options chain we’re looking at for July 17th expiration shows the ATM call at 745 strike with a delta of 0.54 and IV of 16.56, while the ATM put at 745 strike has a delta of -0.45 but IV of only 12.64. That’s a 3.92-point IV skew between calls and puts. That skew tells you something: the market is slightly more worried about downside than upside, which is consistent with macro uncertainty. Traders are paying more for protection below the market than they are for upside above it.

So here’s where the regime question becomes actionable for your premium-selling strategy. If we’re in a regime where GDP is slowing and the economic cycle is rolling over from expansion into contraction, then selling premium on SPY becomes riskier because you’re going to get bigger directional moves and longer trends. The regime will work against you. Your short calls or short puts won’t just sit there collecting decay — they’ll get tested hard, and you’ll be managing losses instead of watching theta work. But if we’re in a regime where GDP is stable or accelerating, and this volatility spike is just noise around data releases or sector rotation, then selling premium is attractive because the regime supports mean reversion and range-bound trading. The natural question here is: how do I actually figure out which regime we’re in before I put on a trade? Here’s the reasoning: you don’t need to predict GDP to the decimal point. You need to read the signals the market is already showing you. Look at the sector performance — tech up 2.51 percent, energy down 1.96 percent, utilities up 0.58 percent. That’s a growth-stock rotation, which typically happens when the market is pricing in either continued growth or a soft landing scenario. Look at the IV skew — puts are cheaper than calls, which suggests the market isn’t pricing in a crash, just volatility. Look at the five-day chart — we had a bounce today after two down days, which is consistent with a market that’s digesting information, not in free fall. All of this together suggests we’re in a regime where growth is uncertain but not broken. That’s a regime where premium selling can work, but you need to be disciplined about where you sell.

Now, the trading implication for you today: given that IV rank is at 100.0, you’re looking at historically elevated option prices. That’s attractive for premium sellers. But you need to match your strike selection to the macro regime you’re reading. If you’re selling calls to capture upside premium, you want to sell closer to ATM — like the 747 or 746 strikes — because in this regime, the market is more likely to oscillate than to break out dramatically higher. The 746 call has a delta of 0.53 and IV of 16.69, which means you’re getting paid for volatility that’s at the extreme end of the distribution. But here’s the catch: that IV rank of 100.0 is a warning flag that volatility could contract sharply once macro uncertainty resolves. If we get a strong GDP print or clear guidance from the Fed, IV could drop from 16.56 to 14 or lower in a single day. When that happens, your short calls benefit from vega decay — the options you sold lose value as IV falls. That’s a tailwind for your premium selling. But the flip side is that if GDP disappoints and we’re actually rolling into a slowdown regime, IV could spike higher, and your short calls will hurt. The regime matters because it determines whether you’re selling into a volatility peak that will contract, or whether you’re selling into volatility that will expand further.

Let me be concrete about this. You’re looking at the 745 call for July 17th expiration with a 0.54 delta. In a growth regime where GDP is stable, that call is likely to expire worthless or finish ITM by a small amount, and you keep most of the premium. In a slowdown regime where GDP disappoints, that call could move significantly ITM, and you’re managing a loss. The macro regime determines which scenario you’re in. Right now, the market is sending mixed signals — tech is rallying, energy is crashing, volatility is maxed out. That tells you the regime is in transition or uncertain. In transitions, you don’t want to be caught with naked short calls or puts at strikes that could get blown through. You want defined-risk structures that give you a fighting chance if the regime shifts. That’s why a call spread — selling the 747 call and buying the 750 call — might make more sense than a naked short call. You’re capping your risk in case the growth regime breaks and SPY rallies hard. You’re still collecting premium from the elevated IV, but you’re not betting your account on a regime that’s clearly uncertain. The July 17th expiration gives you 29 days to let the macro picture clarify. GDP data, Fed communications, earnings guidance — all of that will tell you whether we’re staying in growth or rolling into slowdown. Your spread lets you profit from premium decay while you wait for that clarity.

All right, let’s break down what happened in the market today. SPY closed at 745.38, up about 0.6 percent on the day. Now, that’s a modest move in absolute terms, but context matters here. We came into today off the back of a brutal two-day selloff—down 1.25 percent yesterday alone—so this bounce feels like a relief trade more than conviction buying. The intraday range was tight: we opened at 747.76, hit a high of 748.18, and bottomed at 743.86. Volume came in at 11.1 million shares, which is below average, so we’re not seeing institutional conviction behind this move. The real story isn’t the 0.6 percent gain; it’s what’s underneath it.

Let’s talk sector attribution because this is where the meat is. Technology, represented by XLK, absolutely carried the load today, up 2.51 percent. That’s the single biggest contributor to SPY’s gain. Industrials followed with a 1.16 percent move, and Consumer Discretionary added 0.7 percent. But here’s the flip side: Energy, XLE, got absolutely hammered, down 1.96 percent. That’s the biggest drag on the index by a country mile. Healthcare also lagged, down 0.65 percent. Communication Services was essentially flat, and Consumer Staples actually declined slightly. So what we’re seeing is a classic bifurcation—growth and cyclicals leading, defensive and commodity-sensitive sectors getting sold. That’s not a broad-based rally; that’s a sector rotation.

Why is this happening? Well, the Fed released its decision yesterday, and Chairman Warsh made it crystal clear that easy money is not coming. The Fed held rates steady at 3.63 percent, but more importantly, they removed language that had been signaling a potential rate cut bias. The median projection now shows a rate hike coming before year-end, not a cut. That’s a meaningful shift in messaging. Gundlach came out this morning saying Warsh is not the accommodative Fed chair people were hoping for, and that’s actually supportive of longer-term rates staying elevated. The ten-year Treasury is at 4.43 percent, down just four basis points from yesterday, so we’re not seeing a flight to safety. Energy’s decline likely reflects the reality that higher rates for longer means slower economic growth, which pressures crude demand. Meanwhile, tech is rallying because—and this is important—higher rates actually make the mega-cap tech names with strong cash flows and buyback programs more attractive on a relative basis. It’s a rotation into quality and growth, not a broad risk-on move.

Now let’s talk about what this means for premium sellers. Your IV rank is sitting at 100.0, which is the absolute top of the range. Your IV percentile is 29.96, meaning you’re in the 30th percentile historically. This is a bit of a mixed signal. The IV rank being maxed out suggests we’ve seen a spike in implied volatility relative to the last 252 days, but the percentile tells you that in absolute terms, IV is still relatively low compared to historical norms. The VIX is at 17.1, which is elevated from where we were a week ago but nowhere near panic levels. Here’s the practical read: if you’re thinking about selling premium, you’re getting paid, but you’re not getting paid a ton. The call IV on the 745 strike for July expiration is 16.56, and the put IV is 12.64. That’s a 4-point skew, which is normal but not extreme. The delta on the ATM call is 0.54, the put is minus 0.45. You’re looking at a fairly balanced market. If you’re going to sell premium, you want to be selective about strikes and think about defined-risk structures rather than naked short calls or puts. The risk-reward isn’t screaming at you to take on naked directional risk.

Let’s talk about price levels. SPY is trading right at 745.38. Your immediate resistance is the 748 level we hit today, and beyond that, the 52-week high is 760.4. That’s about 2 percent away. Your support is more interesting: yesterday’s low was 740.96, and that’s held so far. Below that, you’ve got the 738 to 740 zone as secondary support. The five-day chart shows we bounced hard off the 740 level, so that’s a level worth watching. If we break below 740 on a close, you’re looking at a test of the 735 to 737 range. The key thing to understand is that we’re in a range-bound market right now. We’re not trending decisively in either direction. That’s actually an environment where defined-risk spreads—call spreads, put spreads, iron condors—can work well because you’re not fighting a strong directional bias.

What’s on the calendar? The Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update is coming in seven days, on June 25th. That’s a medium-importance event, but it’s not going to move the market dramatically unless there’s a surprise. The next big event is the Fed meeting on July 29th, which is 41 days away. That’s outside your immediate trading window if you’re working with monthly or weekly expirations, but it’s worth keeping in the back of your mind. There are no major earnings releases in the next seven days, so you’re not dealing with binary event risk. That means any moves you see are going to be driven by macro sentiment and sector rotation, not individual stock surprises. That’s actually cleaner for options trading because you can focus on the Greeks and IV rather than trying to predict earnings beats.

Here’s your one-sentence takeaway: with IV rank at the top of the range but absolute IV still historically low, and the market range-bound between 740 and 750, consider selling call spreads or put spreads at the edges of that range rather than chasing the bounce—you’ll get paid for time decay without fighting the directional uncertainty.

Look, I need to be straight with you. None of these posts are going to move the needle for what you’re actually working on right now.

You’re a senior PM learning to think systematically about defined-risk spreads on SPY. That means you’re wrestling with real problems: position sizing relative to portfolio heat, understanding when IV rank actually changes your edge, managing the psychological weight of defined-risk versus undefined upside, and building repeatable decision frameworks that don’t rely on gut feel.

The three pieces in front of us are solid content, but they’re all pointing at infrastructure, automation, and product scaling problems. AI operating models, agent loop design, CI/CD pipelines—these are valuable if you’re shipping software at scale or managing engineering teams. They’re not valuable if you’re trying to systematize your approach to selling premium.

Here’s what I think is actually happening: you’re in a phase where you need content that bridges your PM intuition into trading mechanics. You already understand prioritization, stakeholder trade-offs, and shipping under constraint. What you don’t have yet is the specific language and frameworks for thinking about Greeks decay, volatility clustering, and position management in ways that map onto the PM skill set you’ve already built.

The move here is to be ruthless about input quality. You’ve got limited cognitive bandwidth, and right now it’s better spent on content that directly challenges your assumptions about how spreads behave under different market regimes, or how professional traders think about risk allocation, than on reading about Kubernetes and GitOps.

I’d rather skip a segment entirely than give you content that feels relevant but isn’t actually actionable for your specific goal. That’s what happened today.

So here’s your action this week: instead of consuming whatever lands in your feed, spend thirty minutes writing down three specific questions you have about spread management that you can’t answer yet. Things like: how do I know when my IV rank threshold is actually calibrated correctly, or why does my win rate drop when I size bigger, or what’s the relationship between vega and my actual P&L. Then go find content or people who are specifically addressing those questions. That’s how you build real intuition, not by reading general PM advice repackaged for traders.

segments.name: SPY Learning: How Earnings Season Moves the Whole Market: Why One Company's Report Can Shake SPY
PM Concept: Organizational Change is Exhausting - All Things Product Podcast with Teresa Torres & Petra Wille

episodes.shownotes_html:
Daily Brief — 2026-06-17

What we covered

  • World news: Fed Unity Expected, Iran Deal Uncertainty Lingers, China-US Farm Diplomacy Quietly Advancing
  • SPY learning: How Earnings Season Moves the Whole Market: Why One Company’s Report Can Shake SPY
  • Market brief: SPY Daily Brief: Tech Carries the Load as IV Rank Hits 100 — June 17
  • PM/analytics: Organizational Change is Exhausting - All Things Product Podcast with Teresa Torres & Petra Wille

Summary

Americas: Prediction markets are pricing in a unanimous FOMC decision at Wednesday’s meeting under new board member Warsh, a sharp reversal from April’s divided vote—this matters because it signals the Fed is coalescing around a single policy path, reducing uncertainty for rate expectations and equity valuations. CME Group’s leadership transition is also underway with CFO Lynne Fitzpatrick set to become CEO in March 2027, a routine succession that keeps the derivatives infrastructure steady. Meanwhile, Trump has delayed Jay Clayton’s confirmation as intelligence director and is conditioning FISA reauthorization on passage of a voter ID bill, introducing political friction into national security and surveillance policy. Asia/China: US farm-state officials held a subnational dialogue with Chinese provincial counterparts this week, marking a pivot toward agricultural cooperation and closer engagement ahead of midterm elections—this matters because agriculture is a key trade lever in US-China tensions, and quiet diplomatic channels at the state level can signal de-escalation even as headline tensions remain. China published a whitepaper on AI safety and governance as the G7 wrapped without Beijing, positioning itself as a responsible player in global AI regulation. Trump also signaled the Iran nuclear deal is not yet final and remains subject to renegotiation, with ongoing talks and a planned press conference in France creating uncertainty around energy markets and Middle East geopolitical risk. Middle East & Africa: Israeli air strikes on Lebanon are continuing despite the newly announced US-Iran framework agreement, showing that de-escalation rhetoric does not guarantee an immediate halt to kinetic operations—this matters because sustained military activity keeps energy prices elevated and regional risk premiums intact. Trump has accused Obama of bribing Iran in the 2015 nuclear deal, signaling his skepticism of the current framework and raising the risk of deal collapse or hardening of terms.

Alright, let’s start with something you’ve probably experienced. Imagine you’re on a group project for school, right? And there are five kids in your group. Four of them show up with solid work, but one kid — the one everyone’s counting on — shows up with something that’s either amazing or completely falls apart. What happens? The whole group’s grade gets affected. Maybe it doesn’t tank completely, but everyone notices. That one person’s performance ripples through the entire team outcome. That’s exactly what happens when the biggest companies report earnings during earnings season.

Now imagine those five kids aren’t equal. One kid is doing half the project’s weight. Apple, Microsoft, Nvidia, Tesla — these aren’t just any kids in the group. They’re the ones carrying the load. So when Apple reports earnings and misses expectations, or crushes it, that’s not just an Apple problem anymore. It’s a group problem. The whole project — the whole team’s grade — shifts because of what that one heavy-hitter does. And SPY, the S&P 500 index, is basically that group project. It’s made up of 500 companies, but a handful of them carry disproportionate weight.

Now let’s translate that into what’s actually happening in the market. When we talk about earnings season, we’re talking about the windows in the calendar — roughly mid-April through May, and mid-July through August — when the bulk of public companies report their quarterly results. These reports contain actual profit numbers, forward guidance, and management commentary. For the mega-cap stocks that dominate the S&P 500 — your Magnificent Seven types like Apple, Microsoft, Nvidia, and Google — a single earnings miss or beat can move not just that stock, but the entire index. SPY today is sitting at 750.91, and we’ve seen a four-dollar move in the last five days. That volatility? A lot of it traces back to earnings expectations and sector rotation around earnings.

Here’s the natural question that comes up: why does one company’s earnings move the whole index if there are 500 companies in SPY? The answer is weighting. The S&P 500 is market-cap weighted, which means the biggest companies have the biggest influence. As of mid-2026, the top ten holdings in SPY represent roughly 30 percent of the index’s total value. So when Microsoft reports earnings and beats, Microsoft stock might jump five percent, but because Microsoft is such a huge piece of SPY’s pie, that five percent move in Microsoft translates to a meaningful move in SPY itself. It’s not one-to-one, but it’s substantial. And when you have multiple mega-caps reporting in the same week — which happens during earnings season — you get compounding effects. Tech led today with XLK up 1.02 percent, and that’s not random. That’s sector rotation driven by earnings expectations and results.

So here’s what this means for you as someone thinking about selling premium on SPY using defined-risk spreads. Earnings season is a macro factor that fundamentally changes how volatility behaves and how directional conviction builds. Right now, we’re in June, which means we’re heading into the tail end of Q2 earnings and the ramp-up to Q3 earnings expectations. Look at our IV environment: IV rank is sitting at 100.0, which is the absolute highest percentile. That’s not random. That’s the market pricing in uncertainty because we’re in an earnings-heavy window. When you’re thinking about selling call spreads or put spreads on SPY, you need to understand that this elevated IV is partially a function of earnings season risk premium.

Here’s the second natural question: if IV rank is at 100, doesn’t that mean IV is super high and I should be selling? Not necessarily, and this is where earnings season thinking changes your approach. Yes, high IV rank means IV is elevated relative to its own history, which is generally good for premium sellers. But here’s the catch: during earnings season, that IV can evaporate fast if the earnings surprise is benign or bullish across the board. Or it can spike further if results disappoint. The IV you’re selling today might be the IV you wish you had back in three days if a major company reports and shocks the market. That’s why systematic traders don’t just look at IV rank in isolation during earnings season. They look at the calendar of upcoming earnings and ask: which of my short strikes are at risk of being tested by a major earnings move?

Let’s get specific to today’s setup. SPY is at 750.91, and we’re looking at July 17th expiration, which is 30 days out. The ATM call at 751 has a delta of 0.54 and an IV of 14.09. If you’re thinking about selling a call spread — say, short the 753 call and long the 755 call — you’re looking at relatively tight strikes because the market is calm-ish on a day-to-day basis. But here’s what earnings season thinking adds to that analysis: between now and July 17th, how many of the mega-cap constituents will report? If you’ve got Microsoft, Google, and Apple all reporting within that window, you’re not just managing delta and theta. You’re managing event risk. A single earnings surprise from one of those names could move SPY two to three percent in a single day, which would blow past your short call strike and turn your defined-risk spread into a defined-loss situation faster than you expected.

The third natural question: so should I just avoid selling premium during earnings season? No, but you should think about it differently. Earnings season is actually when premium is richest, which is when systematic sellers make their best money — if they size appropriately and pick their spots. The key is understanding which earnings are already priced in and which are surprises waiting to happen. Tech is leading today — XLK up 1.02 percent — which suggests the market is feeling good about tech earnings expectations heading into the next wave of reports. When the market is already pricing in a positive outcome, there’s less upside surprise risk. That’s when selling premium becomes more attractive. You’re selling premium that already reflects the expected good outcome. But if you’re looking at a sector where earnings expectations are uncertain or where the market is bracing for potential disappointment, that premium is richer for a reason — there’s genuine event risk embedded in it.

So here’s your trading implication for today: with IV rank at 100 and 30 days until July expiration, you’re in a premium-seller’s paradise, but only if you’re selective about which strikes and which sectors you’re shorting. If you’re building a short call spread on SPY, you want to make sure your short strike is far enough away that it doesn’t get tested by a single mega-cap earnings surprise, but close enough that you’re still capturing meaningful premium. Given that tech is rallying and leading, and given that earnings season is in full swing, the market is already rotating into names and sectors where results are expected to be solid. That means your short calls on SPY are less likely to be tested by a surprise to the upside from the heavy-hitter names. That’s a green light to be more aggressive with your strike selection — you can sell calls closer to ATM because the surprise risk is lower. But it’s not a green light to ignore the calendar. You still need to know which mega-caps report before your expiration and size your position accordingly.

Alright, let’s talk about what happened in the market today. SPY closed up just eight basis points at 750.91, so we’re talking about a completely flat day in terms of price action. But here’s the thing — and this is important for how you think about positioning — that flatness is masking some real internal rotation happening underneath. We’re up point zero eight percent on the day, which is basically noise, but the week-to-date picture is a lot more interesting. We’ve gone from 737.76 last Thursday to where we are now, so we’re up about 1.8 percent in five trading days. That’s a meaningful move, and today’s consolidation after yesterday’s half-percent pullback tells me the market is catching its breath before deciding what comes next.

Let’s dig into the sector breakdown because this is where the real story lives. Technology, the XLK, led the charge today with a 1.02 percent gain. That’s the biggest mover on the board and it’s basically carrying the entire index. Industrials came in second with 0.93 percent, and Financials with 0.91 percent. Those three sectors are doing the heavy lifting. But look at the laggards — and this is where you see the tension in the market. Communication Services, the XLC, fell 1.13 percent. Consumer Staples, the XLP, down 1.04 percent. Energy, the XLE, down 0.96 percent. Healthcare down 0.57 percent. So we’ve got a classic risk-on tilt happening here, where investors are rotating out of the defensive stuff and into the cyclicals and growth names. That’s a meaningful tell about sentiment.

Why is tech leading? Well, part of it is the AI narrative continuing to drive those names, but there’s also something macro happening underneath. The Fed board is looking more unified heading into the next meeting on July 29th, according to prediction market traders on Kalshi. That’s actually bullish for growth stocks because it suggests less volatility around policy surprises. The yield curve is still inverted at 38 basis points, but it’s flattening — we went from 40 basis points yesterday to 38 today. That’s a small move, but it’s in the direction that favors equities. The ten-year is at 4.47 percent, down a basis point, and the two-year is at 4.07 percent, down two basis points. So we’re seeing a slight steepening bias, which is typically positive for cyclicals and growth. Energy’s weakness is interesting though — that’s likely a function of the softer macro backdrop we’re seeing in housing starts and retail sales. Housing starts came in at 1.177 million, down from 1.392 million in the prior period. That’s a meaningful decline and it’s weighing on the energy complex.

Now let’s talk about what matters for your premium-selling thesis right now. IV rank is at 100 — that’s the highest reading possible. We’re at the 100th percentile of IV over the past year. That’s a screaming signal that implied volatility is expensive relative to history. The current IV is 14.09 percent, and the VIX is at 16.4. Here’s the thing though — and I want you to sit with this — IV rank at 100 doesn’t automatically mean you should be selling. What it means is that if you’re going to sell premium, you’re getting paid the most you’ve been paid in a year to take on that risk. The question is whether realized volatility is going to stay low enough to justify the premium you’re collecting. Given that we’re in a consolidation phase after a strong five-day run, and given that the macro backdrop is mixed — inflation is still elevated at 4.27 percent year-over-year, unemployment is stable at 4.3 percent, and the Fed is on hold — I’d say the conditions are actually pretty favorable for selling defined-risk spreads. You’re getting paid well, and the market isn’t showing signs of panic.

Let’s talk about the technical picture. SPY is sitting at 750.91, right around the ATM strike for July expiration. The fifty-two-week high is 760.4, so we’re about nine points below that. The fifty-two-week low is 591.89, so we’re well above that. Immediate resistance is at 754.83, which is where we closed on Monday — that’s the recent high from this week’s rally. If we break above that, the next level to watch is 760.4, the fifty-two-week high. Support is at 749.61, which is today’s low. Below that, we’ve got 750.33, yesterday’s close, and then 745 becomes a meaningful level if we start to roll over. The options chain is showing decent liquidity at the ATM strikes. The 750 and 751 calls have the most open interest, and the put side is similarly liquid. Delta on the ATM calls is running around 0.54 to 0.55, which means you’re roughly at a fifty-fifty probability of expiring in or out of the money by July 17th.

What’s coming in the next forty-eight hours? Tomorrow we get the Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update, which is a medium-importance data point. That’s not going to move the market dramatically, but it’ll give us another read on housing affordability, which ties back to that weakness we’re seeing in housing starts. The next big event is the Fed meeting on July 29th, which is forty-two days away, so that’s not an immediate catalyst but it’s definitely on the radar. There are no major earnings releases in the next seven days, so we’re in a relatively quiet period from a corporate earnings perspective. That actually works in your favor if you’re thinking about selling premium — lower event risk means lower realized volatility, which means your short premium positions have a better chance of working.

Here’s my practical takeaway for you as you’re thinking about positioning this week: IV rank at 100 combined with a consolidating market after a strong rally is a textbook setup for selling call spreads or call spreads against long stock. You’re getting paid maximum premium, the market is taking a breather, and there’s no major catalyst until the Fed in six weeks. If you’re going to sell premium on SPY this week, do it with defined risk, keep your short strike above resistance at 754.83, and size it so you’re comfortable holding through any near-term noise. The risk-reward is in your favor right now.

Here’s the thing that separates senior PMs from the rest: most of us spend enormous energy trying to fix the org around us, and almost none of it sticks. Torres and Wille nail something that’s brutal to admit—organizational change is exhausting precisely because we keep attacking the wrong variable. We think if we just document the process better, run better standups, or get stakeholders aligned, the machine will work. It doesn’t.

What actually works is way more direct. Instead of trying to force systemic change, you optimize for the decisions you can actually control and let the system adapt around you. Think of it like managing IV crush into earnings. You can’t control what happens to implied vol—that’s the market. What you control is position sizing, entry timing, and whether you’re fighting the macro or flowing with it. The traders who win aren’t the ones trying to predict vol behavior; they’re the ones who build repeatable processes that work regardless of the environment.

In a PM context, that means stop trying to change how your org prioritizes. Instead, build a discovery and measurement practice so airtight that the right priorities become obvious to everyone without you having to convince them. Document what you learn, not what you think people should do. Share data, not directives. The org will shift because the signal is undeniable, not because you restructured a process.

What that means in practice: if you’re a senior PM or GPM candidate, you probably have stakeholders pulling in different directions. Instead of running another alignment meeting, run a tight two-week discovery sprint. Get real user data. Get actual usage metrics from your product. Present it in a way that makes the trade-off visible to everyone at once. No politics, no interpretation—just signal. You’ll be shocked how fast people self-organize around clarity.

The move here is to audit your own calendar this week. Count how much time you’re spending trying to change process or convince people versus time spent generating new insight or measuring what matters. If it’s more than thirty percent on the former, you’re fighting the wrong battle. Redirect that energy into becoming the person in the room with the best data and the clearest read on what customers actually need. Orgs reorganize around that person naturally.

That’s how you build leverage without burning out.
