curriculum.lesson: Bear call spread: structure and when to use it

segments.name: SPY Learning: Bear Call Spreads: Selling Premium When You Think the Party's Over
PM Concept: How to stop tokenmaxxing and cut AI spend 10x

episodes.shownotes_html:
Daily Brief — 2026-06-05

What we covered

  • World news: AI Talent Wars Heat Up as China Poaches from the Valley—and Energy Costs Spike in Japan
  • SPY learning: Bear Call Spreads: Selling Premium When You Think the Party’s Over
  • Market brief: SPY Daily Brief: Tech Selloff Masks Defensive Rotation — June 5, 2026
  • PM/analytics: How to stop tokenmaxxing and cut AI spend 10x

Summary

China is aggressively recruiting AI talent from the US, with Tencent’s new Chief AI Scientist coming directly from OpenAI and targeting artificial general intelligence development. This matters because it signals Beijing is accelerating its AI capability race and may reduce the US tech sector’s talent moat in a sector that’s driving market leadership. Japan approved a $19 billion emergency budget to cushion fuel costs as Iran tensions persist, while also beginning export talks on advanced destroyers with Indonesia. The fuel subsidy signals energy price pressure is real enough to warrant fiscal intervention, which could affect both Japan’s growth trajectory and broader commodity price dynamics. India’s GDP grew 7.8% in the March quarter despite geopolitical headwinds, showing resilience in one of the world’s fastest-growing major economies. This data point reinforces that emerging market growth remains intact even as US-China tensions escalate, which has implications for capital allocation and inflation expectations globally.

Okay, imagine you’re at a concert venue and tickets are selling like crazy. Everyone’s convinced the show is going to be packed, so scalpers are asking premium prices for seats. You think, though — the hype is overdone. The show’s probably not going to sell out completely. So here’s what you do: you agree to sell someone a ticket at a really high price, say one hundred fifty dollars. But at the exact same time, you buy a ticket from someone else at a slightly lower price, maybe one hundred thirty dollars. Now you’re protected. If the show does sell out and ticket prices explode, you’re only on the hook for the difference — twenty dollars. But if the hype dies and nobody wants tickets, you keep the full twenty-dollar spread as profit. You didn’t have to put up a ton of money upfront because you hedged yourself. That’s the beauty of it: you’re making money from the hype deflating, but you’re not gambling your house on it.

That’s exactly what a bear call spread is in the options world. You’re selling a call option at one strike price — that’s like selling the high-priced ticket — and simultaneously buying a call option at a higher strike price. That purchase is your insurance. You collect the difference in premiums between what you sold and what you paid. The maximum profit is capped, but so is the maximum loss. You’re not betting the market crashes. You’re betting it doesn’t rally past a certain point. And the natural question here is: why would you ever want to cap your profit? Here’s how to think about it: unlimited profit sounds great until you realize it comes with unlimited risk. A bear call spread lets you trade a smaller, defined profit for a much smaller, defined loss. It’s the trade-off between being right about direction and being smart about risk management.

Now let’s ground this in what’s actually happening with SPY right now. SPY is sitting at 749.60 as of today, June 5th. The market’s down almost a percent, and tech is getting hammered — down three and a half percent. But here’s what’s interesting: the IV rank is sitting at 100, which is the absolute highest it’s been in the past year. That means implied volatility is expensive. When volatility is expensive, the premiums you collect from selling calls are juicy. You’re getting paid a lot for taking on risk because everyone’s nervous. So imagine you look at the June 30th expiration, which is twenty-five days out. The 750 call — right at the money — has a delta of 0.53 and is trading with an IV of 13.9. That’s your short call. You’d sell that. Then you buy the 755 call as your hedge. The spread between those two strikes is five dollars wide, and you’re collecting maybe two dollars or so in net credit. That two-dollar credit is your maximum profit. The five-dollar width minus the two-dollar credit you collected is your maximum loss — three dollars. So you’re risking three to make two. That’s a defined-risk trade.

Here’s the trading implication you need to sit with: IV rank at 100 is a gift for credit spreads, but it’s also a warning signal. When volatility is at its extreme high, it usually means the market just got scared. SPY dropped almost one percent today. Tech collapsed. The VIX is at 16.54. This environment is exactly when bear call spreads become attractive because you’re selling expensive premium. But — and this is the critical part — you have to ask yourself: is the market scared enough that it’s going to keep falling, or is this a capitulation bounce? If you sell a bear call spread here, you’re saying the market doesn’t rally past your short call strike by expiration. With IV rank this high, that’s actually a reasonable bet. The market tends to mean-revert when vol spikes this hard. You’re not trying to catch a falling knife; you’re trying to collect premium from people who are panicking and overpricing calls.

The other thing jumping out: look at the sector divergence. Consumer staples up 1.58 percent, healthcare up 1.22 percent — classic defensive rotation. Technology down 3.56 percent. This tells you the market is rotating into safety, not setting up for a massive rally. That’s the exact environment where bear call spreads work. You’re selling calls on an index that’s showing defensive characteristics. The natural question here is: doesn’t selling calls mean you’re bearish? And the answer is more nuanced than that. You’re not bearish. You’re neutral to mildly bearish. You’re saying, ‘I don’t think SPY rallies past 755 in the next twenty-five days.’ That’s not a bold prediction. SPY would need to rally about 0.7 percent from here, and given today’s action and the sector rotation, that’s a reasonable line in the sand. You’re not predicting a crash. You’re just saying the upside is limited. And because IV is so elevated, you’re getting paid well for that view. That’s systematic thinking: you’re matching your conviction level to the trade structure, and you’re getting compensated fairly for the risk you’re taking.

All right, let’s talk about what happened in the market today because it’s a lot more interesting than the headline number suggests. SPY closed down 0.99 percent, finishing at 749.60, and that’s a meaningful move when you zoom out and look at the week. We opened at 752.31 this morning, so we’re down about 8.70 points from Monday’s close at 758.54. That’s a 1.15 percent decline over five trading days, and that matters because it tells us something important about what’s driving the market right now — and spoiler alert, it’s not uniform selling.

Let’s dig into the sector breakdown because this is where the real story lives. Technology, the XLK, absolutely got hammered today, down 3.56 percent. That’s the laggard, and it’s the primary reason SPY is negative. But here’s what’s fascinating: while tech was getting sold, defensive sectors were actually rallying. Consumer Staples, the XLP, rose 1.58 percent and was the clear leader. Healthcare, the XLV, gained 1.22 percent. Real Estate, XLRE, up 0.97 percent. Utilities, XLU, up 0.89 percent. Even Financials, XLF, managed a 0.47 percent gain. Energy, XLE, did sell off 0.94 percent, but that’s a smaller drag than tech’s three-and-a-half percent decline. What you’re seeing here is a classic defensive rotation — money is moving out of growth and into safety. That’s not a neutral signal.

So why is tech getting hit? The headlines are pointing to AI concerns and competitive pressure, especially with China poaching talent from the U.S. as firms like Tencent pursue artificial general intelligence. But honestly, the bigger macro picture is that we’ve got the jobs report released this morning, and the market is clearly pricing in a scenario where the Fed might need to stay patient on rate cuts. The ten-year Treasury is at 4.49 percent, up three basis points from Wednesday. The two-year is at 4.08 percent, also up three basis points. That yield curve steepening, combined with CPI that came in hotter than expected last month at 3.95 percent year-over-year — up 63 basis points from the prior reading — is making growth stocks look expensive relative to bonds. Tech is the most sensitive to that dynamic, so it gets sold first and hardest.

Now let’s talk about what this means for you as someone thinking about selling premium. Your IV rank is sitting at 100.0, which is the absolute ceiling. That’s the highest percentile reading possible. Your current IV is 13.9, and the VIX closed at 16.54. This is a fascinating setup because IV rank at 100 tells you that implied volatility is at the highest level it’s been over the lookback period — typically 252 trading days. That’s textbook premium-selling territory. You want to be short vega when IV rank is elevated, and 100 is as elevated as it gets. The ATM 750 call is trading at 13.9 IV with a 0.53 delta, and the ATM 750 put is at 12.54 IV with a negative 0.47 delta. Notice the call IV is higher than the put IV — that’s a skew that reflects the tech selloff and some fear about further downside. But here’s the thing: even with that skew, you’re getting paid well to sell premium right now. A call spread or a put spread at these IV levels is going to collect meaningful theta decay over the next 25 days to June expiration.

Let’s establish your key price levels. SPY’s 52-week high is 760.40, which we’re now 10.8 points below. That’s immediate resistance if we get a bounce. Your immediate support is the low from today at 748.22, and if that breaks, the next level to watch is 745, which is roughly where we’d see a two percent decline from Monday’s open. The 52-week low is 591.89, but that’s not relevant for this week’s trading. What matters is whether we hold 748 or if the defensive rotation turns into something more aggressive.

On the calendar, the jobs report released this morning is the big one — that’s already in the market. But here’s what’s coming that could move things: Retail Sales drops in four days on June 9th, that’s medium importance. Then on June 10th, you’ve got the Fed meeting and the CPI inflation release back-to-back. That’s a high-importance double header. The market is going to be parsing every word from the Fed and every decimal point on inflation to figure out whether rate cuts are coming in the second half of the year or if we’re stuck at 3.63 percent for longer. That’s a five-day window, so you’ve got some time to position, but volatility could compress or expand significantly depending on what those releases show.

Here’s your one-sentence takeaway: IV rank at 100 with a defensive rotation underway is a green light to sell call spreads above 752 or put spreads below 745, but size accordingly because the next five days could bring significant repricing once we get CPI and the Fed decision.

Here’s the thing about cost optimization that most PMs get wrong: they treat it like a feature cut. You’re trying to do the same thing cheaper, which almost never works at scale. But Ravi Mehta’s framing of tokenmaxxing—the tendency to just throw more compute at a problem because it’s easier than thinking hard about what you actually need—that’s a different animal entirely. And it maps perfectly to how you should think about position sizing and capital allocation in spreads.

The core insight is that you’re probably optimizing for the wrong metric. You’re not trying to maximize tokens per dollar or trades per week. You’re trying to maximize signal per unit of risk. What that means in practice is ruthlessly questioning whether your setup actually needs the width it has, the duration it has, or the notional it has. Most traders I know will say they’re being “disciplined” about position size, but they’re actually just taking the same setup and scaling it proportionally. That’s not discipline—that’s tokenmaxxing with Greeks.

Mehta’s three fixes boil down to: measure the actual unit economics of what you’re doing, cut the low-signal noise, and rebuild from first principles. For you, that’s asking: what’s the actual edge I’m getting from this call spread? Is it worth the capital I’m locking up? And critically—am I taking this trade because the setup is genuinely asymmetric, or because I’ve normalized being in the market?

The move here is to run a forensic audit on your last ten closed spreads. For each one, calculate what your actual return was per dollar of max risk, then compare it to what you’d get from a tighter, shorter-dated version of the same setup. Chances are you’ll find that your best trades used maybe 60 percent of the width you thought you needed. You were tokenmaxxing—using more Greeks, more time decay, more notional—to solve a problem that tighter positioning would have handled better.

This week, pick your highest-conviction setup for next week’s expiration. Instead of your normal structure, cut the call strike two points tighter and the put strike one point wider. Run the math on theta, delta, and max profit. You’ll probably find the return per dollar of risk goes up, not down. That’s the signal.

episodes.title: Daily Brief — June 01, 2026

curriculum.lesson: Iron condor case study: walk through a real SPY setup

segments.name: SPY Learning: Iron Condors on SPY: The Parking Lot Principle
PM Concept: I Don't Review the Code. I Review the Artifacts.

episodes.shownotes_html:
Daily Brief — 2026-06-01

What we covered

  • World news: Buffett’s Succession Tested, China Factory Beats, AI Reshuffles the Deck
  • SPY learning: Iron Condors on SPY: The Parking Lot Principle
  • Market brief: SPY Daily Brief — June 1, 2026 | Energy Leads, Tech Holds, Utilities Tank
  • PM/analytics: I Don’t Review the Code. I Review the Artifacts.

Summary

Berkshire Hathaway announced a $6.8 billion acquisition of Taylor Morrison, marking one of the first major strategic deals under new CEO Greg Abel—a signal that Buffett’s successor is ready to deploy capital on housing exposure. This matters because it shows whether the market will trust Abel’s capital allocation after decades of Buffett’s shadow. Nvidia is partnering with Chinese startup Unitree on a humanoid robotics platform as Unitree eyes an IPO, cementing US-China tech collaboration even as geopolitical tensions simmer. The move signals that despite rhetoric, semiconductor and AI infrastructure deals between the superpowers are still moving forward. China’s factory activity beat forecasts in May according to private surveys, though official data softened—a split signal that suggests underlying demand resilience but potential measurement divergence. This is the first real read on whether China’s stimulus is gaining traction after months of weakness.

Alright, let’s start with something you deal with every single day. Imagine you own a parking lot. Every day, you charge people to park there. Most days, the lot fills up to about seventy percent capacity — that’s your normal. Some days it’s packed, some days it’s half empty, but you know the range. Here’s the thing: you don’t care if the lot gets completely full or completely empty. You just care that it stays somewhere in the middle, in that predictable zone where you keep collecting your daily parking fees. If someone offers you money to promise that the lot won’t exceed ninety-five percent full, and someone else offers you money to promise it won’t drop below fifty percent, you’d take both bets. Because you’re pretty confident it’ll stay between those lines. You’re collecting money on both sides just for things you expect to happen anyway. That’s the entire psychology of what we’re about to talk about.

Now here’s the beautiful part: you don’t need the lot to stay perfectly at seventy percent. You don’t even need it to go up. You just need it to stay in that middle zone. The lot could drift to sixty-five percent, or climb to eighty percent, and you’re still collecting on both sides of those bets. You’re not trying to predict direction. You’re just betting that things stay relatively normal, relatively contained, relatively boring. And most of the time, that’s exactly what happens.

Okay, so let’s translate that to what’s actually happening in the market right now. Today is June first, twenty twenty-six. SPY is sitting at 755.83. That’s your parking lot at seventy percent capacity. When we talk about an iron condor on SPY, we’re talking about a defined-risk spread that works exactly like those parking lot bets. You’re selling call options at a higher strike price — that’s your “don’t go above ninety-five percent full” bet. And you’re selling put options at a lower strike price — that’s your “don’t go below fifty percent” bet. Then you buy calls even higher up and puts even lower down to cap your maximum loss. The natural question here is: why would anyone pay you to make these bets? Here’s how to think about it: every option has time value. Right now, with twenty-five days until the June twenty-sixth expiration, that time value is real money sitting in those contracts. Buyers of options are paying for the possibility that SPY moves big. You’re getting paid to take the other side — you’re saying SPY probably won’t move that far. Today’s IV rank is at one hundred, which is the highest it’s been all year relative to its historical range. That sounds scary, but it’s actually a gift to a condor seller. It means the market is pricing in high volatility, which means those option premiums are fat and juicy. You’re selling expensive options and buying cheaper protection further out.

Let’s build a real setup using today’s numbers. SPY at 755.83, expiration in twenty-five days. You could sell the 758 calls — those have a 0.51 delta, meaning there’s roughly a fifty-one percent probability they expire in the money. That’s your upper boundary. You’d buy the 762 calls as protection, capping your loss if SPY rockets. On the put side, you’d sell the 754 puts — those have a negative 0.43 delta, so roughly a forty-three percent probability of expiring in the money. You’d buy the 750 puts for protection. The key insight here is that you’re not trying to be right about direction. You’re trying to be right about the range. SPY has moved from 750.59 five trading days ago to 755.83 today. That’s a five-dollar move in five days. A reasonable person might think: will SPY move another five dollars up or down in the next twenty-five days? Maybe. But the iron condor is saying: I’m betting it doesn’t move more than three to four percent in either direction. And I’m getting paid premium to take that bet.

Here’s where the trading implication hits you in the face. With IV rank at one hundred, you’re in what we call an “IV crush” setup. The market is scared. Volatility is priced at the absolute top of its range for the year. But here’s the thing: when volatility is that high, it usually means something scared the market recently. Energy is up 2.17 percent today — that’s unusual strength. But utilities are down 1.65 percent, healthcare is down 0.99 percent, and consumer discretionary is down 1.5 percent. There’s internal disagreement in the market. That disagreement creates volatility, which creates expensive options. Now, when you sell an iron condor in this environment, you’re essentially saying: “I think this disagreement gets resolved, and we settle down somewhere in the middle.” That’s not a prediction that the market goes up. That’s not a prediction it goes down. You’re predicting that the emotional extremes get wrung out. And statistically, that happens more often than not. The premium you collect — and it’s substantial right now because IV rank is at the top — that’s your profit if SPY just stays relatively contained. Even if SPY moves against you a little, say from 755.83 up to 760, you’re still profitable on the iron condor because you sold those expensive options and they’ve decayed in value. The math works because you’re not fighting the market’s direction. You’re harvesting the market’s indecision. And right now, with IV this high, that harvest is fat.

Good afternoon. Let’s break down what happened in the market today and what it means for your premium-selling thesis this week. SPY closed at 755.83, down just nine basis points on the day. Now, that’s a flat close in absolute terms, but context matters here. We’re coming off a pretty solid week where SPY rallied from 750.59 last Tuesday all the way up to 756.48 on Friday. So today’s pullback is basically a consolidation move — nothing alarming, nothing that breaks the uptrend we’ve established. Volume came in at 7.9 million shares, which is moderate. The intraday range was tight: we hit 756.77 at the high and 754.69 at the low, a spread of just over two dollars. That’s the kind of range you’d expect on a Monday after a holiday weekend when the market is still finding its footing.

Now let’s talk about what actually drove the market, because the sector breakdown tells a really interesting story today. Energy absolutely crushed it — XLE up 2.17 percent, making it the clear leader and accounting for most of SPY’s positive contribution. Meanwhile, Technology held up reasonably well with XLK gaining 0.93 percent, which is solid given the broader market was flat. But here’s where it gets interesting on the downside: Utilities got hammered, XLU down 1.65 percent, making it the worst performer by a wide margin. Consumer Discretionary also rolled over, down 1.5 percent. Materials fell 1.14 percent, Healthcare down 0.99 percent, and Industrials slipped 0.9 percent. So you’ve got a classic risk-on tilt today — energy and tech leading, defensive sectors like utilities and staples getting sold. That’s the narrative.

Why did energy rip 2.17 percent? The macro backdrop is actually pretty supportive. We’ve got crude oil benefiting from China’s manufacturing data beating forecasts in May, which suggests demand might be holding up better than some feared. That’s a positive signal for energy prices and energy equities. The fact that tech held up despite the flat market is also telling — it suggests there’s still appetite for growth and AI-related names. You’re seeing that in the headlines too: Nvidia partnering with Unitree on humanoid robotics, Meta and Schlumberger leading AI adoption. That’s the kind of narrative that keeps tech bid even when the broader market is treading water. The weakness in utilities and defensive names, though, suggests investors aren’t rushing into safety today. They’re willing to take risk, which is a healthy sign for the market’s internal structure.

Let’s talk about what this means for your premium-selling decision. IV rank is sitting at 100 percent right now, which is a massive red flag if you’re thinking about selling premium. That means implied volatility is at the absolute top of its 52-week range. Your IV percentile is 34.69, which means IV is elevated relative to historical levels, but the rank being at 100 tells you that from a relative perspective, we’re at peak IV. The VIX is at 16.15, which is elevated but not panic territory. Current IV on the ATM 756 call is 13.69 percent. Here’s the thing: when IV rank is at 100, you’re getting paid maximum premium for selling, but you’re also taking on the risk that IV collapses and your short vega position gets crushed. This is the classic dilemma. If you believe IV is going to stay elevated or expand further, selling premium right now is attractive. If you think we’re about to see a volatility crush, you want to wait. Given that we’re flat on the day and consolidating after a rally, I’d lean toward the idea that IV might compress a bit as the market settles. That said, with the Fed meeting nine days out and the jobs report coming in four days, there’s legitimate event risk that could keep IV bid.

Let’s nail down the technical levels you need to watch. Immediate resistance is 756.77, which is today’s high and also very close to Friday’s close of 756.48. If SPY breaks above 757, you’re looking at the 758 level, which has some open interest in the options chain. On the downside, support is at 754.69, today’s low. Below that, 754 is a key level with heavy put volume — 1,088 contracts traded there today. If we break below 754, the next support is around 750.59, which was last Tuesday’s close. The 52-week high is 758.08, so we’re trading just below that. The 52-week low is 585.06, which is so far away it’s not relevant for this week’s trading.

Now, what’s coming in the next 48 hours that could move the needle? We’ve got the Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update coming in three days on June 4th — that’s medium importance, so it could move rates and financials a bit. More importantly, the Non-Farm Payrolls report drops in four days on June 5th, and that’s high importance. Jobs data is always a market mover, especially with the Fed watching closely. Then you’ve got the Fed meeting itself on June 10th, nine days away. That’s the big one. So your 25-day June 26th expiration is sitting right in the middle of two major events. That’s actually good for premium sellers because event risk keeps IV elevated. The jobs report especially could be a catalyst for a move, so if you’re thinking about selling call spreads or put spreads, you want to be aware that we could see a 1 to 2 percent move in either direction on Friday morning when that number hits.

Here’s your practical takeaway for the week: IV rank at 100 is screaming at you to sell premium, but with the jobs report and Fed meeting on the calendar, you’re getting paid for real event risk — so if you’re going to sell call spreads against a rally or put spreads against a dip, make sure your strikes are positioned to give you a 1 to 2 percent buffer on either side of current price. The flat close today and the consolidation pattern suggest the market is taking a breath before the next move, which means premium decay is working in your favor right now. That’s your edge this week.

Here’s the thing that separates PMs who scale to GPM and those who don’t: knowing what to actually review. And it’s not the code. It’s the artifacts.

What that means in practice is this. You’re sitting in a design review or a dev standup, and your instinct is to ask about implementation details—how they’re building it, what framework they’re using, whether the database schema is normalized. Stop. That’s not your job anymore, and frankly, it wastes everyone’s time. What you should be reviewing is what the code produces. The output. The user experience. The metrics dashboard. The edge case handling in a real user scenario.

Think about it like selling premium on SPY. You don’t spend your time reviewing every tick of the underlying price action. You review the Greeks on your position, the IV rank relative to historical levels, the P&L sensitivity under different market regimes. You’re reviewing the artifacts of your strategy—the outputs that actually matter to your risk and return. A developer who hands you a feature without showing you how it behaves under load, or what happens when the API times out, or how it looks on a slow network—that’s like selling a call spread without checking your Greeks. You’re flying blind.

The move here is to shift your review process. When a team ships something, ask to see it live. Ask for the instrumentation dashboard. Ask what the failure modes look like. Ask for the before-and-after metrics. If they can’t show you artifacts—actual evidence of how the thing works—then the work isn’t done, regardless of what the PR says.

This also changes how you staff and delegate. As a GPM, you’re not looking for people who can execute your exact specification. You’re looking for people who can produce good artifacts. Someone who ships a feature with incomplete telemetry, even if the code is elegant, is producing a bad artifact. Someone who ships with strong instrumentation, clear degradation paths, and measurable user impact is producing the right artifact, even if the implementation is messy.

The specific action this week: pick one feature in flight. Don’t look at the code. Instead, write down the three artifacts you need to see before you can confidently say it’s ready. That might be a video walkthrough, a metrics dashboard, a load test report, whatever. Then ask the team to produce those artifacts by EOW. Notice what conversations that unlocks. That’s the level you need to operate at.

Daily Brief — 2026-05-27

What we covered

  • World news: Strait of Hormuz uncertainty and big tech M&A appetite collide with chip supply reshuffling
  • SPY learning: Managing the trade
  • Market brief: SPY Daily Brief: Consumer Strength Masks Tech Weakness as IV Rank Hits 100
  • PM/analytics: None of these posts fit your audience

Summary

JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon signaled the bank is hunting for a $20 billion acquisition target, which would rank among the largest deals in the firm’s history and likely draw regulatory scrutiny given JPM’s already dominant position in US banking assets. Traders on Kalshi are skeptical Iran can reopen the Strait of Hormuz to normal traffic within a month of a peace deal, despite official timelines—a meaningful signal that energy markets aren’t pricing in near-term relief from one of the world’s most critical chokepoints. Nvidia announced a $150 billion capex spending plan, triggering a rally in Taiwan chip stocks while mainland Chinese chipmakers like Cambricon sold off hard, reshaping the geopolitical calculus around semiconductor supply chains.

Today’s lesson is Managing the trade: when to close early vs hold to expiry. SPY most recently closed at 750.57, with current implied volatility around 13.65 and the near-the-money strike near 751.0. That gives us a concrete anchor instead of pretending options theory lives in a vacuum. When you think about this concept, ask how a small move in SPY would change the option’s behavior, and how that risk profile shifts as time and volatility change. The point is not memorizing jargon. The point is understanding what kind of position becomes more fragile or more forgiving when the market moves.

All right, let’s break down what happened in the market today. SPY closed essentially flat at 750.57, down just a penny from yesterday’s 750.59. Now, I know flat sounds boring, but context matters here. We’re up nearly nine dollars for the week—that’s a 1.2 percent move from last Wednesday’s close of 741.25. So today was a consolidation day after a pretty solid four-day run. The index traded in a narrow range, high of 751.38, low of 748.22, and volume came in at 24.5 million shares, which is right around normal. The real story today isn’t the price action—it’s what’s happening underneath.

Let’s talk sectors, because this is where the tension is. Consumer Discretionary led the charge, up 1.44 percent. Consumer Staples followed close behind at plus 1.4 percent. Communication Services added 1 percent. But here’s the kicker: Technology fell 0.47 percent, and Energy got hammered, down 1.09 percent. Financials also lagged, dropping 0.8 percent. So you’ve got a classic rotation story here. Defensive and cyclical consumer names are catching a bid while the mega-cap tech names and energy are getting sold. This is the kind of divergence that tells you there’s some real debate in the market about what comes next.

Why is this happening? A few things are in play. First, the yield curve just steepened—the 10-2 spread widened to 43 basis points from 49 basis points yesterday. That’s actually a meaningful move. When the curve steepens, it can signal either growth optimism or recession fears, depending on which end is moving. In this case, the 10-year fell one basis point to 4.56 percent while the 2-year rose five basis points to 4.13 percent. That’s a classic steepening pattern that often precedes a growth slowdown. Energy’s weakness also makes sense given the headlines around Iran and the Strait of Hormuz—traders on Kalshi are skeptical about Iran’s timeline for reopening that passageway, which is keeping geopolitical risk premium in the market. And tech? Well, Nvidia announced a $150 billion spending plan, which is massive, but that’s also a reminder that the AI capex cycle is real and potentially unsustainable. The market’s rotating into names that don’t require infinite growth assumptions.

Now let’s talk about what matters for you as a premium seller. IV rank just hit 100. That’s the highest reading possible. Current IV is 13.65, IV percentile is 37.78, and the VIX closed at 16.57. This is a critical moment. IV rank of 100 means implied volatility is at the highest point it’s been over the last 52 weeks. For someone thinking about selling premium, this is textbook setup territory. You’re getting paid maximum dollars for taking on defined risk. The ATM 751 strike on the June 26 expiry—that’s 30 days out—is showing IV of 13.65 across both calls and puts. Your short call delta is around 0.54, your short put delta is around negative 0.46. That’s a balanced risk profile if you’re thinking about a call spread or a put spread. The fact that IV rank is at 100 while the VIX is only at 16.57 tells you this is more about realized volatility expectations than panic. It’s a seller’s market, plain and simple.

Let’s nail down the levels. SPY is trading right at the ATM strike of 751. The 52-week high is 752.13, just 1.56 points above us. The 52-week low is 583.24, which is ancient history at this point. For the week, we’re sitting near the highs. Immediate resistance is 752, then 753. Support is 750, then 749. The range has been tight, so don’t expect a breakout without a catalyst. The yield curve steepening is a subtle signal that the market is pricing in some caution, so I wouldn’t be surprised to see us consolidate here for a few more days.

What’s on the calendar that could move us? Tomorrow, May 28th, we get the Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update—that’s medium importance. Then on May 29th, we get University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment. That one matters because consumer sentiment has been deteriorating—it dropped 3.5 points to 49.8 in April. If it rolls over further, that could validate the rotation out of growth and into defensive names we saw today. The big one is the Fed meeting on June 10th, which is 14 days away. That’s outside your immediate trading window if you’re thinking about June expiries, but it’s definitely on the radar for July positioning. No major earnings in the next seven days, so we’re in a quiet period.

Here’s your one-liner takeaway: IV rank at 100 with a steepening yield curve is a green light to sell defined-risk call spreads or put spreads on SPY, but size them conservatively because the market is clearly rotating, and that rotation could accelerate if consumer sentiment data disappoints this week.

I need to be straight with you here — none of these three posts are actionable for a senior PM building intuition on SPY defined-risk spreads.

The Codex piece is about a product feature framework, which is solid PM content, but it’s generic enough that a senior PM with 5-7 years already runs some version of this. It’s not novel framing of a hard problem you’re actually facing. The OpenCode and job market pieces are pure engineering/hiring content with zero relevance to your stated goal of thinking like a systematic trader.

What you actually need for Segment D right now is one of three things: First, a post from a practitioner who’s built systematic option strategies and shares the specific mental model they use to choose between spread types based on volatility regime — not textbook Greeks, but “here’s why I picked a call spread over a strangle last week.” Second, something on how to measure edge in defined-risk positions when you’re running small sample sizes and dealing with slippage. Third, a deep look at how senior traders actually prioritize capital allocation across multiple spreads when IV rank is in the 60-70 range and everything looks attractive.

Right now you’re getting fed content that assumes you need help with basic PM skills or engineering trends. You don’t. You need someone who’s traded through a vol crush, sized positions wrong, and learned the hard way how to think about risk in a way that connects to your PM intuition.

The move here is to either surface posts from actual options traders or practitioners writing about systematic trading decisions, or skip this segment this week rather than force a connection that wastes your time. Your audience is sophisticated enough to know when something’s been retrofitted to fit the brief.

If you want me to write Segment D next time, send me content from someone like a quant who’s published on volatility regime detection, a trader writing about position sizing in defined-risk spreads, or a PM who’s shipped a product that required them to think systematically about trade-offs under uncertainty — which is actually the same skill you’re building with spreads.

Today’s lesson is Managing the trade: when to close early vs hold to expiry. SPY most recently closed at 750.59, with current implied volatility around 0.05 and the near-the-money strike near 751.0. That gives us a concrete anchor instead of pretending options theory lives in a vacuum. When you think about this concept, ask how a small move in SPY would change the option’s behavior, and how that risk profile shifts as time and volatility change. The point is not memorizing jargon. The point is understanding what kind of position becomes more fragile or more forgiving when the market moves.

Good morning. Let’s talk about what SPY did yesterday and what it means for your premium-selling thesis this week. SPY closed at 750.59, up 0.66 percent on the day. That’s a modest move in absolute terms, but in the context of this week it’s actually the fourth consecutive day of gains. We’ve climbed from 733.73 last Tuesday to where we are now — that’s a 2.3 percent rally in five trading days. So while yesterday’s 0.66 percent doesn’t sound like much, it’s part of a meaningful uptrend. Volume came in at 40.96 million shares, which is solid but not exceptional, so we’re not seeing panic buying or euphoric capitulation. This is steady accumulation.

Now, here’s where it gets interesting for your spread construction. Technology absolutely carried this market. XLK rose 2.63 percent yesterday and was the clear leader — that sector accounted for the bulk of SPY’s gain. Industrials followed with a 1.47 percent move, and Materials added 1.39 percent. But here’s the critical part: Energy got absolutely hammered. XLE fell 2.76 percent, making it the worst performer by a wide margin. Financials were slightly negative at minus 0.17 percent, Consumer Staples dropped 1.38 percent, and Healthcare fell 0.92 percent. So we had a very narrow leadership story yesterday — Tech and Industrials doing the heavy lifting while defensive and commodity-linked sectors lagged. That’s a risk-on setup, but it’s fragile because it’s concentrated.

Why is Energy getting crushed? There’s actually a counterintuitive dynamic at play here. Yes, Piper Sandler came out saying the Strait of Hormuz could remain closed for months and oil could hit new highs this summer. That should be bullish for energy stocks, right? But what we’re seeing instead is a rotation away from energy into growth and technology. The market is pricing in that prolonged geopolitical tension and higher oil prices will eventually slow the economy, so traders are front-running that by rotating into Tech, which benefits from lower rates and higher growth expectations. Meanwhile, China’s industrial profits jumped 24.7 percent in April — the fastest gain in over two years — and that’s fueling optimism about global growth and tech demand. European companies are doubling down on China manufacturing despite EU de-risking pressure, which tells you supply chains are normalizing and cost structures are improving. That’s all pro-Tech, pro-growth. So the Energy weakness isn’t about fundamentals getting worse; it’s about the market repricing risk and rotating into what it thinks will outperform in a slower-growth, higher-rate environment.

Let’s talk about the IV picture because this is crucial for your decision-making. IV rank is sitting at zero — that’s the floor. We’re at the absolute bottom of the historical range for volatility. IV percentile is 52.59, which means we’re right in the middle on a percentile basis, but the rank being at zero tells you that realized volatility has compressed significantly. The VIX closed at 17.01 yesterday. That’s calm. That’s a market that’s not pricing in tail risk. For someone thinking about selling premium, this is a double-edged sword. Yes, you’re selling into low IV, which means you’re getting paid less per contract. But the flip side is that the market is complacent, which historically creates opportunities for defined-risk spreads where you’re short premium but protected. The treasury curve steepened slightly — the 10-year is at 4.56 percent, down one basis point, while the 2-year moved up five basis points to 4.13 percent. That 49-basis-point spread is the widest we’ve seen in a while, and it’s suggesting the market is pricing in rate cuts further out. That’s supportive for equities, especially growth stocks like Tech.

On price levels, you need to watch 752.13 as immediate resistance — that’s the 52-week high we just tagged yesterday. Support is at 748.37, which was yesterday’s low. Below that, the next meaningful level is 745.64, which was Friday’s close. We’re in a narrow range right now, and that’s actually ideal for selling premium. The ATM strike for June expiry is 751, and you can see the call side is heavily skewed toward in-the-money strikes — the 750 call has a 0.92 delta, the 749 call is 0.97 delta. That tells you the market is pricing in continued upside bias. On the put side, the 751 put is only 0.16 delta, the 750 put is 0.08 delta. So if you’re thinking about a call spread, you’re looking at selling the 753 or 754 strike and buying further out. If you’re thinking about a put spread, you’ve got room to work with because puts are cheap.

Looking ahead at the next 48 hours, we’ve got the Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update coming in two days on May 28th. That’s medium importance, but given that mortgage rates have jumped 15 basis points on the 30-year and 14 basis points on the 15-year over the past week, any data showing housing stress could trigger a rotation. Then on May 29th, we get University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment. That’s also medium importance, but remember that consumer sentiment has already rolled over — it dropped 3.5 points from 53.3 to 49.8 in April. If we see another decline, that could challenge the Tech rally because it would suggest the consumer is weakening. The big event is the Fed meeting on June 10th, which is 15 days away. That’s outside your immediate trading window, but it’s looming, and the market is already pricing in rate cut expectations based on that steepening curve.

Here’s your practical takeaway: IV rank at zero and a narrow, risk-on market setup makes this an ideal environment to sell call spreads against the 753 to 755 range on June expiry, collecting premium while the market is complacent and protecting yourself against the geopolitical and consumer sentiment risks that could derail this Tech-led rally. You’re getting paid to be right about mean reversion, and the Greeks are working in your favor.

I need to be straight with you here — none of these three posts are the right fit for what we’re building in Segment D.

The first is a listicle of book recommendations. That’s the exact pattern we’re skipping. Your time is too valuable, and you didn’t tune in to hear me read a curated reading list.

The second is a philosophical conversation about whether tech is good or bad. Look, it’s thoughtful, but it’s not actionable for a PM shipping products or learning to think systematically about risk management in options trading. There’s no concrete tactic here, no stakeholder trade-off to navigate, no metric to track.

The third is labor market analysis. Useful context for career planning, sure, but it doesn’t solve a hard PM problem you’re facing right now — whether that’s prioritization under uncertainty, discovering what your users actually need, or building intuition around how to structure a trade when IV rank shifts.

What we need for this segment is something different. We’re looking for a post that shows you how a practitioner actually solved a prioritization nightmare, or how someone rethought a core metric that changed the way they ship, or how a senior PM navigated a stakeholder conflict that forced them to choose between two good options. That’s the stuff that sticks because it’s real and immediately applicable.

Or — and this matters for you specifically — something that shows how systematic traders think about position sizing, or how they decompose risk in ways that parallel how you should be thinking about feature rollouts or metric trade-offs.

The move here is simple: if the next batch of posts doesn’t include something with that kind of specificity and friction, we skip the segment that day. Better to have nothing than to fill time with generic advice you could get anywhere.

Keep sending good stuff. We’ll find it.

PM Concept: The AI Paradox: More Automation, More Humans, More Work

episodes.shownotes_html:
Daily Brief — 2026-05-25

What we covered

  • World news: Iran Deal Hopes Lift Risk Assets; China Chip Rivalry Heats Up
  • SPY learning: Iron Condors on SPY: Strike Selection and the Profit vs. Max Gain Tradeoff
  • Market brief: SPY Daily Brief: Healthcare Leads as IV Rank Hits 100 — Time to Reassess Your Short Premium Strategy
  • PM/analytics: The AI Paradox: More Automation, More Humans, More Work

Summary

US and Iran are making real progress on a potential peace deal involving a 60-day ceasefire extension and Strait of Hormuz reopening, though both sides are cautiously saying a final agreement isn’t imminent yet. This matters because any Iran sanctions relief would unlock crude supply and reshape energy costs for the entire global economy. Huawei announced a new chip design methodology that improves semiconductor capabilities despite US export restrictions, signaling China’s determination to reduce dependence on Western suppliers in the AI and smartphone wars. This escalates the tech decoupling and could pressure US semiconductor stocks if Huawei gains meaningful market share in high-end chips. Toyota is cutting production abroad due to Iran war disruptions and supply chain strain, while Japan’s stock market hit all-time highs as investors bet on an Iran deal resolving those tensions. This shows how directly Middle East geopolitics flow into auto manufacturing and global equity sentiment. India has heightened Ebola surveillance at airports and borders as the outbreak spreads in Central Africa, though no immediate market impact; this is mostly a monitoring posture at this stage. Israel is signaling it may strike Iranian nuclear and regional targets even if a US-Iran deal happens, creating tail risk that could unwind the optimism priced into oil and equities if escalation occurs.

Let me start with something you already understand intuitively. Imagine you’re selling insurance policies on a street corner. You could sell really cheap insurance—say, a dollar a policy—but you’d have to insure against almost everything: hurricanes, earthquakes, alien invasions, whatever. Almost nobody’s going to trigger that payout, so you’d collect a lot of dollars. But if something actually does happen, you’re on the hook for thousands. On the other hand, you could sell expensive insurance policies that only cover extremely unlikely events—a meteor hitting exactly your block. You’d collect a lot per policy, but almost nobody would buy them because the odds of payout are so tiny. The sweet spot is somewhere in the middle: sell insurance for things that are unlikely but not impossible, at a price that feels fair to both you and the buyer. You want to collect decent money without taking on crazy risk.

Now here’s the thing people don’t think about: the cheaper your insurance is, the more people buy it. So if you want to maximize the number of policies sold, you price them lower. If you want to maximize the profit per policy, you price them higher and accept fewer sales. You can’t have both. That’s the core tension, and it’s exactly what happens when you’re building an iron condor on SPY.

An iron condor is just a fancy name for selling insurance on both sides of the market at once. You’re selling call options above where SPY trades right now—that’s insurance against the stock going way up. And you’re selling put options below where SPY trades—that’s insurance against it going way down. You keep the premium from both sales as profit if SPY stays between those two price levels by expiration. The natural question here is: why would you sell both sides instead of just picking a direction? Here’s how to think about it: when you sell a call, you’re betting SPY doesn’t rocket higher. When you sell a put, you’re betting it doesn’t crash lower. If you’re genuinely uncertain about direction—and honestly, most of the time we should be—selling both sides lets you profit from time decay and volatility crush in either scenario. You’re not making a directional bet; you’re making a bet that SPY will stay roughly where it is. That’s a much higher-probability outcome than SPY moving significantly in one direction.

Now let’s make this concrete with today’s data. SPY is at 745.64, and we’re looking at June 26th expiration, which is 32 days out. You could sell the 750 call and the 740 put—that’s a wide condor. Those strikes are about 4 dollars away from current price in each direction. The market is pricing in very low probability of SPY moving more than that in 32 days, so you’d collect maybe 0.30 to 0.50 total from both sides combined. That’s your max profit: somewhere around 30 to 50 bucks on a 100-share contract. But here’s the upside: the probability of both of those strikes being breached by expiration is quite low. You’re probably looking at something like 70 to 75 percent probability of profit. Alternatively, you could sell the 748 call and the 743 put—much tighter strikes, only 2 to 3 dollars away. You’d collect maybe 0.80 to 1.20 total, so max profit around 80 to 120 bucks. But now your probability of profit drops to maybe 55 to 60 percent. SPY only needs to move a little bit in either direction to blow through those strikes and turn this into a loser. That’s the tradeoff: wider strikes mean lower max profit but higher probability of success. Tighter strikes mean higher max profit but you’re accepting a real chance of taking a loss.

The question that usually comes up next is: how do I know which one is actually better for me? Here’s the reasoning: it depends entirely on your account size and your risk tolerance. If you’re trading a small account, you might need the higher max profit per trade to make your monthly targets. If you’re trading a bigger account, you can afford to take more trades at lower profit per trade, so the higher probability version actually compounds better over time. But there’s something else happening today that matters. Look at the IV rank: it’s at 100. That’s the highest it’s been in a year. When IV is this elevated, option premiums are juicy. You’re getting paid a lot to take risk. This is actually one of the few times where the tighter strike selection makes more sense, because you’re compensated generously for the lower probability. When IV was at the 20th percentile—which we saw just a few months ago—those tight strikes wouldn’t have paid enough to justify the risk. The market was stingy. But at IV rank 100, you’re being paid fairly for the probability you’re taking.

Here’s what this means for your actual trading decision today. The current IV environment is telling you something: volatility sellers are being rewarded. Healthcare is leading the market up 1.17 percent, but Communication Services is lagging at minus 0.55 percent. That sector divergence, combined with elevated IV, suggests the market is pricing in real uncertainty about dispersion. An iron condor at tighter strikes—say, selling the 748 call and 743 put—makes sense right now because you’re being paid enough premium to justify the lower probability. If you were building this trade in two weeks when IV has probably crushed back down to the 30th or 40th percentile, you’d want to go wider and accept lower max profit, because the premium would be so thin that tight strikes wouldn’t be worth the risk. The trade setup changes based on the volatility regime. You’re not just picking strikes based on “where do I think SPY will go”—you’re picking strikes based on whether the market is pricing risk fairly relative to actual probability. Today, at IV rank 100, the market is pricing risk generously. That’s your signal to be a little more aggressive with strike selection.

All right, let’s break down what happened in the market today and what it means for your premium-selling thesis this week. SPY closed at 745.64, up just 29 basis points on the day. Now, I know that sounds quiet, but context matters here. We’re sitting near the 52-week high of 749.53, and we’ve climbed 7 points in the last five trading days without any real pullback. That’s a steady grind higher, which is exactly the kind of environment that can lull you into complacency before volatility snaps back.

Let’s talk about what actually drove today’s move, because the sector breakdown tells you something important about market psychology right now. Healthcare, the XLV, led the charge with a 1.17 percent gain and was explicitly the biggest contributor to SPY’s strength. Technology followed with a 1.0 percent move, and Utilities, Industrials, and Energy all chipped in with modest gains between 0.6 and 0.8 percent. But here’s the thing that should catch your eye: Communication Services, the XLC, was the only sector in the red, down 0.55 percent. That’s your mega-cap tech names — the Magnificent Seven types — underperforming. So while the headline says tech is up, the reality is that the breadth of the rally is coming from Healthcare and the more defensive, cyclical parts of the market. That’s a nuance worth noting if you’re thinking about where to position your short premium.

The healthcare strength likely reflects some combination of factors. We’ve got CPI sitting at 3.95 percent year-over-year, which is elevated and sticky, and that’s been pushing investors toward defensive sectors. Healthcare tends to outperform in environments where growth is uncertain but you still need exposure to equities. The fact that Communication Services lagged suggests some profit-taking in the names that have run hard, or maybe some rotation out of the mega-cap growth story. Either way, it’s not a broad-based rip. It’s selective, and that matters for how you think about selling premium.

Now let’s talk about the elephant in the room: IV rank is sitting at 100.0. That’s a full percentile — the highest reading in the lookback period. Your current IV is 15.41, and the VIX is at 16.63. For someone whose entire thesis is built on selling premium, this is a critical moment. An IV rank of 100 means volatility is at the extreme high end of its historical range for this period. That sounds great for premium sellers, right? Sell high, collect theta. But here’s the reality check: IV rank of 100 doesn’t mean volatility is about to collapse. It means it’s already elevated relative to where it’s been. If you’re selling call spreads right now, you’re getting paid well, but you’re also taking on the risk that the market continues to grind higher into that 749 to 752 zone, and your short calls get tested. The IV percentile of 42.96 tells you that while IV is high relative to its own history, it’s not extreme relative to all the data points in the dataset. So you’re in that sweet spot where premium is juicy, but you need to be thoughtful about strike selection and position sizing.

Let’s nail down the technical picture. SPY is trading at 745.64, with the high of the day at 748.94 and the low at 744.48. Your immediate resistance is that 52-week high at 749.53, and if we break through there, the next target is probably 752 to 755. On the downside, support is sitting around 744, which is basically today’s low. The 50-day moving average is probably somewhere in the 742 to 743 range, so that’s your secondary support. For someone selling call spreads, I’d be looking at the 748 to 750 zone as your short strike. You’re getting paid decent premium there with a delta around 0.51 to 0.52 on the ATM calls, and you’ve got a reasonable cushion to the resistance level.

On the calendar front, we don’t have any major economic data releases in the next 48 hours, which is actually important. The next meaningful event is the Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update in three days, followed by University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment in four days. Both of those are medium importance, but they’re not going to move the needle dramatically. The real event on the horizon is the Fed meeting on June 10th, which is 16 days away. That’s outside your immediate trading window if you’re working with weekly or monthly expirations, but it’s definitely in the back of the market’s mind. The Fed funds rate is holding steady at 3.64 percent, and the 10-year Treasury is flat at 4.57 percent, so there’s no new information there. What you should be watching is whether the market starts pricing in any rate cuts or holds before that June meeting. Right now, it’s quiet, which means you’ve got a window to execute your premium-selling thesis without a ton of headline risk in the immediate term.

Here’s your one-liner for the day: IV rank at 100 is a green light to sell premium, but do it in defined-risk spreads with strikes that respect the 749 to 750 resistance zone, because the market’s still grinding higher and you don’t want to be caught short naked calls if we break through. The healthcare-led rally and Communication Services weakness suggest this isn’t a broad-based bull move, so your edge is in selling premium to the laggards and taking profits on any quick pops into resistance. Good luck out there.

Here’s the thing that most PMs miss when they’re thinking about automation and AI tooling: the real constraint isn’t whether you can automate something. It’s whether automation actually reduces the total human judgment required, or just shifts it upstream.

Dan Shipper’s framing cuts right to this. He’s saying that as we get better at automating execution—code generation, content drafting, whatever—we don’t actually end up with fewer people. We end up with more humans in the loop, because every agent needs a human to steer it. The work doesn’t disappear; it gets redistributed toward decision-making and quality gates.

What that means in practice is this: if you’re a PM evaluating whether to invest in an AI tool or workflow, you can’t just look at the surface-level time savings. You have to model the new supervision burden. You’re trading execution work for oversight work. Sometimes that’s a good trade—oversight is often higher-leverage than execution. But if you’re not explicit about where the human judgment now lives, you’ll end up with a system that looks efficient on paper but actually requires more coordinated attention than before.

Think about it like a defined-risk spread. You’re not trying to eliminate risk; you’re accepting it in a bounded, knowable form. Same with AI in your product workflow. You’re not eliminating human judgment; you’re concentrating it at specific decision nodes. The move here is to map those nodes before you deploy the tool, not after.

Shipper’s also bullish on PMs and designers specifically because they’re the ones who have to own those decision nodes. You’re not being replaced by the agent; you’re becoming the person who decides what the agent should do and whether its output is actually good. That’s a leverage play if you do it right, but it requires thinking about your job differently. You’re not executing faster. You’re arbitrating higher-level tradeoffs.

So here’s your action for this week: pick one workflow you’ve been considering automating—maybe it’s a weekly analysis, a trade review, whatever. Write down the three moments where a human currently makes a judgment call. Now imagine an AI doing the legwork. Where does judgment still have to happen? What’s the new review cadence? That’s your real cost structure. That’s what you’re actually buying.

curriculum.lesson: Iron condor anatomy: selling two spreads at once

segments.name: SPY Learning: Iron Condors on SPY: Selling Two Spreads at Once
PM Concept: The best prototypes get thrown away

episodes.shownotes_html:
Daily Brief — May 22, 2026

What we covered

  • World news: US-China AI Jostling and Critical Minerals Surge—Supply Chain Reshaping Underway
  • SPY learning: Iron Condors on SPY: Selling Two Spreads at Once
  • Market brief: SPY Daily Brief: Healthcare Leads Modest Rally as IV Rank Hits Extremes — May 22, 2026
  • PM/analytics: The best prototypes get thrown away

Summary

The US is pushing American AI integration across Asia following the Trump-Xi meeting, with a senior APEC official flagging this as a top agenda item. This signals a strategic bifurcation of AI ecosystems—the US wants to lock in its tech standards in the region before China consolidates dominance. Perpetua Resources just locked down a $2.9 billion US Export-Import Bank loan for an Idaho gold and antimony project, marking a serious federal push to onshore critical minerals away from China dependency. This is supply-chain de-risking in real time—the US is willing to finance domestic extraction at scale. Estée Lauder walked away from merger talks with Spanish beauty conglomerate Puig over compensation disputes, ending a potential $40 billion beauty powerhouse deal. This signals caution in cross-border M&A even among traditional allies—regulatory and valuation friction is rising globally. China’s commerce minister skipped the APEC opening meeting citing ‘urgent official business,’ a diplomatic signal amid broader US-China trade friction. Combined with Wang Yi’s upcoming UN Security Council visit, this suggests Beijing is recalibrating engagement posture rather than escalating—worth watching for tariff and tech policy shifts. Indonesia’s commodity exporters are flagging major obstacles to a state monopoly push on raw material exports, creating potential supply-chain bottlenecks for global manufacturers reliant on Indonesian nickel, palm, and tin. If monopoly rules tighten, commodity prices could spike and input costs for consumer goods rise.

Alright, let’s talk about iron condors. And I’m going to start with something you’ve probably done a hundred times without thinking about it as a trade.

Imagine you’re selling movie tickets at the box office. Here’s the thing: you’ve got a huge line of people who want to buy tickets for tonight’s show, and you know the theater is going to fill up. So you make a deal with your manager. You say, “I’ll sell tickets at full price, but only if you promise to buy back any extra tickets I oversell at a small discount.” Your manager agrees because she knows the theater has a limit. Now here’s the clever part: you also make the same deal with the concession stand manager on the other side of the building. You tell him, “I’ll buy popcorn from you at a set price, but only if you agree to sell me extra at a small markup if I need it.” You’re not actually planning to oversell or need extra popcorn. You’re just protecting yourself on both sides. If the line gets crazy, you’ve got a safety net. If it’s slow, you keep the difference. You’re making money from the middle ground, not from predicting which direction the chaos goes.

That’s the intuition behind an iron condor. You’re setting up two safety nets at the same time—one on the upside, one on the downside. You’re not betting on direction. You’re betting that SPY stays somewhere in the middle, and you collect money from both sides of the market for the privilege of being that middle ground. The beautiful part? You get paid immediately, before you even know if you’re right.

Now let’s translate that into what’s actually happening with SPY options right now. An iron condor is literally two spreads sold simultaneously. On the upside, you’re selling a call spread—that means you sell a call at a higher strike and buy a call at an even higher strike as protection. On the downside, you’re selling a put spread—you sell a put at a lower strike and buy a put at an even lower strike as protection. You’re defining your risk on both sides of the market at the moment you enter the trade.

Look at where we are today. SPY is at 745.64, and we’re looking at the June 18 expiration, which is 27 days out. The IV rank is sitting at 100, which is actually at the very top of the range historically—that’s extremely high. This matters because high IV means options are expensive, which means the premium you collect when you sell those spreads is fatter than usual. Here’s the natural question: if IV is already at 100, why would you want to sell premium now? Shouldn’t you wait for IV to come down? The reasoning here is actually inverted from what beginners think. When IV is at the extremes like this, you’re not trying to predict whether it goes higher or lower. You’re recognizing that at these levels, the market is pricing in a lot of uncertainty, and that uncertainty has real dollar value. You’re selling that uncertainty to someone else. The person buying your spread is paying you for protection they think they need. You’re taking the other side of that bet, saying, “I don’t think SPY moves that far in 27 days.” With IV this high, the width of the move the market is pricing in is generous. That’s your edge.

So concretely, on a 27-day iron condor on SPY at 745.64, you might sell the 750 call spread and the 740 put spread. You’d sell the 750 call and buy the 755 call for protection on top. You’d sell the 740 put and buy the 735 put for protection on the bottom. The strikes I’m using here are just examples, but the logic is: you’re saying SPY will stay between 740 and 750 through June 18. If it does, every penny of premium you collected is yours. If it breaks through one side, your bought spread caps your loss. You know your max loss the moment you enter.

Here’s where it gets interesting for your actual trading decision right now. With IV rank at 100 and current IV at 16.1, you’re in what we call a volatility expansion environment. The market is nervous. Look at the sector data—healthcare is up 1.14 percent, tech is up 0.81 percent, but communication services is down 0.15 percent. There’s rotation happening, which usually means uncertainty. That’s exactly the environment where iron condors make sense, because the premium you’re collecting reflects that fear. But here’s the second natural question: doesn’t high IV mean SPY is more likely to move big and break my wings? Not necessarily, and here’s why. IV rank of 100 means we’re at the top of the historical range, but it doesn’t tell us whether volatility is going to expand further or contract. What matters for your iron condor is whether the actual realized move over the next 27 days is smaller than what the market is pricing in right now. And historically, when IV rank gets this extreme, mean reversion happens. The market tends to calm down. You’re essentially selling fear at peak fear levels, which is often a profitable trade.

Let’s get tactical. With SPY at 745.64 and 27 days to expiration, if you wanted to sell a 1-standard-deviation iron condor—that’s the move the market thinks is “normal”—you’d be looking at something like selling the 750 call spread and the 741 put spread. The delta on the 745 call is 0.54, meaning there’s roughly a 46 percent chance it expires in the money. For puts, the 746 put has a delta of -0.46, so similar probability. You want those deltas to be roughly symmetrical if you’re building a neutral condor. The premium you’d collect for selling those spreads would be significantly fatter than it would be if IV rank were at 30 or 40, because the option prices are inflated by the fear premium. That’s your edge right now. You’re not predicting direction. You’re recognizing that the market is overpricing the probability of a huge move, and you’re getting paid to take the other side of that overpriced bet. Over 27 days, with healthcare leading and most sectors moving up modestly, the realized volatility is likely to be lower than what’s priced in. That’s how you make money on an iron condor in this environment.

Good afternoon. Let’s break down what happened in the market today and what it means for your premium-selling thesis this week. SPY closed up 0.39 percent at 745.64, a modest but steady move that capped off a week of consolidation. We’re up about seven points from Monday’s close of 738.65, so we’ve recovered from Tuesday’s 67-basis-point dip and we’re holding gains. The volume came in at 5.8 million shares, which is fairly normal for a Friday afternoon, nothing that screams conviction either way. But here’s what matters: we’re now just 3.9 points below the 52-week high of 749.53, and we’ve built a solid floor above the 741 level. That’s the context you need before we talk about positioning.

Now let’s talk about what actually drove today’s move, because the sector breakdown tells you everything. Healthcare, the XLV, was the clear leader, up 1.14 percent and accounting for the lion’s share of SPY’s gain. Technology followed with a solid 0.81 percent move, and Financials chipped in 0.70 percent. Those three sectors — healthcare, tech, and financials — basically carried the index. On the other side, Communication Services was the only real laggard, down 0.15 percent. Energy, despite all the geopolitical noise, actually managed a small positive move of 0.34 percent. Consumer Staples was essentially flat at plus 0.15 percent. So this wasn’t a broad-based rally; it was concentrated in the defensive and growth pockets of the market.

Why healthcare? That’s the question. We don’t have a specific catalyst in the data I’m looking at, but the broader macro context matters here. CPI came in hotter than expected last month at 3.95 percent year-over-year, up 63 basis points from the prior reading. That’s inflation that’s sticky, and it’s keeping the Fed on hold at 3.64 percent. When inflation is elevated and rates are likely to stay higher for longer, defensive sectors like healthcare tend to outperform because they have pricing power and stable cash flows. Tech’s move makes sense too — we’re seeing real momentum in the AI narrative, with SpaceX and OpenAI potentially going public at trillion-dollar-plus valuations according to prediction markets. That’s fueling a risk-on bid in the mega-cap names that dominate the XLK. Financials benefit from higher rates, so that’s straightforward.

Here’s where it gets interesting for you as a premium seller: IV rank is sitting at 100 percent. That’s the highest reading on the scale. Your current IV is 16.1, and the VIX is at 16.91. Now, before you get excited about selling premium into a 100 IV rank, let me give you the full picture. IV percentile is only 29.17, which means that while IV is at its highest point relative to the past 52 weeks, it’s still in the lower half of the distribution when you look at the absolute level. The 10-year Treasury has dropped 10 basis points to 4.57 percent, and the 2-year is down 9 basis points to 4.04 percent. That’s a flattening yield curve — it’s now at 49 basis points, down from 53 basis points yesterday. That kind of move usually happens when traders are pricing in either a pause or a cut cycle, and that’s a risk-off signal hiding under the surface of today’s small green close. So yes, IV rank is maxed out, but the absolute level is still compressed. You’re not selling into panic; you’re selling into complacency with a technical edge.

Let’s talk price levels. Immediate resistance is the 749.53 high from earlier this week — that’s only 3.9 points away, so we could tag it on any small bid. If we break through there, the next level is 752, which is a psychological round number and has some open interest in the options chain. On the downside, support is building at 741 to 742, which is where we’ve held the last two days. If we break below 741, the next meaningful support is 738, which was Monday’s open. The 745 strike in the June 18 expiry is your ATM, and it’s got healthy open interest at 20,721 contracts on the call side and 3,605 on the puts. That’s where the market is pricing equilibrium right now.

Looking at the calendar, we’ve got a quiet next 48 hours from a macro perspective. There’s no major data releases today or tomorrow. However, looking ahead to next week, the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment number releases in seven days on May 29th, and that’s a medium-importance print. More importantly, the Fed meeting is June 10th, which is 19 days out. That’s well beyond your June 18 expiry, but it’s the elephant in the room for anyone thinking about longer-dated positions. Consumer sentiment has been deteriorating — it’s at 53.3, down 3.3 points from the prior month — so there’s a real question about whether the consumer can hold up if rates stay elevated. That’s the macro risk that’s keeping a lid on how much premium you should be comfortable selling right now.

Here’s your one-sentence takeaway: IV rank is maxed out and the yield curve is flattening, which creates a tactical window to sell call spreads against resistance at 749 to 752, but don’t get greedy on width or duration because the macro setup — sticky inflation, a pause cycle, and weakening sentiment — suggests volatility could expand quickly if we get a negative surprise.

Here’s the thing that separates PMs who ship at scale from those who get stuck in the prototype graveyard: you’re probably throwing away your best work for the wrong reason, and it’s costing you months of iteration you don’t have.

Ravi Mehta just mapped out two failure modes that look different on the surface but come from the same root cause — a misunderstanding of what a prototype actually proves. The first is teams that build something brilliant, show it to users, get validation, and then… rebuild it from scratch because “it’s not production-ready.” The second is teams that prototype so loosely that when they try to scale it, the architecture collapses and they end up rewriting anyway. Both paths feel like progress. Neither is.

What that means in practice: you’re conflating two separate questions. One is “does this solve the problem?” The other is “can this scale and maintain quality?” Those aren’t the same question, and answering them in the wrong order is why your best prototypes die.

The move here is to think about your prototype as a hypothesis validator, not a code artifact. If your prototype proves the core interaction works and users get the value, that prototype is your north star. The production version isn’t a rewrite — it’s a hardening of the exact thing you proved, not a reimagining of it. You’re optimizing for signal preservation, not for starting fresh.

Apply this to how you’re thinking about your spread trading thesis right now. You’ve probably got a prototype thesis — maybe it’s “sell premium into IV rank above 70 on SPY, 21 DTE, 15 delta short call.” You get some winning trades. Then you start second-guessing the core logic because “it’s not production-ready” — maybe you want to add filters, adjust deltas, optimize entry timing. But here’s the trap: each tweak dilutes the signal from your original hypothesis. You end up with something that’s neither the original thesis nor a coherent new one.

The move is to validate your core thesis as-is over a larger sample size first. Let the prototype tell you what it actually does — win rate, max loss, theta capture — before you rebuild it with bells and whistles. Then, and only then, you harden it with position management rules, Greeks rebalancing, or exit discipline. You’re not rewriting; you’re scaling what already works.

This week, pick one spread setup you’ve been tinkering with and commit to trading it exactly as originally defined for twenty live fills. No tweaks, no filters. Just signal. Let the prototype tell you what it’s actually worth.
